EDCS-00600227.translation: Epigona lived 40 years. Epigona lived 6 months and X days. His wife made this for him, well deserving.
EDCS-00600227.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Epigona, set up by his wife.

EDCS-00600382.translation: To the departed spirits. Euplus lived 19 years.
EDCS-00600382.summary: This funerary inscription records the age of Euplus at death.

EDCS-03600009.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Annius, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-03600010.summary: This inscription records the name Quintus Arvius, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-03600011.summary: This inscription records the name Gaius Caenius, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-03600012.summary: This inscription records the name Publius Cornelius, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-03600014.summary: This inscription records the name Gaius Umbricius Philologus, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-03600015.summary: This inscription records the name Aulus Sestius Dama, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-03600016.summary: This inscription records the name Salvius, son of Sextus Annius, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-03600017.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Titius Caupo.

EDCS-03600020.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gnaeus Ateius.

EDCS-03600021.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Avillius.

EDCS-03600022.translation: Lucius, son of N., son of P.
EDCS-03600022.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius, son of N., son of P.

EDCS-03600028.translation: To Lucius Julius Agrippa, son of Caius, a benefactor, who [built] the street from Sosia's [property] as far as the temple.
EDCS-03600028.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Julius Agrippa for his benefactions, likely from the late Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-05200285.text_edition: \n Lucio Cassio Maniliano clarissimo viro curioni minori quaestori provinciae Siciliae curatori rei publicae Lilybitanorum ob insignem eius abstinentiam patientiam praestantiam erga patriam suam ordo splendidus coloniae Lilybitanorum decreto publico facto et aliam in patria eius pecunia publica posuit\n
EDCS-05200285.translation: To Lucius Cassius Manlianus, most distinguished man, minor curio, quaestor of the province of Sicily, curator of the Republic of Lilybaeum, on account of his outstanding abstinence, patience, and excellence towards his fatherland, the distinguished council of the colony of Lilybaeum, by public decree, erected [this monument] and also [placed] another [monument] with public funds in his fatherland.
EDCS-05200285.summary: This is a public dedication by the council of Lilybaeum to Lucius Cassius Manlianus, honoring his service and virtues, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100269.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Iulius Eutychus vixit annos XXXX uxor bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-06100269.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caius Julius Eutychus lived 40 years. His wife made [this] for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-06100269.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Julius Eutychus, erected by his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100270.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Vibius Soter vixit nnos XLI Vibia Legata vixit nnos XXXVI Lucius Vibius Niceros fecit filius\n
EDCS-06100270.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Vibius Soter lived 41 years. Vibia Legata lived 36 years. Lucius Vibius Niceros, their son, made [this].
EDCS-06100270.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Vibius Soter and Vibia Legata, erected by their son Lucius Vibius Niceros, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100271.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucio Arrio Hermae vixit menses V dies XXV fecit bene merenti Charito vernae suo\n
EDCS-06100271.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Lucius Arrius Herma, who lived 5 months and 25 days. Charito, his own slave, made [this] for her well-deserving master.
EDCS-06100271.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Arrius Herma, erected by his slave Charito, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100274.text_edition: \n Publius Scribonius Sollemnis sibi et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-06100274.translation: Publius Scribonius Sollemnis made [this] for himself and his family while alive.
EDCS-06100274.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Scribonius Sollemnis and his family, made during his lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100275.text_edition: \n Grattia Cai filia Paulla uxor Cai Ofilli mater Cai Ofilli Veri duumviralium funere publico elata et loco publico sepulta et statua data in foro decurionum decreto\n
EDCS-06100275.translation: Grattia Paulla, daughter of Gaius, wife of Gaius, mother of Gaius Ofillius Verus, was carried out to burial at public expense and buried in a public place, and a statue was given to her in the forum by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-06100275.summary: This inscription records the public burial and honors, including a statue, granted to Grattia Paulla by a decree of the decurions.

EDCS-06100281.text_edition: \n Caius Roscius proconsul provinciae Siciliae Sextia Mucia fecerunt\n
EDCS-06100281.translation: Gaius Roscius, proconsul of the province of Sicily, and Sextia, made this.
EDCS-06100281.summary: This inscription records a dedication or building action by Gaius Roscius, proconsul of Sicily, and Sextia.

EDCS-06100292.text_edition: \n Hic requiescit in pace Liberata quae vixit annos XVII deposita sub die X Kalendas Iunias indictione X Requievit in pace Victoria quae vixit annos XVIIII deposita IIII Idus Maias indictione X\n
EDCS-06100292.translation: Here lies in peace Liberata, who lived 17 years, laid to rest on the 10th day before the Kalends of June, in the 10th indiction. Victoria rested in peace, who lived 19 years, laid to rest on the 4th day before the Ides of May, in the 10th indiction.
EDCS-06100292.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription recording the burial dates and ages of two individuals, Liberata and Victoria.

EDCS-06100296.text_edition: \n Laribus Augusteis sic et Genio Caesaris liberorumque eius Caius Publilius Philargurus sevir primus et prior \n
EDCS-06100296.translation: To the August Lar Familiaris and to the Genius of Caesar and his children, Gaius Publilius Philargurus, first and foremost *sevir*.
EDCS-06100296.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Gaius Publilius Philargurus, a *sevir*, to the household gods of the emperor and his family.

EDCS-06100298.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Auli Hirti Liberalis vixit nnos XXVIII menses XI Claudia Sozomene mater filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-06100298.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aulus Hirtius Liberalis lived 28 years and 11 months. Claudia Sozomene, his most devoted mother, (dedicated this).
EDCS-06100298.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Claudia Sozomene to her son Aulus Hirtius Liberalis, who died at the age of 28 years and 11 months.

EDCS-08400266.text_edition: \n Lucia Arria Parthenion vixit annos XXVI Parthenius pater filiae dulcissimae fecit\n
EDCS-08400266.translation: Lucia Arria Parthenion lived 26 years. Her father Parthenius made this for his dearest daughter.
EDCS-08400266.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Parthenius for his daughter Lucia Arria Parthenion, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-08600288.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Victor vixit nnos LI fecit Charis\n
EDCS-08600288.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Victor lived 51 years. Charis made this.
EDCS-08600288.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Charis to her husband Victor, who died at the age of 51.

EDCS-08600290.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aulus Larcius anus vixit annis \n
EDCS-08600290.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Aulus Larcius lived [age illegible].
EDCS-08600290.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Aulus Larcius, with his age illegible.

EDCS-08900391.text_edition: \n Publius Antistius Publi filius Claudia Cavarianus\n
EDCS-08900391.translation: Publius Antistius, son of Publius. Claudia Cavarianus.
EDCS-08900391.summary: This inscription appears to record the names of two individuals, Publius Antistius and Claudia Cavarianus, possibly as a tombstone or a record of citizenship.

EDCS-08900392.text_edition: \n Ex decreto decurionum Rufeio Luci filio Quirina Laeto in fronte pedes XXV in agro pedes XXX\n
EDCS-08900392.translation: By decree of the decurions: to Rufius Laetus, son of Lucius, of the tribe Quirina, 25 feet in front and 30 feet in width of land.
EDCS-08900392.summary: This inscription records a land grant of 25 feet frontage and 30 feet depth to Rufius Laetus, son of Lucius, by a decree of the decurions.

EDCS-08900393.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Cornelius Auctus vixit annos LXXX\n
EDCS-08900393.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Cornelius Auctus lived 80 years.
EDCS-08900393.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Cornelius Auctus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-08900394.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelius Castor vixit annos XVI menses VI\n
EDCS-08900394.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelius Castor lived 16 years and 6 months.
EDCS-08900394.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelius Castor, recording his age at death.

EDCS-08900395.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelia Scepsis vixit annos |(circiter XXXVIII Auctus coniugi piissimae fecit\n
EDCS-08900395.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelia Scepsis lived about 38 years. Auctus made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-08900395.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Auctus for his wife Cornelia Scepsis, recording her approximate age at death.

EDCS-08900396.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelius Soteris vixit annos LXXXV Auctus fratri fecit\n
EDCS-08900396.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelius Soteris lived 85 years. Auctus made this for his brother.
EDCS-08900396.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Auctus for his brother Cornelius Soteris, recording his age at death.

EDCS-08900399.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, possibly for a shared monument or dedication.

EDCS-08900400.summary: This inscription simply records the name Lucius Publicius Iucundus, possibly identifying him as the owner of a property or the subject of a dedication.

EDCS-08900401.summary: This is a dedication or epitaph to a woman named Seppia Laidis.

EDCS-08900402.text_edition: \n Publius Statius Hilarus in fronte pedes XII in agro pedes VIII G\n
EDCS-08900402.translation: Publius Statius Hilarus: 12 feet in front, 8 feet in the field.
EDCS-08900402.summary: This inscription records land measurements related to Publius Statius Hilarus, possibly for property boundaries or a land grant.

EDCS-08900403.text_edition: \n Locus Luci Vecili Basilidis vixit annos XII\n
EDCS-08900403.translation: The place of Lucius Vecilius Basilidis, who lived 12 years.
EDCS-08900403.summary: This inscription marks a burial plot or a place associated with Lucius Vecilius Basilidis, noting his age at death.

EDCS-09200305.text_edition: \n Caio Popillio Cai filio Maecia Prisco militi cohortis X urbanae |(cohortis) Avilli Maximi optioni a victoria Liciniana militavit annis VI mensibus III vixit annis XXVII mensibus VIII diebus VIII Turrania Publi filia Primilla cognato bene merenti\n
EDCS-09200305.translation: To Gaius Popillius Priscus, son of Gaius, of the Maecia tribe, soldier of the 10th Urban Cohort, under the optio Avillus Maximus, from the victory of Licinianus. He served for 6 years and 3 months, and lived 27 years, 8 months, and 8 days. Turrania Primilla, daughter of Publius, [set this up] for her deserving kinsman.
EDCS-09200305.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Popillius Priscus, a soldier who served in the 10th Urban Cohort, detailing his military service and lifespan, and was set up by Turrania Primilla.

EDCS-09300257.text_edition: \n ae Luci filiae Proculae Spuri filio Claudia Plaetorio quaestori aedili IIviro io Quinti filio Quirina Galeoni cui ordo decurionum ornamenta aedilicia decrevit ae Luci filiae Longinae filius Claudia Proculus quaestor IV praefectus pro IIviro IIvir quinquennalis tribunus militum legionis VI ferratae\n
EDCS-09300257.translation: To Procula, daughter of Lucius, wife of Spurius, daughter of Claudia Plaetorius, quaestor, aedile, duovir, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, Galeo, to whom the town council decreed the ornamenta aedilicia; to Longina, daughter of Lucius, daughter of Claudia, son of Proculus, quaestor, prefect for the duovirate, quinquennial duovir, military tribune of the VI Ferrata legion.
EDCS-09300257.summary: This inscription honors two women, Procula and Longina, and lists the careers of their sons, who held various magistracies and military positions.

EDCS-09400437.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vibiae Pothine Vibia Euphrosyne filia matri pientissimae et Theophanes coniugi merenti\n
EDCS-09400437.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Vibis Pothine, and for Theophanes, her most dutiful daughter, and her deserving husband.
EDCS-09400437.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Vibis Pothine, set up by her daughter and husband.

EDCS-09401427.text_edition: \n Quintus Caecilius Quinti libertus Humerus seviri \n
EDCS-09401427.translation: Quintus Caecilius Humerus, freedman of Quintus, a sevir.
EDCS-09401427.summary: This inscription records the name and affiliation of Quintus Caecilius Humerus, who was a sevir, likely in a collegium.

EDCS-09401429.text_edition: \n Lucio Naevio Luci filio Romilia Firmino Maniliano \n
EDCS-09401429.translation: To Lucius Naevius, son of Lucius, from the Romilia tribe, Firminus Manilianus.
EDCS-09401429.summary: This is a dedication or memorial inscription for Lucius Naevius, son of Lucius, possibly by Firminus Manilianus, mentioning his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-09401430.text_edition: \n Cereri sacrum Iulius Acilius Hermes pro honore seviratus dono dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-09401430.translation: Sacred to Ceres. Iulius Acilius Hermes dedicated and consecrated this as a gift for the honor of his sevirate.
EDCS-09401430.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Ceres by Iulius Acilius Hermes, commemorating his attainment of the office of sevir.

EDCS-09701489.text_edition: \n Iuliae Augustae matri castrorum Imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthi[ci maximi ------\n
EDCS-09701489.translation: To Iulia Augusta, mother of the camps, of the Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus [---
EDCS-09701489.summary: This is a dedication to Iulia Domna, mother of Emperor Caracalla and wife of Septimius Severus, likely as Augusta and Mater Castrorum.

EDCS-10200330.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavius Meiovius lived 16 years, 3 months, and 5 days. Born a Macedonian.
EDCS-10200330.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age and origin of Flavius Meiovius.

EDCS-10701750.translation: Eumenes, may you live! The Council and the People of Lilybaeum honored Alpeinius Magnus, the most distinguished consular and most pious judge, because of his benefactions to the fatherland, as patron.
EDCS-10701750.summary: This is a decree from the city of Lilybaeum honoring Alpeinius Magnus, a former consul and judge, for his services to the city.

EDCS-10701751.text_edition: \n Rufius Potianus vixit nnnos LXXII menses III qui omnibus municipalibus honoribus functus Quintus Rufius patri piissimo\n
EDCS-10701751.translation: Rufius Potianus lived 72 years and 3 months, having discharged all municipal offices; Quintus Rufius, to his most dutiful father.
EDCS-10701751.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Rufius Potianus, who lived 72 years and 3 months and held all municipal offices, set up by his son Quintus Rufius.

EDCS-10900644.text_edition: \n Auctario Primigene sic vixit annos XXI\n
EDCS-10900644.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of Auctarius Primigenius at death.

EDCS-10900646.text_edition: \n Caninia Epitychia vixit annos XVIII dies XIII\n
EDCS-10900646.translation: Caninia Epitychia lived 18 years and 13 days.
EDCS-10900646.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of Caninia Epitychia at death.

EDCS-10900650.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Coponia Amta vixit annos L\n
EDCS-10900650.translation: To the Divine Manes. Coponia Amta lived 50 years.
EDCS-10900650.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the Divine Manes, recording the age of Coponia Amta at death.

EDCS-10900653.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Euhodia Salutari sic filia vixit annis III mensibus II\n
EDCS-10900653.translation: To the Divine Manes. Euhodia Salutaris, thus, his daughter, lived 3 years and 2 months.
EDCS-10900653.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the Divine Manes, recording the age of Euhodia Salutaris at death.

EDCS-10900654.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Euplia vixit annos IV\n
EDCS-10900654.translation: To the Divine Manes. Euplia lived 4 years.
EDCS-10900654.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the Divine Manes, recording the age of Euplia at death.

EDCS-10900660.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of Rustia Asterium at death.

EDCS-10900661.text_edition: \n Lucius Sentius Luci libertus Liberalis vixit annos XXX\n
EDCS-10900661.translation: Lucius Sentius Liberalis, freedman of Lucius, lived 30 years.
EDCS-10900661.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of Lucius Sentius Liberalis at death.

EDCS-10900665.text_edition: \n Volumnia Chione pia have et tu have vixit nnos Agathopus fecit\n
EDCS-10900665.translation: Volumnia Chione the pious. Greetings. And you, greetings. Agathopus made [this]. Lived [unknown number of] years.
EDCS-10900665.summary: A funerary epitaph of Volumnia Chione, for whom a certain Agathopus provided the monument.

EDCS-11501745.translation: Ofillia Proba for herself and for Lucius Ofillius Faustus, her patron.
EDCS-11501745.summary: A funerary dedication by Ofillia Proba for herself and her patron, Lucius Ofillius Faustus.

EDCS-12001875.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Domitius Succesus lived 30 years. Domitia Cosmitice, his wife, made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-12001875.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Domitia Cosmitice for her husband, Domitius Succesus, who lived 30 years.

EDCS-12600252.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lepidia Cale lived for 22 years.
EDCS-12600252.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lepidia Cale, who died at the age of 22.

EDCS-12600317.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely the end of a name, possibly Eurotas.

EDCS-12800323.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12800323.text_edition: \n Pro salute et reditu et victoria Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Medici Parthici maximi liberorumque eius Annius Luci filius Lemonia Tertius IIvirum aedilis quaestor pecuniae publicae curator muneris publici gladiatorii ob honorem aedilitatis promisit ex sua pecunia HS XXV milia nummum ex quibus iussu Valeri Seponiani quaestoris Siciliae clarissimi memoriae viri in straturam plataeae sic Cererum sacrae HS XIII milia nummum numeravit et reliquos HS XII milia nummum dedit donis\n
EDCS-12800323.translation: For the safety, return, and victory of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Maximus, and his children. Annius Tertius, son of Lucius, of the Lemonia tribe, former IIvir, aedile, quaestor of public funds, curator of public gladiatorial games, promised from his own money 25,000 sesterces in honor of his aedileship, of which, by order of Valerius Seponianus, the most distinguished quaestor of Sicily, he paid 13,000 sesterces for the paving of the Sacred Square of Ceres, and gave the remaining 12,000 sesterces as gifts.
EDCS-12800323.summary: An inscription detailing a large financial contribution made by Annius Tertius for public games and paving in Sicily, dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-12800324.text_edition: \n opere plataeam sic vici Septizodi lapide Drepanitano sua pecunia stravit Quintus Fabius Quinti filius Maecia Caesilius Modius Titianus quaestor pro praetore provinciae Siciliae plataeam sic vici Septizodi sternendam et perficiendam curavit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-12800324.translation: Quintus Fabius Caesilius Modius Titianus, son of Quintus, of the Maecia tribe, quaestor pro praetore of the province of Sicily, paved the square of the Septizonium quarter with Drepanitan stone at his own expense, saw to the paving and completion of the square of the Septizonium quarter, and dedicated it.
EDCS-12800324.summary: An inscription recording the paving of a public square in Sicily by Quintus Fabius Caesilius Modius Titianus, who served as quaestor pro praetore.

EDCS-12800325.text_edition: \n Caio Mevio Quinti filio Donato Iuniano quaestori pro praetore provinciae Siciliae optimo et humanissimo XII tribus patrono\n
EDCS-12800325.translation: To Gaius Mevius Donatus Iunianus, son of Quintus, quaestor pro praetore of the province of Sicily, an excellent and most humane patron of the twelve tribes.
EDCS-12800325.summary: A dedication to Gaius Mevius Donatus Iunianus, recognizing his patronage of the twelve tribes and his service as quaestor pro praetore in Sicily.

EDCS-13200388.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Marcius Successus lived for 6 years, 7 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-13200388.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Marcius Successus, who died at a young age.

EDCS-13300513.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Octavius Thermitanus made this for Comicus, who deserved well.
EDCS-13300513.summary: A funerary dedication made by Octavius Thermitanus for Comicus.

EDCS-13800758.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Quintus Pompeius Sosius Clementianus, son and dearest infant, who lived for 7 months and 28 days. From his parents.
EDCS-13800758.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Pompeius Sosius Clementianus, an infant, dedicated by his parents.

EDCS-13800902.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Nomius made this for his pious wife, Pomponia Casta, slave of Domitius Domitianus, who lived for 25 years and 20 days.
EDCS-13800902.summary: A funerary epitaph for Pomponia Casta, wife of Nomius, dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-13801739.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Rosc(ia) Secundilla lived for 80 years.
EDCS-13801739.summary: A funerary epitaph for Rosc(ia) Secundilla, who lived to the age of 80.

EDCS-13801799.translation: To the departed spirits. Rubria Sozusa lived 30 years. Marcus Refrius Dubitatus made [this] for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13801799.summary: A funerary epitaph for Rubria Sozusa, set up by her husband Marcus Refrius Dubitatus.

EDCS-13900107.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lollia Irenus sic vixit annos XLIII Lucius Helvius Coprianus sic coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-13900107.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lollia Irenus lived 43 years. Lucius Helvius Coprianus made [this] for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-13900107.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lollia Irenus, set up by her husband Lucius Helvius Coprianus.

EDCS-13900430.text_edition: \n Numini praesentissimo Aesculapio Roscius Aelianus Salvius ut voverat restituta salute libens donum dedit Nonis Martiis Albino et Maximo conssulibus\n
EDCS-13900430.translation: To the ever-present divine power of Aesculapius. Roscius Aelianus Salvius, as he had vowed, gladly gave this gift upon his health being restored, on the Nones of March during the consulship of Albinus and Maximus.
EDCS-13900430.summary: A votive offering to Aesculapius by Roscius Aelianus Salvius, dated to March 7, 187 AD.

EDCS-14201009.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Statius Zosimus lived 65 years.
EDCS-14201009.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Statius Zosimus.

EDCS-14201029.translation: Stephanus lived [an unknown number of] years and [an unknown number of] days.
EDCS-14201029.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Stephanus, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-14700098.text_edition: \n Magno Pompeio Magni filio Pio imperatore augure consule designato portum et turres Lucius Plinius Luci filius Rufus legatus pro praetore praetor designatus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14700098.translation: Lucius Plinius Luci filius Rufus, propraetorian legate, praetor-designate, saw to the construction of the port and towers for Magnus Pompeius, son of Magnus, the pious emperor, augur, consul-designate.
EDCS-14700098.summary: A dedication by Lucius Plinius Rufus for the construction of a port and towers, commissioned by Magnus Pompeius, likely dating to the reign of Commodus.

EDCS-14800288.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Antonia Rufina lived 48 years, 6 months, and 15 days. Ant[onius...]
EDCS-14800288.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonia Rufina, recording her age and lifespan, possibly set up by a relative named Antonius.

EDCS-14800331.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aphrodisia lived 12 years. Quarta lived 14 years. Her mother Eutychia made [this].
EDCS-14800331.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aphrodisia and Quarta, set up by their mother Eutychia.

EDCS-14800510.translation: To the departed spirits. Arescusa [made this] for her well-deserving daughter Soteris, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-14800510.summary: A funerary epitaph for Soteris, set up by her mother Arescusa.

EDCS-14800832.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Atilius Onesimus lived 100 years [and...] 40 days. Rufius F[...]
EDCS-14800832.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Atilius Onesimus, recording an exceptionally long lifespan.

EDCS-14801304.translation: To the departed spirits. Thelonicus Caesar (aged) 15, made this for his family, who deserved well.
EDCS-14801304.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a family member named Thelonicus Caesar, who died at the age of 15.

EDCS-14802301.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Varius Dius. [---]
EDCS-14802301.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription.

EDCS-14802827.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Vibia Hagne lived 60 years. Vibius Alypus made this for his most excellent wife.
EDCS-14802827.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Vibius Alypus for his wife Vibia Hagne, who lived 60 years.

EDCS-14802972.translation: The two old men Vipsanius, Thalamus and [3]venna, were consecrated here.
EDCS-14802972.summary: This inscription records the consecration of two old men, Thalamus and [3]venna, possibly in a burial or memorial context.

EDCS-14805804.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cartilia Irene vixit nnos XXXVIII Claudius Theseus uxori sanctissimae\n
EDCS-14805804.translation: To the departed spirits. Cartilia Irene lived 38 years. Claudius Theseus (made this) for his most holy wife.
EDCS-14805804.summary: This funerary inscription by Claudius Theseus commemorates his wife Cartilia Irene, who died at age 38.

EDCS-14805805.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Claudianus vixit nnos XIIII Claudius Theseus pater filio dulcissimo\n
EDCS-14805805.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Claudianus lived 14 years. Claudius Theseus (made this) for his sweetest son.
EDCS-14805805.summary: This funerary inscription by Claudius Theseus commemorates his son Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, who died at age 14.

EDCS-14805806.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Licinio Liciniano vixit annis XXXVI Didia Pistis coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-14805806.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Licinius Licinianus lived 36 years. Didia Pistis (made this) for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-14805806.summary: This funerary inscription by Didia Pistis commemorates her husband Publius Licinius Licinianus, who died at age 36.

EDCS-15100631.translation: Marcus Favonius Amarantus, freedman of Marcus, and Marcus Cassius Heracla, his ward, made this.
EDCS-15100631.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Marcus Favonius Amarantus and his ward Marcus Cassius Heracla.

EDCS-15600432.translation: To the departed spirits. Cassia lived 18 years. Narcissus (made this) for her well-deserving wife. [He made it].
EDCS-15600432.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Narcissus for his wife Cassia, who died at age 18.

EDCS-16100301.text_edition: \n ianus Augusti libertus tabularius vixit annis XXII mensibus II diebus XI uxor et filius patri piissimo\n
EDCS-16100301.translation: Ianus Augusti, freedman and archivist, lived 22 years, 2 months, and 11 days. His wife and son (made this) for their most devoted father.
EDCS-16100301.summary: This funerary inscription by the wife and son commemorates Ianus Augusti, an archivist who died at age 22 years, 2 months, and 11 days.

EDCS-16200344.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Rupilius Onesimus to his wife Acceae Calliope, who deserved well of him.
EDCS-16200344.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband to his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16600237.text_edition: \n Tito Fulvio Aurelio Antonino Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti filio Lucius Aponius Rufinus ob honorem seviratus pecunia sua Pompeianis vita ob insignem iustitiam et merita litterarum et amorem quem non solum circa patriam sed per omnem provinciam conlocavit Iulio Claudio Peristerio Pompeiano viro clarissimo ex consulari provinciae Siciliae universa curia in coetu splendidu sic suo patrono digno et praestantissimo statuam conlocavit Amazoniis vita\n
EDCS-16600237.translation: Lucius Aponius Rufinus, for the honor of the sevirate, at his own expense, erected this statue to Titus Fulvius Aurelius Antoninus, son of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, for his outstanding justice and merits in letters and the love he showed not only to his homeland but throughout the entire province; the entire senate, gathered in a splendid assembly, erected this statue to their worthy and most excellent patron, Julius Claudius Peristerius Pompeianus, a most distinguished man, former consular of the province of Sicily, to be honored with Amazonian games.
EDCS-16600237.summary: A public dedication of a statue to Titus Fulvius Aurelius Antoninus by Lucius Aponius Rufinus and the curia, commemorating his sevirate and his merits, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17201527.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Anthimus lived 62 years, 6 months, 3 hours. Flavia Faustina made this for her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-17201527.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Anthimus and dedicated by his wife Flavia Faustina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17900191.summary: A fragmentary epitaph recording a person's lifespan and self-dedication, dating uncertain due to damage.

EDCS-17900195.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Magulnia Chia.
EDCS-17900195.summary: A funerary dedication to Magulnia Chia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20401555.translation: Lucius Caecilius Martiales, son of Aulus, of the tribe Petaius, superintendent, restored the temples of the Forum gods and adorned them with his own money.
EDCS-20401555.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of temples and their adornment by Lucius Caecilius Martiales, likely a public official, dating uncertain but possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21900323.translation: [...]. [Publius] Cornelius Grammeus, son of Publius, grandson of Publius, and Publius Cornelius Longinus(?), son of Publius, grandson of Publius, father and son, made the gymnasium for the honor of the duovirate.
EDCS-21900323.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a gymnasium by a father and son for the honor of the duovirate, likely from a Roman provincial city.

EDCS-21900333.text_edition: \n Vernantibus saeculis dddominorum nnnostrorum genio splendidae urbis Catinae Facundus Porfyrius Mynatidius vir clarissimus consularis eiusdem\n
EDCS-21900333.translation: In the flourishing ages of our lords, for the spirit of the splendid city of Catania, Facundus Porfyrius Mynatidius, a most distinguished consular, of the same city.
EDCS-21900333.summary: A dedication invoking prosperity for the city of Catania and its rulers, attributed to Facundus Porfyrius Mynatidius, a consular official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21900336.text_edition: \n Plurium saeculorum iniuria nymfeum informi tabe foedatum cuius etiam aqua licet antiquo meatu tamen corruptione formarum ita fuerat polluta ut quandam quasi noxam haurientibus inferre videretur providentia Flavi Arsini viri clarissimi consularis provinciae Siciliae restitutum adque sic usui populi splendidissimi Catinensium redditum reformatumque est curante Flavio Ambrosio viro perfectissimo d de aere publico\n
EDCS-21900336.translation: Due to the damage of many ages, a nymphaeum, disfigured by ugly decay, whose water, though flowing from an ancient channel, had become so polluted by the corruption of its forms as to seem to inflict a kind of harm on those drinking it, was restored by the foresight of Flavius Arsinius, a most distinguished consular of the province of Sicily, and thus returned to use for the most splendid people of Catania, and was reformed, under the care of Flavius Ambrosius, a most perfect man, at public expense.
EDCS-21900336.summary: An inscription detailing the restoration of a polluted nymphaeum in Catania by the provincial consular Flavius Arsinius and supervised by Flavius Ambrosius, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-21900342.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Quintus Atilius Severus, son of Quintus, of the Claudian tribe, former prefect of craftsmen, twice a magistrate elected by the votes of the people.
EDCS-21900342.summary: A funerary dedication for Quintus Atilius Severus, who held various public offices including prefect of craftsmen and duovir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21900345.translation: Quintus Lutatius, quaestor by decree of the decurions, aedile, for the honor [he provided] a dining room with three lecti (?).
EDCS-21900345.summary: This inscription records the public works or benefactions of Quintus Lutatius, a quaestor and aedile, likely dating to the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-21900360.translation: She lived 60 years. Amabilis to her excellent nurse, deserving of her. Likewise Crescenta, lived 25 years. Amabilis to her excellent husband, deserving of him.
EDCS-21900360.summary: This epitaph records the death of Amabilis' nurse and her husband, Crescenta, with Amabilis erecting the monument for both, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21900368.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aphrodito, a mime actress, lived 40 years. Eutychus made [this] for her deserving wife.
EDCS-21900368.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aphrodito, a mime actress, and was erected by her husband Eutychus, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21900378.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Cassius Hermes, best of men, lived 60 years. His four sons set [this up] for their deserving father.
EDCS-21900378.summary: This epitaph honors Lucius Cassius Hermes, a highly regarded man, and was erected by his four sons, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21900379.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Ceparius Nymphidianus lived 34 years. His mother made [this].
EDCS-21900379.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Ceparius Nymphidianus and was erected by his mother, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21900383.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Tito? Atiliano? Lived...
EDCS-21900383.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription mentions Tito? Atiliano? and records that he lived, but the full details are lost.

EDCS-21900388.translation: Gnaeus Fabius Felix, father, and Antonia Sympherusa, mother, [for their son] Gnaeus Fabius Titianus, lived [years...]
EDCS-21900388.summary: This epitaph records the death of Gnaeus Fabius Titianus and was erected by his parents, Gnaeus Fabius Felix and Antonia Sympherusa.

EDCS-21900396.translation: Lucius Lollius Daphnus lies here. He lived 50 years.
EDCS-21900396.summary: This is a simple epitaph recording the burial and lifespan of Lucius Lollius Daphnus.

EDCS-21900408.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Publicius Nicon lived 55 years. Pompeia Epictesis made [this].
EDCS-21900408.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Publicius Nicon and was erected by Pompeia Epictesis, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-21900409.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Publicia Ifis lived 68 years. Publius Pedius Severus, patron, made [this] // Sacred to the departed spirits. Publius Pedius Marianus lived 25 years and Publicia Ifis. Titus Pedius Severus, son, aged 18 years. Pedius Severus and Arria Volumnilla, mother, and Gaius Pedius Severus, son, made [this].
EDCS-21900409.summary: This complex funerary monument appears to record multiple family members, including Publicia Ifis, Publius Pedius Marianus, Titus Pedius Severus, and others, erected by different family members over time.

EDCS-21900411.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Sextilius Stephanio [erected this] for his most devoted father, Sextilius Severus, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-21900411.summary: A funerary dedication to a father by his son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21900416.translation: To the departed spirits. Eutyches made [this] for his most devoted wife, Statilia Hermais, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-21900416.summary: A funerary epitaph for a wife, erected by her husband, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21900418.translation: To the departed spirits. Tyche lived 14 years, 3 months, and 21 days. Her parents erected [this].
EDCS-21900418.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl, Tyche, erected by her parents, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21900419.translation: To the departed spirits. Culucvitas lived 2 years. Tyche and Herma made [this].
EDCS-21900419.summary: A funerary epitaph for a very young child, Culucvitas, erected by Tyche and Herma, likely parents, from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21900423.translation: To the departed spirits. Vipsanius Atticus [erected this] for himself and his family while alive.
EDCS-21900423.summary: A funerary dedication for a family, set up by Vipsanius Atticus during his lifetime, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21900434.text_edition: \n Iuliae Florentinae infanti dulcissimae atque innocentissimae fideli factae parens conlocavit quae pridie Nonas Martias ante lucem pagana nata Zoilo correctore provinciae mense octavo decimo et vices<i=T>ma secunda die completis fidelis facta hora noctis octava ultimum spiritum agens supervixit horis quattuor ita ut consueta repeteret ac defuncta Hyble hora diei prima septimum Kalendas Octobres cuius occasum cum uterque parens omni momento fleret per noctem maiestatis vox extitit quae defunctam lamentari prohiberet cuius corpus pro foribus martyrorum cum loculo suo per prosbiterum sic humatum est IIII Nonas Octobres \n
EDCS-21900434.translation: To the sweetest and most innocent and faithful infant Julia Florentina, her parent erected [this]. Born pagan on the day before the Nones of March, with Zoilus as corrector of the province, in her eighteenth month and twenty-second day, faithful, she completed her life in the eighth hour of the night, and after four hours she breathed her last, so that she repeated her usual actions. Having died on the first hour of the day on the seventh day before the Kalends of October, while both parents wept every moment, a voice of majesty during the night forbade them to lament her. Her body was buried with her coffin outside the doors of the martyrs by the presbyter on the fourth day before the Nones of October.
EDCS-21900434.summary: A detailed funerary epitaph for an infant named Julia Florentina, recounting her birth, death, and burial, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-21900478.text_edition: \n Caio Vistilio filio Clustumina Rufo curatori ollas XII sua pecunia emit Atilia MMarcorum |(mulieris) liberta Gymnas\n
EDCS-21900478.translation: Atilia Marcorum, freedwoman of the gymnasium, bought 12 pots for her son Gaius Vistilius Clustuminus Rufus, curator, with her own money.
EDCS-21900478.summary: A private purchase record by a freedwoman for her son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-21900533.translation: Sacred to Concordia of the Agrigentines. The republic of the Lilybitani dedicate [this], with Marcus Haterius Candidus as proconsul and Lucius Cornelius Marcellus as quaestor and pro-praetor.
EDCS-21900533.summary: A dedication to the goddess Concordia by the city of Lilybaeum, made during the proconsulship of M. Haterius Candidus and the quaestorship of L. Cornelius Marcellus, likely from the late Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-22000790.translation: To our most clement and victorious lord, Flavius Valerius Constantinus Maximus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus. Betitius Perpetuus, a most distinguished man and corrector of the province of Sicily, devoted to his divine majesty and greatness, always dedicated.
EDCS-22000790.summary: A dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great by Betitius Perpetuus, the corrector of Sicily, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-22000808.translation: Marcus Paconius Vipsanius Proculus, son of Lucius, of the Voltinia tribe, aedile, built the temple to the Genius of the colony of Augusta Lilybitanorum with his own money.
EDCS-22000808.summary: A dedication of a temple to the Genius of the colony of Lilybaeum by the aedile Marcus Paconius Vipsanius Proculus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22000809.translation: Lucius Pinarius Rufus, on account of the honor of the aedilship, dedicated a silver statue of the Genius of the municipality of Lilybaeum, weighing 5 pounds, at his own expense.
EDCS-22000809.summary: This is a private dedication by Lucius Pinarius Rufus of a silver statue of the Genius of Lilybaeum, likely made upon completion of his term as aedile.

EDCS-22000813.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Domitiano Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatori VII consuli X patri patriae opus aque ductus exstructum supra terram et lapidibus quadratis a novo tectum\n
EDCS-22000813.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, Imperator for the 7th time, Consul for the 10th time, Father of the Fatherland: an aqueduct, built above ground with squared stones, a new roof.
EDCS-22000813.summary: This is an inscription recording the construction of an aqueduct, possibly a public works project under Emperor Domitian, dated to his reign.

EDCS-22000820.translation: To Domitius Zenophilus, vir clarissimus, corrector of the province of Sicily, for his outstanding mildness and benevolent administration, a most strenuous and praiseworthy judge.
EDCS-22000820.summary: This is an honorary inscription to Domitius Zenophilus, a vir clarissimus and corrector of Sicily, recognizing his judicial qualities.

EDCS-22000825.text_edition: \n Tito Quartio Masculo Titi Quarti Crescentinii Quirina filio\n
EDCS-22000825.translation: To Titus Quartius Masculus, son of Titus Quartius Crescentinius, of the tribe Quirina.
EDCS-22000825.summary: This is a brief dedication or memorial to Titus Quartius Masculus, providing his name and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-22000826.translation: The council and people of the city of Lilybaeum to their perpetual patron, the councillors (Greek: οἱ δεκορίωνες) to Marcus Valerius Diognetus, son of Megas(?), Chortus, their benefactor.
EDCS-22000826.summary: This is a bilingual (Latin and Greek) honorary inscription from the city of Lilybaeum to Marcus Valerius Chortus, recognizing him as a perpetual patron and benefactor.

EDCS-22000849.translation: To the deified son of the deified [Emperor], Lucius Sempronius(?)...
EDCS-22000849.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely dedicating something to a deified emperor and possibly another individual.

EDCS-22000856.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 17th time, Consul for the 3rd time, for the republic of the people of Panhormus.
EDCS-22000856.summary: This is an official dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, likely by the city of Panhormus, listing his full titulature and imperial lineage.

EDCS-22000860.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, the Arab, the Adiabene, the Parthian the greatest, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the sixth time, imperator for the eleventh time, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul, son of the Emperor Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici, brother of divi Commodi, nephew of divi Antonini Pii, great-nephew of divi Hadriani, great-great-nephew of divi Traiani Parthici, great-great-great-nephew of divi Nervae, the most indulgent and most clement prince, our lord, by decree of the town council of the magistrates Publius Satyrius Donatus and Marcus Maecius Rufinus of the town of Panormus.
EDCS-22000860.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus by the magistrates of Panormus, likely erected in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22000861.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, Imperator for the 11th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Father of the Fatherland, son of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power, [brother], to our most indulgent lord: for the republic of the people of Panhormus, by decree of the IIviri Publius Satyrius Donatus and Marcus Maecius Rufus. [[To Lucius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar]] [[[son]]] [[[brother]]]
EDCS-22000861.summary: This is an official dedication to Emperors Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), with a deleted section possibly referring to Geta, by the city of Panhormus.

EDCS-22000862.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power, Proconsul, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, to our most indulgent and most merciful prince and lord: by Maesia Fabia Titiana, clarissima femina, and Maesius Fabius Titianus, clarissimus puer.
EDCS-22000862.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Caracalla by Maesia Fabia Titiana and Maesius Fabius Titianus, listing the emperor's extensive titulature and lineage.

EDCS-22000864.translation: To the Divine Emperor, Caesar, grandson of the Divine Severus Pius, son of the Divine Antoninus.
EDCS-22000864.summary: This is a titulus inscription for a deceased emperor, likely Antoninus Pius or Marcus Aurelius, identified by his parentage and divine lineage.

EDCS-22000865.translation: To the Divine Emperor, Caesar, son of the Divine Antoninus Magnus, grandson of the Divine Septimius Severus, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country, by decree of the town councillors of the colony Augusta Panhormitanorum.
EDCS-22000865.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Severus Alexander, listing his titles and honors, set up by the city of Panhormus (Palermo).

EDCS-22000867.translation: To the Divine Claudius, by the state of the Panhormitani.
EDCS-22000867.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Emperor Claudius, set up by the community of Panhormus (Palermo).

EDCS-22000868.translation: To the Divine Emperor, Caesar, Gaius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul, by decree of the town councillors of the Panhormitani, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-22000868.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, listing his imperial titles and honors, set up by the community of Panhormus (Palermo).

EDCS-22000870.translation: To the Restorer of liberty and founder of public safety, our lord Licinianus Licinius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Domitius Latronianus, a man of consular rank, corrector of the province of Sicily, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-22000870.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Licinius, honoring him as a restorer of liberty and founder of security, set up by Domitius Latronianus, the corrector of Sicily.

EDCS-22000871.translation: To the Gods of the Underworld. Publius Aelius Felix and his wife made this for their most dutiful and sweetest son, Publius Aelius Verus, a Roman equestrian of the first troop, who lived 12 years, 10 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-22000871.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy, Publius Aelius Verus, who died at age 12, set up by his parents.

EDCS-22000876.translation: To Ulpius Severus, of Raetian nationality, an equestrian soldier of the Emperor from the troop of Busturonus, who lived 38 years, [lacuna]. Mansuetus and Titus, his heirs, made this for him, a deserving man.
EDCS-22000876.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ulpius Severus, an imperial equestrian soldier, who died at age 38, set up by his heirs.

EDCS-22000883.translation: Flamma, a secutor gladiator, lived 30 years. He fought 34 times, won 21, stood his ground 7, and was dismissed 4 times. He was of Syrian nationality. Delicatus made this for him, a deserving comrade.
EDCS-22000883.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flamma, a successful secutor gladiator, who died at age 30, set up by his comrade.

EDCS-22100054.translation: Quintus Decumius, son of Quintus, duovir, [built] the colonnade at his own expense.
EDCS-22100054.summary: This is an inscription recording a public building project, the construction of a portico, funded by Quintus Decumius, a duovir.

EDCS-22100076.translation: Hail to Publius Alfesius Salvius, son of Publius, who lived 19 years.
EDCS-22100076.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Alfesius Salvius, who died at age 19.

EDCS-22100077.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Marcus Antonius Natalis.

EDCS-22100086.translation: To Publius Cestius Catullus, aged 27, and to Publius Cestius Catullus, his father.
EDCS-22100086.summary: A funerary dedication for Publius Cestius Catullus and his father, also named Publius Cestius Catullus.

EDCS-22100087.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Publius Cestius Catullus.

EDCS-22100088.translation: To Cestia Catulla, dutiful, lived 35 years.
EDCS-22100088.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Cestia Catulla.

EDCS-22100092.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Claudius Maximus.

EDCS-22100102.translation: Domitia Chimenna [dedicates this monument to] Aulus Aemilius Aristomenes and his [family]; this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-22100102.summary: A funerary monument by Domitia Chimenna for Aulus Aemilius Aristomenes and his family, intended to be perpetual.

EDCS-22100103.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Ducenius Epaphroditus lived 72 years.
EDCS-22100103.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Ducenius Epaphroditus.

EDCS-22100591.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII imperator IIII consul V pater patriae\n
EDCS-22100591.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 4th time, consul, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-22100591.summary: A titulature inscription for Emperor Trajan, likely part of a larger monument, listing his official titles and powers.

EDCS-22100592.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Verus Caesar, consul, son of Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, at public expense by decree of the curial body.
EDCS-22100592.summary: A dedication of a statue or monument to Marcus Aurelius Verus, son of Antoninus Pius, funded by public money and decreed by local officials.

EDCS-22100594.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Aurelius [Verus] Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, by decree of the curial body of the colony Augusta Tyndaritanorum, under the supervision of Marcus Valerius Vitalis, curator of the public affairs.
EDCS-22100594.summary: A dedication of a monument by the colony of Tyndaris to Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus, listing his imperial titles and the officials responsible for its erection.

EDCS-22100597.translation: To Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, and of the camp; from the public treasury of the colony of Tyndaris, devoted to the divine power.
EDCS-22100597.summary: This is a dedication by the colony of Tyndaris to Julia Mamaea, mother of Emperor Alexander Severus.

EDCS-22100608.translation: To Cornelius Mansuetus, procurator of Tiberius Caesar Augustus and Julia Augusta, from the decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-22100608.summary: This is a dedication to Cornelius Mansuetus, a procurator under Tiberius and Julia Augusta, funded by public money.

EDCS-22100609.translation: Lucius [---]onnius, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Quirina, Nomentanus, in his own name and that of his colleague Clodius Thorianus, has given from the bronze of his aedilician year the remaining money from his own funds; the place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22100609.summary: This inscription records a donation by L. [---]onnius Nomentanus and his colleague for a public space, funded by remaining money from their aedilician year.

EDCS-22100610.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Munatia Paula, Quintus Laternina, his most devoted mother.
EDCS-22100610.summary: This is a tombstone for Munatia Paula, dedicated by her son Quintus Laternina.

EDCS-22100613.translation: Chrestion, freedman of Augustus, procurator of the islands of Malta and Gaul, restored the columns with their entablatures and the walls of the temple of the goddess Proserpina, which were on the verge of ruin due to age, and he also gilded the pillar.
EDCS-22100613.summary: This inscription records the restoration of the temple of Proserpina and its gilded pillar by Chrestion, the procurator of Malta and Gaul.

EDCS-22100620.translation: To Ceres. Lollia C. f. Lutatia, priestess and perpetual priestess of the Emperor, wife of Marcus Livius Optatus, son of Marcus, of the voting tribe Quirina, flamen of Gaul, and of Julia Augusta, perpetual priestess of the Emperor, together with her five children, dedicated this at her own expense.
EDCS-22100620.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Ceres by Lollia Lutatia, a priestess and wife of a flamen, along with her children.

EDCS-22100623.translation: To our lord Gaius Aurelius Valerius Constantius Augustus. From the public treasury of the people of Gaul, under the administration of F. Pollio and Rufus M. F., IIvirs.
EDCS-22100623.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantius Augustus by the people of Gaul, during the magistracy of F. Pollio and Rufus.

EDCS-22100624.translation: To our lord Marcus Galerius Valerius Maximianus Augustus. From the public treasury of the people of Gaul, under the administration of LV[---] D[---] Pollio and Ruf[---] A[---]annius, IIvirs.
EDCS-22100624.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Maximian Augustus by the people of Gaul, during the magistracy of LV[---] Pollio and Rufus.

EDCS-22100626.translation: To Gaius(?) Vallius(?) Postumus, son of Quirina, patron of the municipality, perpetual flamen of the deified Hadrian, appointed to the five decuries of judges from the quadringentary class by divine Antoninus Augustus Pius, who most honorably discharged all the offices of his city, and also undertook a gratuitous legation to the deified Hadrian and to the most eminent order concerning the recall of revenues; the people of Gaul, from a collection of money, by decree of the decurions, for his many merits.
EDCS-22100626.summary: This inscription honors C. Vallius Postumus, patron of Gaul, for his service as a flamen and his legation, funded by public contributions.

EDCS-22100627.translation: To Marcus Vallius Rufus, son of Caius, of the voting tribe Quirina, who was honored with the equestrian rank by the deified Antoninus Augustus Pius; the people of Gaul, from money collected, for his merits and in solace for Caius Valli Postumus, patron of the municipality, his father [---]atem.
EDCS-22100627.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Vallius Rufus for his merits and in solace for his father, C. Valli Postumus, funded by the people of Gaul.

EDCS-22100631.translation: Gaius Julius Felix lived for X years. Appuleia Rogata lived for X years.
EDCS-22100631.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the ages of two individuals, Gaius Julius Felix and Appuleia Rogata.

EDCS-22200391.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Domitia L. [---]
EDCS-22200391.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits, mentioning the name Domitia.

EDCS-22200395.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Caius Fretensis Numerius lived for 35 years.
EDCS-22200395.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Caius Fretensis Numerius.

EDCS-22200397.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Nasidia Epitynchanusa lived for X years.
EDCS-22200397.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Nasidia Epitynchanusa.

EDCS-22200399.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Refrius Restitutus lived for 60 years. Restitutus, pious [---]
EDCS-22200399.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Refrius Restitutus, recording his age and mentioning his piety.

EDCS-22200403.translation: [Sacred] to the Divine Spirits. [---]onius [---]tus lived for 17 years. [---]
EDCS-22200403.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of an individual whose name is mostly lost.

EDCS-22901665.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus Augustus. [---]ius, a most perfect man.
EDCS-22901665.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine, mentioning a high-ranking official named Ius.

EDCS-23000016.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a property or ownership mark, belonging to Sextus Murrius Pisanus.

EDCS-23000024.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a property or ownership mark, belonging to Sextus Murrius Pisanus.

EDCS-23000043.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a property or ownership mark, belonging to Sextus Murrius Pisanus.

EDCS-23001424.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, mentioning Sextus Murrius Festus, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23001459.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, mentioning Gnaeus Ateius, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23300038.summary: This inscription records the name 'Magnus' alongside 'emperor', likely referring to a ruling emperor or a military figure of high rank, possibly from the Late Roman period.

EDCS-23300065.translation: By the authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, prefect of the city.
EDCS-23300065.summary: This inscription indicates an act performed under the authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, the prefect of the city, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-23300076.summary: This is a possessive inscription, possibly indicating ownership of an object or property by Numerius Marcus Plaetorius Celer, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-23300095.translation: Of Titus Clodius Saturninus, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-23300095.summary: This inscription likely refers to Titus Clodius Saturninus, a prominent individual, possibly indicating a dedication or ownership, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-23300185.translation: Of the Caecilii, Servilianus and Potitus Herennianus the younger.
EDCS-23300185.summary: This inscription likely refers to members of the Caecilii family, possibly a joint dedication or a record of lineage, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-23300193.summary: This inscription likely refers to Gaius Licinius Cornutus Gabinianus, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-23400467.translation: To the departed gods. Helvius Titus lived 55 years. They made [this] for a well-deserving citizen.
EDCS-23400467.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Helvius Titus, who lived 55 years, dedicated by unspecified individuals, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25601300.translation: To the son of Caius, Augustus.
EDCS-25601300.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, mentioning the son of Caius, named Augustus, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25601302.translation: To M... Hirtianus, who was also called Lupo...
EDCS-25601302.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely commemorating a person named Hirtianus, possibly identified by the cognomen Lupo, with the exact nature and recipient of the dedication unclear due to lacunae.

EDCS-25601304.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Aphrodisia, daughter of Sozomenus...
EDCS-25601304.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Aphrodisia, daughter of Sozomenus, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-25601306.summary: This is a short inscription, possibly a dedication or a memorial, from Saturninus to someone named Canace.

EDCS-25601313.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of a person named Limnaio, who died at the age of 22.

EDCS-25601320.translation: Gaius Publicius Clarus, known as Auctus.
EDCS-25601320.summary: This inscription appears to be a name and cognomen, possibly on a tombstone or a dedication, for Gaius Publicius Clarus, also called Auctus.

EDCS-26001192.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelius Acathobus sic vixit nnos LV\n
EDCS-26001192.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Cornelius Acathobus lived 55 years.
EDCS-26001192.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Cornelius Acathobus lived for 55 years.

EDCS-30000433.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Annius Antoninus, the freedman of Fabianus, made this for his excellent father.
EDCS-30000433.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Annius Antoninus, a freedman of Fabianus, dedicating a monument to his father.

EDCS-30400524.translation: Work of dolia manufacturing from the Kaninian lands of Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30400524.summary: This inscription records the production of dolia (large earthenware jars) from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, and is dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600217.translation: Work of dolia manufacturing from the Licinian lands of Anicetus, freedman of Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600217.summary: This inscription records the production of dolia by Anicetus, a freedman of Domitia Lucilla, and is dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-32701116.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely beginning with a dedication to Augustus, with the remainder lost.

EDCS-32701122.text_edition: \n Iulia Gamice vixit nnos LXX hic sita est Hygia filia matri optimae\n
EDCS-32701122.translation: Iulia Gamice lived 70 years, she is buried here. Hygia, daughter, to her excellent mother.
EDCS-32701122.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Iulia Gamice and its dedication by her daughter.

EDCS-32701126.text_edition: \n Sexto Nonio Sexti filio Quirina Africano NPP vixsit sic annos XXIIX Caeciliae Zoticae Sextus Nonius Albanus optimae uxori fecit epulum singulis HS nummum dignum eius\n
EDCS-32701126.translation: Sextus Nonius Africanus, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, lived 28 years. Sextus Nonius Albanus made a feast for his excellent wife Caeciliae Zoticae, worthy of her, for 100 sesterces each.
EDCS-32701126.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caeciliae Zoticae, set up by her husband Sextus Nonius Albanus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32900046.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or ownership mark, naming Gaius Cornelius Ursus.

EDCS-34700077.translation: To Gaius Valerius Apollinaris, a most excellent man, corrector of the province of Sicily, a unique example of abstinence, integrity, and innocence, a judge without any favour, with scales of justice. Gaius Valerius Pompeianus, a most excellent man, curator of the public affairs of Lilybaeum, to his eternal patron. May the gods preserve you.
EDCS-34700077.summary: A dedication to Gaius Valerius Apollinaris, former corrector of Sicily and judge, by Gaius Valerius Pompeianus, curator of Lilybaeum, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-34700333.translation: Roof tile from the tileworks of Iulia Procula, under consuls Quintus Articulaeus Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-34700333.summary: An inscription on a roof tile indicating its origin from the tileworks of Iulia Procula and the consuls of the year, likely the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-35400847.translation: Under consuls Apronianus and Paetinus, of Publius Decimius Epitynchanus.
EDCS-35400847.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning the consuls Apronianus and Paetinus and Publius Decimius Epitynchanus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-36100342.translation: Doliare work of Dionysius, of Domitia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, under consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-36100342.summary: An inscription on a doliare (pottery) work identifying the maker (Dionysius), the owner (Domitia Lucilla), and the consuls of the year, likely the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-39101690.translation: [3] my name [3] Primus [3] I suffered much [3] his parents [3] and they ask of God [for him?] [3] to live to see [3]
EDCS-39101690.summary: A heavily damaged funerary inscription in Greek, lamenting the deceased and possibly asking for prayers.

EDCS-39101691.translation: You see the tomb, passerby, of the renowned Rhodogounes, whom a wicked man slew with stones. Her husband Abianius grieved and mourned for his wife, and offered this favour with this small stele. My name was formerly called by all Epagathos; now Rhodogounes, the namesake of a queen.
EDCS-39101691.summary: A funerary epigram in Greek commemorating Rhodogounes, who was murdered, and detailing her husband Abianius's grief and the change in her name.

EDCS-39101694.translation: The renowned city of the pious, the prosperous one, erected this. Zosymiades, the agonothetes (organizer of games), Severus. So that also for future mortals [3]++ZE+ [3]
EDCS-39101694.summary: A Greek inscription dedicated to Severus, an agonothetes, by Zosymiades, likely honoring him for his role in public games in a prosperous city.

EDCS-39101979.translation: While he was priest of the Syrian goddess, Tiberius, son of Tiberius, a pontifex, son of Dapsilus Amphilobus, whose wife was Paeadia, daughter of Theodorus, son of Morreccinus, who performed the rites for the presidents of the Syrian goddess each year: Publius Plot[i]us, son of Publius; Marcus Pomponius Di[...]; Lucius Lucinius [...]; Lucius Octavius L[...]; Titus Phaecius [...]; Attale [...]; Eutich[...].
EDCS-39101979.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription listing officials, likely related to the priesthood and administration of the cult of the Syrian goddess, with several names in the dative case suggesting a dedication or honorific purpose, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102081.summary: This is a simple tomb inscription in Greek, identifying the deceased as Hermadion, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102175.translation: Silvanos, deacon of the [clergy/order] from the Kalends, died on the 11th of June.
EDCS-39102175.summary: This is a Greek inscription recording the death of Silvanos, identified as a deacon, and specifying a date (June 11th), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102239.translation: To Polyestephanos, his saviour, Nicomedes, also called Dionysus, [dedicated this as] a saviour.
EDCS-39102239.summary: This is a Greek dedication in theophoric style, where Nicomedes, who also went by the name Dionysus, dedicates something to Polyestephanos, possibly a deity or a person, functioning as a 'saviour', likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102256.translation: Imylch, son of Imilchon / Iniballos Chlōros made a hospitality agreement with Lysons, son of Diognetos, and his descendants.
EDCS-39102256.summary: This is a Greek inscription recording a formal hospitality agreement (xenian) between Imylch/Iniballos Chlōros and Lysons and his descendants, likely from the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702040.translation: Pompeia, [daughter] of Rufus, Hail Mega! Hail Mega!
EDCS-55702040.summary: This is a Greek inscription, possibly a funerary epitaph, addressing Pompeia and repeating the greeting 'Mega xaīre' (Hail Mega), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61800276.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription in Latin, mentioning what appears to be a workshop ('officina') and the name Eusebius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400029.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Curtius Oiadus [erected this for] his wife Fufica Calene, and for Valeria, well-deserving.
EDCS-64400029.summary: This is a Latin funerary inscription dedicated to the deceased wife of Curtius Oiadus, Fufica Calene, and also to Valeria, possibly another relative, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400033.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead and to eternal sleep, security, and everlasting memory. For Aelia Flavia Melitana, who lived 31 years, 3 months, 10 days. Titus Flavius Hymnus to his most holy, most sweet wife, with whom he lived 13 years. Titus Flavius Collina Marcellus and Titianus and Flavia Ampelle, daughter of Titus, to their most dutiful mother. They made and consecrated a sarcophagus with figures and bases, on the Ides of August, when Pudens and Orfitus were consuls.
EDCS-64400033.summary: This is a detailed Latin funerary inscription for Aelia Flavia Melitana, recording her lifespan and the duration of her marriage, with the monument commissioned by her husband and children, and dated by the consulship to 146 AD.

EDCS-64400329.translation: Heraclius, son of Dionysius, the Petreine, from the market officials, at his own expense.
EDCS-64400329.summary: This is a Greek inscription recording that Heraclius, son of Dionysius, identified as 'Petreine' and an 'agoranomos' (market official), paid for something from his own funds, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400407.translation: To the Underworld Gods. Ianaris Florentis, good and blameless, lived 45 years, 7 months, 8 days. She was taken to the earth blamelessly. Victorina Donata, venerable and irreproachable, [perhaps?] beloved of the gods.
EDCS-64400407.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ianaris Florentis, recorded by Victorina Donata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400458.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely referring to an emperor, possibly Magnus Maximus, from the late Roman period.

EDCS-65200115.translation: Gaius Vibius, son of Lucius Vibius Barbara; Lollia his wife; Agbor, son of Boukis; Gaius Rammius Comedia; Tittolla, daughter of Gnaeus Istanus; Gaius Istanus; Lollis, brother of Lollia; Titinis Lycus; and all the PERATOI.
EDCS-65200115.summary: A fragmentary inscription listing numerous Roman family members and individuals, likely from the Roman Imperial period, possibly related to legal or familial transactions.

EDCS-65300059.summary: An inscription indicating the authority of Quintus Salvidienus, possibly a legate or other official, associated with the Tenth Legion, from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-65300060.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning Salvidienus and the Tenth Legion, likely from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-65700670.summary: A very brief inscription with a Greek name, likely a personal dedication or ownership mark from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65700710.summary: A very brief inscription with a Greek rendering of a Roman name, likely a personal dedication or ownership mark from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65900089.summary: A short inscription listing three personal names, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-66000001.translation: [...] Grypianus' freedman for himself and for Phrontis his freedwoman.
EDCS-66000001.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for a freedman and his freedwoman, Grypianus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67400213.summary: A fragmentary inscription with Latin and Greek names, possibly related to ownership or a dedication, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-67700122.summary: This is a dedication to household gods, with the name Longinus preserved.

EDCS-70900650.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription possibly from Heracleia, with the name Lys preserved.

EDCS-70900652.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription with possible names Nike and Kesson.

EDCS-70900747.translation: Lapos, son of Sabinus the elder, [---]teuta[---].
EDCS-70900747.summary: This inscription fragment mentions Lapos, son of the elder Sabinus.

EDCS-70900748.translation: A vow to Panches(?) and Ioannes(?), brothers.
EDCS-70900748.summary: This inscription records a vow made by brothers named Panches(?) and Ioannes(?).

EDCS-70900754.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory epigram praising someone named Theodore.

EDCS-70900786.summary: This inscription records that Gelas made the monument, with the name Korydos also preserved.

EDCS-70900818.translation: Paramona, excellent and blameless, farewell. Lived [---] years, 11 months.
EDCS-70900818.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Paramona, who lived approximately [---] years and 11 months.

EDCS-70900982.translation: Earth and Hermes, chthonic gods, receive the servant of Rufus, Benouta.
EDCS-70900982.summary: This inscription is a prayer to chthonic gods to receive Benouta, the servant of Rufus.

EDCS-70900998.translation: To [their] most sweet parents who lived blamelessly towards all friends, Tryphera the daughter dedicated [this] to Elpidiphoros who lived 50 years, and to Nike who lived 50 years.
EDCS-70900998.summary: This is a dedication by Tryphera to her parents Elpidiphoros and Nike, who lived blamelessly and reached the age of 50.

EDCS-70900999.summary: This is a name, likely of a freedman or person associated with the Pompey family, inscribed on a monument.

EDCS-70901002.translation: The beginning and the end. Asyllis.
EDCS-70901002.summary: This inscription is likely a funerary marker, possibly for a person named Asyllis, with the phrase 'the beginning and the end' suggesting finality.

EDCS-70901004.translation: The people [honored] Antimachus, son of Philo, the benefactor.
EDCS-70901004.summary: This is a public dedication by the community honoring Antimachus, son of Philo, as a benefactor.

EDCS-70901007.translation: Eparate, beloved of Callius, be well.
EDCS-70901007.summary: This is a funerary epigram addressed to Eparate, commending her to Callius, and wishing her well.

EDCS-70901008.summary: This is a funerary greeting from Neicias to Artemidore.

EDCS-70901030.translation: Stranger, know this: the tomb of Vettius, son of Boos. Wasted by a harsh illness, his mother buried him. It seems Tyche was not judged. This stone is a witness to what earth covered.
EDCS-70901030.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Vettius, son of Boos, who died of illness, erected by his mother and attested by the stone.

EDCS-70901047.translation: M. Valerius Marcarion lived for 50 years, 8 months, [and some days].
EDCS-70901047.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of M. Valerius Marcarion as 50 years and 8 months.

EDCS-70901048.translation: Severus, good and blameless, lived for 35 years, 3 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-70901048.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Severus as 35 years, 3 months, and 15 days.

EDCS-70901053.summary: This inscription simply records the name and patronymic of Emmenidas, son of Sphodrus, likely on a funerary monument.

EDCS-70901064.translation: Lanthanoysa (and) Agathe, good and blameless, lived for 52 years, 4 months, and still 3 more days. Their daughter made [this] for her devoted mother. Whoever wronged her, has also wronged me; may he not escape God's notice.
EDCS-70901064.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lives of Lanthanoysa and Agathe, who were devoted to each other and honored by their daughter, with a warning against those who would dishonor them.

EDCS-70901065.translation: Amblis, [a] good and blameless [person], lived for 3 years.
EDCS-70901065.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the brief lifespan of a person named Amblis.

EDCS-71000052.translation: In the year 244 of the divine, immortal Emperor Caesar Antoninus Augustus Pius. And of the praetor Annianus [---]nicus for the year, chief priest for the year, polemarch for the year, agoranomos for the year [---]damus [---]. Blueprint of a pentangius, for the year of the agoranomos. A fixed deposit for the year of the metronomos. [---] of Annianus Antistius, devotee, of the bronze worker, of the Eumolpid, the auratus, the agorachus, Kommodianos, while Aurelius Egetianos was agoranomos.
EDCS-71000052.summary: This inscription records the administrative terms of various officials in a Greek-speaking Roman province, likely under Antoninus Pius, noting specific roles and potentially a building project or fund.

EDCS-71000242.translation: Sacred demons, I hand over Onesimus; lead him away, going under the earth.
EDCS-71000242.summary: This is a chthonic invocation or curse, likely related to a funerary context, where Onesimus is being handed over to the underworld deities.

EDCS-71000278.translation: To the love of Anikias. Fides, freedwoman. Earth Hermes, Persephone, accept [---].
EDCS-71000278.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription, likely to a freedwoman named Fides, invoking chthonic deities like Earth Hermes and Persephone.

EDCS-71000281.translation: Earth Hermes, chthonic gods, accept the servant of Benousta, daughter of Rufus.
EDCS-71000281.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription to chthonic deities, requesting them to accept the spirit of a servant named Benousta.

EDCS-71000708.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely listing names, possibly related to a funerary monument or a list of individuals.

EDCS-71000721.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely the name of an individual, possibly a tombstone or a dedication.

EDCS-71000722.summary: This is a simple greeting or epitaph addressed to a person named Ferusa.
