EDCS-22000885.translation: To the departed spirits of Publius Aelius Pius, who lived...
EDCS-22000885.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Publius Aelius Pius.

EDCS-22000886.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Anthus, freedman of Marcus, and Marcus Aemilius Clarus, freedman of Marcus.
EDCS-22000886.summary: This inscription lists two individuals, Marcus Aemilius Anthus and Marcus Aemilius Clarus, both freedmen of Marcus.

EDCS-22100004.translation: Aulus Pinnius Maximus, in accordance with the will of the most distinguished Pinnius, [dedicated this to] Aulus Agrilius Asprenas, son of Aulus.
EDCS-22100004.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a will, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22100005.translation: His sister Osius and Athnision made this for the well-deserving man.
EDCS-22100005.summary: This is a funerary monument made by siblings for a deceased relative, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100006.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Aurelius Ermeros made this for his patron, Marcus Aurelius Callinicus, the best of patrons, who deserved well.
EDCS-22100006.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a freedman to his patron, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100008.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to provide a definitive summary but appears to be a list of names, possibly related to ownership or a tomb.

EDCS-22100010.translation: While still alive, Flavia Valentina, Assyrius Charito, and their freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants purchased for themselves 11 feet in front and 13 feet in the field, with the stipulation that no one else may sell or give it away. Also 12 by 10 feet of the adjacent land belonging to the monument.
EDCS-22100010.summary: This is a funerary inscription that records the purchase and boundaries of a burial plot by a group of individuals, including freedpeople, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100011.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Fortis Caesar, a slave, made this for himself, for his deserving wife Charis, and for their descendants, and for Publius Aelius Felix, freedman of Augustus.
EDCS-22100011.summary: This is a funerary inscription by a slave for himself, his wife, and his patron, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100012.translation: His sisters made this for Fortunatus, their most devoted brother.
EDCS-22100012.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by sisters for their brother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100013.translation: For his well-deserving father, Fortunatus the household slave.
EDCS-22100013.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a household slave for his father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100014.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Iunius Mercurius [erected this] for his patroness Iunia Festiva, his most rare wife.
EDCS-22100014.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a freedman to his patroness and wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100015.translation: Lucius Mallius Philargyrus, freedman of a woman.
EDCS-22100015.summary: This is a funerary inscription identifying a freedman by his Roman name, his patron's status, and likely his original owner's gender, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100017.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of a person named Osia.

EDCS-22100018.translation: Philo, freedman of a certain woman, to his well-deserving daughter NEP[---].
EDCS-22100018.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by a freedman to his daughter, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22100020.translation: Marcus Scribonius Stymphalus, freedman of Marcus, and Caecilia Attica made this.
EDCS-22100020.summary: This inscription records the makers of a monument, likely a tomb, by a freedman and a woman named Caecilia Attica.

EDCS-22100021.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Secundinus, a most sweet infant foster-child, who lived 1 year, 10 months, and 17 days. Hermes and Primitiva made this.
EDCS-22100021.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Hermes and Primitiva to their foster-child Secundinus, who died in infancy.

EDCS-22100022.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Encolpus erected this for Publius Servilius Epaphroditus, who lived 26 years.
EDCS-22100022.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Publius Servilius Epaphroditus, with Encolpus as the dedicator.

EDCS-22100023.translation: To the Divine Manes. Suetia Tyndaris lived 61 years, 1 month, and 18 days.
EDCS-22100023.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Suetia Tyndaris.

EDCS-22100024.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Marcus Virginio Pompeius, son, well-deserving, who lived 25 years. His mother Pompeia Atticilla.
EDCS-22100024.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by a mother, Pompeia Atticilla, to her deceased son Marcus Virginio Pompeius.

EDCS-22100025.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely related to a dedication or memorial for Publius Honorius.

EDCS-22100030.translation: Here rests in peace Munatia Eulalia, a devout woman, who lived more or less 70 years, buried on the day before the Nones of February, [in the year of] Dydamius Sieidius, most illustrious man.
EDCS-22100030.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and burial of Munatia Eulalia, a devout woman, with a specific date and a reference to a prominent man. It is likely from late antiquity or the early Byzantine period.

EDCS-22100031.translation: Here rests in peace Petrus Alexandrinus, a linen merchant, who lived more or less 61 years, buried on the 11th day before the Kalends of February, in the year 20 of our Emperor lord Maurice Tiberius perpetual Augustus, after his consulship, in the 18th year of the indiction.
EDCS-22100031.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and burial of Petrus Alexandrinus, a linen merchant, with a precise date and regnal year within the reign of Emperor Maurice Tiberius, placing it in the late 6th or early 7th century AD.

EDCS-22100032.translation: Here will lie the girl Campana, who lived sixteen years and 21 days, on the 5th day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-22100032.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Campana, indicating her lifespan and burial date.

EDCS-22100033.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name on a tombstone or a personal dedication.

EDCS-22100034.translation: Here is placed Maurica, who lived more or less seven months.
EDCS-22100034.summary: This funerary inscription records the placement of Maurica and her short lifespan.

EDCS-22100035.translation: To Petronius, laid to rest in peace.
EDCS-22100035.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a burial spot to Petronius.

EDCS-22100036.translation: Here rests in peace Thonbr, who lived more or less VI years, laid to rest.
EDCS-22100036.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Thonbr, who died at a young age.

EDCS-22100037.translation: To Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, wife of Antoninus Augustus, by decree of the town council of Soluntini.
EDCS-22100037.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the town of Soluntini to Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, possibly a patron or benefactress.

EDCS-22100044.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the Vth time, imperator for the VIIIth time, consul for the IInd time, father of the fatherland, son of the Emperor Caesar divus Marcus Antoninus Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of divus Commodus, nephew of divus Antoninus Pius, great-nephew of divus Hadrianus, great-great-nephew of divus Traianus Parthicus, most indulgent and most clement prince, by Maesia Fabia Titiana, a most distinguished lady, and Maesius Fabius Titianus, a most distinguished boy.
EDCS-22100044.summary: This extensive inscription honors Emperor Septimius Severus and his family, likely set up by Maesia Fabia Titiana and her son, and dates to his Vth tribunician power, VIIIth acclamation as emperor, and II consulship.

EDCS-22100045.translation: To Lucius Acilius Rufus, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Quirina, quaestor pro praetore of the province of Sicily, tribune of the plebs, praetor, prefect for the distribution of grain by decree of the Senate, by the people of Hispellum, publicly by decree of the town councillors, as patron.
EDCS-22100045.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Acilius Rufus, listing his career offices and designating him as patron by the people of Hispellum.

EDCS-22100046.translation: The council and the people of the most splendid colony of the Augustan Himeraei and Thermitae erected this at their own expense to Gaius Maesius Aquillius Fabius Titianus, a most distinguished man, an excellent consul, a deserving citizen and patron.
EDCS-22100046.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the people of Himera and Thermitae to Gaius Maesius Aquillius Fabius Titianus, honoring him as a distinguished former consul and patron.

EDCS-22100047.translation: To Titianus, a most distinguished man, son of Caius Maesius Titianus and Fonteia Frontina of consular rank, a patrician, on the occasion of his attainment of the toga virilis, Clodius Rufus, a Roman knight, to his incomparable friend.
EDCS-22100047.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Clodius Rufus to his friend Titianus, celebrating his attainment of the toga virilis (coming of age) and his patrician status.

EDCS-22100048.translation: To Secundus, vilicus of the twentieth imperial inheritance, and to Urbana, his mother; from Clymene, relative. Secundus, slave of the twentieth imperial inheritance.
EDCS-22100048.summary: This inscription honors Secundus, a vilicus of an imperial inheritance, and his mother, with a dedication from a relative, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100049.translation: To Marcus Cestius, son of Publius, of the Claudia tribe, former centurion, former commander of the engineers, former military tribune, former duumvir, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-22100049.summary: This is a dedication honoring Marcus Cestius, a man who held numerous military and civic positions, likely erected by decree of the local council in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100050.translation: To Gnaeus Pollienus, son of Gnaeus, military tribune of the 12th Legion.
EDCS-22100050.summary: This inscription records the name and military rank of Gnaeus Pollienus, a military tribune in the 12th Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100053.translation: To Antia, daughter of Marcus Cleopatra, priestess, by the will of the people and decree of the decurions, at public expense; at its dedication, for each plebeian son of a decurion, two denarii were given, and for each decurion, five denarii.
EDCS-22100053.summary: This inscription details the dedication of a monument to Antia, a priestess, funded by public money and accompanied by monetary distributions to decurions and their sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100056.translation: To Gaius Virius Maximus, son of Gaius, of the Claudia tribe, haruspex, and to Donata, freedwoman of Marcus Clodius, for their deserving services.
EDCS-22100056.summary: This dedication honors Gaius Virius Maximus, a haruspex (diviner), and Donata, a freedwoman, for their meritorious service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100064.translation: To Publius, freedman of Publius, called Psorion, contractor.
EDCS-22100064.summary: This inscription records the name and occupation (contractor) of Publius, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100065.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Species, an equestrian, erected this for Callistus Thraecus, his fellow-slave, a victor, well-deserving.
EDCS-22100065.summary: This funerary inscription honors Callistus Thraecus, a victorious former slave and fellow slave of the dedicator Species, an equestrian, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100066.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the name and age of Musice of Vibona, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100067.translation: To Popillius, son of Publius, from Pollentia, of the Pollia tribe.
EDCS-22100067.summary: This inscription records the name, tribal affiliation, and origin of Popillius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100069.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Acellius Eyporistus lived 70 years.
EDCS-22100069.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and age of Publius Acellius Eyporistus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22100070.translation: To Lucius Acilius Adventus, my devoted husband.
EDCS-22100070.summary: This is a funerary dedication from an anonymous wife to her husband, Lucius Acilius Adventus.

EDCS-22100071.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Acilia Crispina, daughter of Marcus, lived 2 years, 7 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-22100071.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Acilia Crispina, recording her parentage and lifespan.

EDCS-22100072.translation: Acilia Vitalis lived 36 years. Soter, to his devoted wife.
EDCS-22100072.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Acilia Vitalis and is dedicated by her husband, Soter.

EDCS-22100073.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelia Alce, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-22100074.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Gaius Aemilius Bacchus lived 40 years.
EDCS-22100074.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Aemilius Bacchus, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-22100075.translation: To Marcus Albius Asiaticus, son of Marcus, lived 14 years. Philocalus, father, Artemisia, mother.
EDCS-22100075.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Marcus Albius Asiaticus and names his parents.

EDCS-22100078.translation: To Marcus Arruntius Brocchus, a place was publicly granted by decree of the decurions, in front [measure?] feet, in area 20 feet.
EDCS-22100078.summary: This inscription records a public grant of land for Marcus Arruntius Brocchus, as decided by local officials.

EDCS-22100079.translation: Athen lived 20 years. To Vergilia, his devoted mother.
EDCS-22100079.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Athen and is dedicated to his mother, Vergilia.

EDCS-22100080.translation: To Caius Audi Verus, space for lying down, in area 22 feet, in front 22 feet.
EDCS-22100080.summary: This inscription records the dimensions of a burial plot allocated to Caius Audi Verus.

EDCS-22100081.summary: This is a dedication, likely funerary, to Lucius Aurelius Niger.

EDCS-22100082.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Calemera lived 1 year and 4 months.
EDCS-22100082.summary: A funerary dedication for Calemera, who died in infancy.

EDCS-22100084.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Cestius.
EDCS-22100084.summary: A funerary dedication for Publius Cestius.

EDCS-22100089.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Chresimus lived 20 years.
EDCS-22100089.summary: A funerary dedication for Chresimus, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-22100090.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Cispius.
EDCS-22100090.summary: A funerary dedication for Titus Cispius.

EDCS-22100091.translation: Eros, freedman of Titus Cispius, son of Titus.
EDCS-22100091.summary: A funerary dedication for Eros, freedman of Titus Cispius.

EDCS-22100093.summary: A funerary dedication for Clodia Thalia, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-22100094.translation: For Tertia, freedwoman of Caius Clovatius, and for her daughter Optata, freedwoman of Caius Clovatius, and for herself.
EDCS-22100094.summary: A funerary dedication by Tertia, freedwoman of Caius Clovatius, for herself and her daughter Optata.

EDCS-22100095.translation: For the pious Coponia Chimenna, wife of Titus Cocceius. She lived 38 years.
EDCS-22100095.summary: A funerary dedication for Coponia Chimenna, wife of Titus Cocceius, who died at the age of 38.

EDCS-22100097.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cornelius Epaphruitus lived 8 years.
EDCS-22100097.summary: A funerary dedication for Cornelius Epaphruitus, who died in childhood.

EDCS-22100098.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cosmia lived 20 years.
EDCS-22100098.summary: A funerary dedication for Cosmia, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-22100099.translation: Domitius Himeraeus, son of Aulus, of the Quirina tribe, for himself, his sister Domitia, daughter of Aulus, and his wife Zotica, freedwoman of Alfia.
EDCS-22100099.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Domitius Himeraeus for himself, his sister, and his wife.

EDCS-22100100.translation: Daphne lived 23 years; [dedicated by] Caninius Agon.
EDCS-22100100.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of a woman named Daphne and mentioning Caninius Agon.

EDCS-22100104.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Eunus lived 16 years.
EDCS-22100104.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of Eunus.

EDCS-22100105.translation: Quintus Fabius Isio, freedman of Quintus, [dedicated this] to Coprilla, freedwoman of Decimus, wife of Laelia.
EDCS-22100105.summary: A funerary dedication by Quintus Fabius Isio to his wife Coprilla.

EDCS-22100106.translation: Aulus Fabricius Primus, son of Aulus, [dedicated this] to Postumia Fausta Fabricius.
EDCS-22100106.summary: A funerary dedication by Aulus Fabricius Primus, likely to a relative named Postumia Fausta Fabricius.

EDCS-22100107.translation: Attio Fausto, Optatus [dedicated this] for himself and his family.
EDCS-22100107.summary: A funerary dedication by Optatus for himself and his family, possibly including Attius Faustus.

EDCS-22100108.translation: To his most pious daughter Felenis, [dedicated by] L. II.
EDCS-22100108.summary: A funerary dedication to a daughter named Felenis.

EDCS-22100109.translation: To Marcus Granio Dextrus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, who lived 50 years; Cestia Pulchella made [this] for herself and her husband.
EDCS-22100109.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Granio Dextrus, and a dedication by Cestia Pulchella for herself and her husband.

EDCS-22100110.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph recording the name Halaesina and her age.

EDCS-22100111.translation: Caius Haterius Agatocles, freedman of Caius.
EDCS-22100111.summary: A funerary epitaph or dedication by Caius Haterius Agatocles, freedman of Caius.

EDCS-22100112.translation: Lucius Herennius Certus lived 13 years. Farewell.
EDCS-22100112.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the short life of Lucius Herennius Certus.

EDCS-22100113.translation: Herennia Polla, daughter of Caius. Farewell.
EDCS-22100113.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying Herennia Polla and her father Caius.

EDCS-22100114.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the short life of Marcus Hortesius Primus.

EDCS-22100115.translation: Ianuarius, the slave, lived 18 years. Successa to her devoted son.
EDCS-22100115.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ianuarius, a slave, likely dedicated by his mother Successa.

EDCS-22100116.translation: Lucius Iulius Attilianus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 19 years, 7 months, and 14 days.
EDCS-22100116.summary: This is a funerary epitaph providing the full lifespan and tribal affiliation of Lucius Iulius Attilianus.

EDCS-22100117.translation: To the departed spirits. Caius Laesanius Auxetus lived 50 years.
EDCS-22100117.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed spirits, recording the lifespan of Caius Laesanius Auxetus.

EDCS-22100118.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a name and possibly a cognomen or title, of Cnaeus Licinius.

EDCS-22100119.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Lollius Myrtilus lived 50 years.
EDCS-22100119.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed spirits, recording the lifespan of Marcus Lollius Myrtilus.

EDCS-22100120.translation: Lucia, freedwoman of a woman, is buried here.
EDCS-22100120.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying Lucia as a freedwoman.

EDCS-22100121.translation: To the departed spirits. For Marcia II, pious, lived 20 years. Lucius Mammius to his most chaste wife.
EDCS-22100121.summary: This is a funerary epitaph likely commemorating Marcia II, with a dedication from her husband Lucius Mammius.

EDCS-22100122.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marga lived 4 years, 11 months, 7 days.
EDCS-22100122.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of a person named Marga.

EDCS-22100123.translation: To Ius Maximus, son of Brogus.
EDCS-22100123.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, possibly incomplete, honouring Ius Maximus, son of Brogus.

EDCS-22100126.translation: To Mevius Froentius, a field of 16 by 40 feet.
EDCS-22100126.summary: This inscription seems to record the dimensions of a plot of land, possibly for funerary or agricultural use, associated with Mevius Froentius.

EDCS-22100128.translation: To Nicarius, daughter of Munatia, freedwoman of Lucius, Zosima. Hymn singer, priestess. I implore you, Nicarius, whoever you may be lying here, may the earth be light upon you, laid to rest in a tender body. She lived 18 years.
EDCS-22100128.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Zosima, freedwoman of Lucius and Nicarius, who was a hymn singer and priestess, detailing her age at death.

EDCS-22100129.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gnaeus Octavius Martialis lived 80 years.
EDCS-22100129.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Gnaeus Octavius Martialis.

EDCS-22100130.translation: Pacilia Techne lived 2 years, 2 months, 27 days. Veteranus and Apolauste, her parents.
EDCS-22100130.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Pacilia Techne, recording her age at death and identifying her parents.

EDCS-22100133.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Primi, who lived 18 years.
EDCS-22100133.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Primi.

EDCS-22100134.translation: Publicia Agathia, hail! Publicius Brutanus made this at his own expense.
EDCS-22100134.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, possibly a tombstone, erected by Publicius Brutanus for Publicia Agathia.

EDCS-22100135.translation: Sextus Raecius, freedman of Sextus, Stephano, from his friends at their own expense.
EDCS-22100135.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely a tombstone, funded by friends for Sextus Raecius Stephano.

EDCS-22100136.summary: This is an incomplete funerary epitaph mentioning a person named Rufinus and their lifespan.

EDCS-22100137.summary: This inscription is a nomenation of Gaius Sabidius Artemo, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-22100139.translation: To Primio, freedman of Sempronius's wife, aged 14.
EDCS-22100139.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Primio, a freedman, specifying his age.

EDCS-22100140.translation: To Publius Servilius Capito, son of Publius, who lived 14 years.
EDCS-22100140.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and age of Publius Servilius Capito.

EDCS-22100141.translation: Telesphorus, son of Caius, and his parents, and his nurse made this monument for [---]iae, daughter of Caius, deserving of their affection; it will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-22100141.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by parents and a nurse for a woman named [---]ia, specifying that the property will not pass to an heir.

EDCS-22100142.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Tennius Herma lived 75 years.
EDCS-22100142.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicating the monument to the Divine Manes for Lucius Tennius Herma, stating his age at death.

EDCS-22100143.translation: Vibia Ammia, freedwoman of Marcus, wife of Gnaeus Terentius Rufus, made this at her own expense for Dionysius and for Gnaeus Terentius Rufus, son of Spurius.
EDCS-22100143.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument funded by Vibia Ammia for Dionysius and Gnaeus Terentius Rufus.

EDCS-22100144.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph stating the name and age of the deceased, Tertia.

EDCS-22100145.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Vecilius Valerianus lived 13 years. Lucius Vecilius Vecilianus lived 8 years.
EDCS-22100145.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to the Divine Manes and records the ages of two individuals, Lucius Vecilius Valerianus and Lucius Vecilius Vecilianus.

EDCS-22100146.translation: Lucia Vecilia, daughter of Lucius, lived 30 years.
EDCS-22100146.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name, parentage, and age at death of Lucia Vecilia.

EDCS-22100147.translation: To the Divine Manes. Veronica lived 3 years and 3 months. Damatria, her most dutiful mother, [made this].
EDCS-22100147.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to the Divine Manes and records the age of a young child, Veronica, and identifies her mother as the dedicator.

EDCS-22100148.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Decimus Vibius.

EDCS-22100149.translation: Lucius Volumnius Parsianus lived for 13 years and 4 months.
EDCS-22100149.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Lucius Volumnius Parsianus.

EDCS-22100150.translation: To Marcus Voluscius Felix, son of Marcus.
EDCS-22100150.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely funerary, to Marcus Voluscius Felix.

EDCS-22100575.translation: Oppius Valens Augustalis [made this] for himself and for Indie Optata, and for Panio Atimetus.
EDCS-22100575.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication made by Oppius Valens Augustalis for himself and others, likely family members.

EDCS-22100576.translation: Copy of a letter: 'Farewell, my lord father. Farewell, my lord father. When I was dictating these things to you, I considered you most unfortunate that you were sending this to me. I beg you to make a monument for me worthy of my youth. I beg you to free my freedman Eutychianus, my foster-child, and release him with the vindicta. Likewise my slave Aprilis, who alone remains from my service. Written on the 15th day before the Kalends of April, when Lucius Calpurnius Piso and Publius Salvius Julianus were consuls.'
EDCS-22100576.summary: This inscription records a letter from a dying person to their father requesting a monument, the manumission of a freedman, and the liberation of a slave, dated to 169 AD.

EDCS-22100577.translation: The municipality [dedicated this] to the Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine [father], father of the country.
EDCS-22100577.summary: This is a dedication by a municipality to Emperor Augustus, likely set up during his reign or shortly thereafter.

EDCS-22100578.translation: Italici [dedicated this] to Lucius Cornelius Scipio, for honor.
EDCS-22100578.summary: This inscription is a dedication honoring Lucius Cornelius Scipio, likely by an individual or group named Italici.

EDCS-22100579.translation: Marcus Limbricius Rufus, son of Marcus from Falerna, [erected this] while alive for himself and for his wife Helvia Arura.
EDCS-22100579.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Marcus Limbricius Rufus for himself and his wife during their lifetime.

EDCS-22100580.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Titienus lived for 36 years. Aurelia Titia made this for her well-deserving brother.
EDCS-22100580.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Aurelia Titia for her brother Lucius Titienus.

EDCS-22100581.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Volumnius Eratus lived for 37 years and 7 months. Caius Ancitus erected this memorial for his friend.
EDCS-22100581.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Caius Ancitus for his friend Marcus Volumnius Eratus.

EDCS-22100582.translation: To Augustus, son of the deified one, chief priest, the municipality.
EDCS-22100582.summary: This is a dedication by a municipality to Emperor Augustus, likely dating to his reign (27 BCE - 14 CE).

EDCS-22100583.translation: To Livia, goddess, wife of Augustus, the municipality.
EDCS-22100583.summary: This is a dedication by a municipality to Livia, wife of Augustus and later deified, likely dating to the early imperial period (late 1st century BCE - early 1st century CE).

EDCS-22100587.translation: Licinia Mecilla, to her husband Quintus Manlius Viro, to their son Quintus Manlius Fronton, and to their daughter Manlia.
EDCS-22100587.summary: This is a familial dedication, likely a funerary monument, for members of the Manlia family, probably dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-22100588.translation: Quintus, son of Publius, for his freedman Titus, aged 11.
EDCS-22100588.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a freedman named Titus, set up by his patron Quintus, dating to the early imperial period based on naming conventions.

EDCS-22100589.translation: Decimus Sempronius, son of Decimus, to his freedman Optatus, son of Decimus Iunius, and to Sempronia, daughter of Decimus.
EDCS-22100589.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Decimus Sempronius to his freedman Optatus and his daughter Sempronia, likely a funerary monument from the early imperial period.

EDCS-22100611.summary: This inscription appears to be the name of an individual, likely a tombstone inscription for Valeria Thryphosa.

EDCS-22100612.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Narcissus lived for 3 years and 28 days. Thallus and Pannychis made this for their dearest son. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-22100612.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Narcissus, set up by his parents Thallus and Pannychis, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100621.translation: To Iulia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp, wife of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus. The municipium of the Gaulitanans dedicated this with public money, with [name illegible] overseeing.
EDCS-22100621.summary: A dedication to Iulia Domna, wife of Septimius Severus, by the citizens of the municipium of Gaul, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100622.translation: [---]ti / [---]S[---] / For Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, brother of [---] Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of [---]. The ordo decurionum dedicated this, with [name illegible]imo overseeing.
EDCS-22100622.summary: A dedication by the local senatorial order (ordo decurionum) to Emperor Caracalla and his father Septimius Severus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100625.translation: To Lucius Cestius Lucius son Gallus Varenianus Lutatius Natalis Aemilianus, patron of the municipium, Titus Marcius Marcianus, for his most excellent and dearest friend, by his own money, as an honor.
EDCS-22100625.summary: An honorary dedication by Titus Marcius Marcianus to his friend Lucius Cestius Gallus Varenianus Lutatius Natalis Aemilianus, a patron of the municipality, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22100630.translation: Quintus Lytatius Longinus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, made this for himself and his most holy wife Iunia Vera, daughter of Caius, while he was alive.
EDCS-22100630.summary: A funerary dedication made by Quintus Lytatius Longinus for himself and his wife Iunia Vera, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-22200384.summary: A fragmented inscription that names an individual, Virius, who held a position of respect, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-22200385.translation: The curators [---] Quintus Maculnius [---] Titus Trutedius [---] Aulus Turius son of Aulus Ochac[---]
EDCS-22200385.summary: A fragmented inscription listing names of individuals, possibly officials or members of a board of curators, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-22200392.translation: Draucus made this for his most dutiful mother, Pia.
EDCS-22200392.summary: A funerary epitaph by Draucus for his mother Pia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-22200393.summary: This is a brief funerary epigram for a person named Dulcitius.

EDCS-22200396.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Caia Iulia Prisca.

EDCS-22900529.summary: This inscription likely marks a piece of pottery or a tool originating from the workshop of Ionius.

EDCS-22900573.summary: This is an ownership or provenance inscription, indicating something belongs to Caius Iunius Draconis.

EDCS-22900574.summary: This appears to be a name inscription, possibly for an owner or the subject of a monument.

EDCS-22900588.summary: This is a name inscription, likely identifying an individual.

EDCS-22901677.summary: This inscription indicates ownership or provenance belonging to Caius Lucius Philippus.

EDCS-22901680.summary: This is a name inscription, possibly identifying an individual or owner.

EDCS-22901683.translation: Of Numerius Alfius, son of Numerius.
EDCS-22901683.summary: This is a filiation inscription, identifying Numerius Alfius by his father Numerius.

EDCS-22901693.summary: This inscription indicates ownership or provenance belonging to Caius Iunius Draconis.

EDCS-22901694.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedicant associated with a monument belonging to Caius Iunius Draconis.

EDCS-22901695.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedicant associated with a monument belonging to Caius Iunius Draconis.

EDCS-22901704.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedicant associated with a monument belonging to Caius Heius Ianuaris.

EDCS-22901714.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Caecilius Saecularis, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-22901723.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedicant associated with a monument belonging to Marcus T. Luconis.

EDCS-22901733.summary: This inscription records the name of Marcus Aemilius Corion, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-22901735.summary: This inscription records the name of Marcus Furius Princeps, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-22901738.summary: This inscription records the name of Marcus Furius Princeps, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-22901748.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedicant associated with a monument belonging to Fabricius Masculi.

EDCS-22901750.summary: This inscription, with an unclear Greek element 'ISAIAONH', likely records the ownership or dedicant associated with a monument belonging to Caius Cornelius Ursus.

EDCS-22901760.summary: This inscription is a genitive naming of Quintus Oppius Justus, likely part of a larger dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-22901766.summary: This inscription is a nominative naming of Gaius Satrius Phoebus, possibly the subject of a lost dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-23400463.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a sarcophagus by Valeria Marcia for her daughter Marciana Iusta, providing detailed lifespan information and indicating a close familial bond.

EDCS-24700044.translation: Teupilus watched for Lucius Gellius and Gnaeus Lentulus six days before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-24700044.summary: This inscription records an event, possibly a performance or viewing, involving Teupilus and the notable figures Lucius Gellius and Gnaeus Lentulus, dated to July 27th.

EDCS-34500056.summary: This inscription records doliare ware from the estates of Faustina Augusta Nostra, supplied by Calvus Crescens.

EDCS-34700073.translation: Buro Titus Flavius [decided this] for himself.
EDCS-34700073.summary: This inscription records a personal monument or dedication for himself by Buro Titus Flavius.

EDCS-34700074.translation: [belonging to] Maximus and Silvanus and their [family/possessions].
EDCS-34700074.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or dedication belonging to Maximus, Silvanus, and their relatives or property.

EDCS-34700076.translation: Caius Servilius Avitus, son of Caius, of the voting tribe Quirina. Be well. He lived 17 years and 11 days.
EDCS-34700076.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Servilius Avitus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-34700318.translation: Roof tile from the brickworks of Julia Procula for river trade.
EDCS-34700318.summary: This inscription on a roof tile indicates it originated from the brickworks of Julia Procula and was intended for use in river trade.

EDCS-34700334.translation: Roof tile from the brickworks of Julia Procula for river trade.
EDCS-34700334.summary: This inscription on a roof tile indicates it originated from the brickworks of Julia Procula and was intended for use in river trade.

EDCS-35400848.translation: Primigenius, a slave of the two Domitii, made this.
EDCS-35400848.summary: This inscription records that Primigenius, a slave belonging to the two Domitii, created the object.

EDCS-36100343.summary: This is a possessive inscription, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Gnaeus Domitius Clemens.

EDCS-38000201.translation: Here lies Leontia, 3 years old. Here lies Calliope, 18 years old.
EDCS-38000201.summary: This is a bilingual funerary inscription recording the names and ages of two individuals, Leontia and Calliope.

EDCS-38000203.translation: Eirenes the elder bought a place, without harming the order.
EDCS-38000203.summary: This inscription records the purchase of a plot by Eirenes the elder, with a stipulation that the existing order should not be harmed.

EDCS-38000204.translation: Iason the elder bought this grave space for himself and his children, without damaging the order.
EDCS-38000204.summary: This inscription states that Iason the elder purchased a grave for himself and his children, ensuring no damage to the existing order.

EDCS-38000213.translation: So that the bema may be sacred, Zacharias has constructed this with well-fitted marble.
EDCS-38000213.summary: This inscription by Zacharias describes the construction of a sacred bema using well-fitted marble.

EDCS-38000214.translation: Eirene the nymph lies here. Therefore, concerning this mystery, let no one dig here.
EDCS-38000214.summary: This funerary inscription states that Eirene the nymph is buried here and warns against disturbing the site, possibly related to a mystery cult.

EDCS-38000215.translation: Let no one open here concerning the future; for Nophios and Nyphē lie here, a blessing to the holy ones here.
EDCS-38000215.summary: This inscription forbids opening a burial site belonging to Nophios and Nyphē, describing their presence as a blessing to the holy.

EDCS-38000218.summary: This is a funerary epigram for an infant named Iason.

EDCS-38000220.summary: This inscription names Attis, identified as an elder.

EDCS-38000221.summary: This inscription names Judas, son of Sabatias.

EDCS-38000224.translation: Hail, Gaius Silius Ptolemaeus from Samaria!
EDCS-38000224.summary: This is a greeting to Gaius Silius Ptolemaeus, a native of Samaria.

EDCS-38000226.translation: Gerousiarch Philentolos and Eulogia, his wife, the elder.
EDCS-38000226.summary: This inscription names Philentolos, a gerousiarch, and his wife Eulogia, who is identified as an elder.

EDCS-38000229.translation: The place of Dionysia and Eirene.
EDCS-38000229.summary: This inscription marks a place belonging to Dionysia and Eirene.

EDCS-38200002.translation: To the Chthonic Gods, Clodia Romana lived 35 years.
EDCS-38200002.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Clodia Romana at the age of 35, dedicated to the Chthonic Gods.

EDCS-38200003.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Clodia Severa at the age of 16.

EDCS-38200004.translation: To the Chthonic Gods, Abiania Sabina lived 55 years.
EDCS-38200004.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Abiania Sabina at the age of 55, dedicated to the Chthonic Gods.

EDCS-38200006.translation: Aurelia Agoraste, an incomparable soul, Hilaritas lived 100 years.
EDCS-38200006.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Hilaritas (with Aurelia Agoraste described as an incomparable soul) at the age of 100.

EDCS-38200009.translation: Marcus Antonius Scorpus of Corinth lies here, aged 25. Greetings, passers-by.
EDCS-38200009.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Antonius Scorpus, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-38200010.summary: This is a tombstone or epitaph for Julia Marcia.

EDCS-38200012.translation: Cornelius Magnus of Apamea, for his foster-son Epaphroditus, in memory.
EDCS-38200012.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Cornelius Magnus for his foster-son Epaphroditus.

EDCS-38200014.translation: Sellius Practicus and Licinia Cleta made this for themselves while alive.
EDCS-38200014.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned by Sellius Practicus and Licinia Cleta for their own burials.

EDCS-38200015.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Clodia Romantilla lived 4 years, 8 months, and 14 days. Claudius Theseus, father, for his sweetest child.
EDCS-38200015.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to the Chthonic Gods for Clodia Romantilla by her father, Claudius Theseus.

EDCS-38200016.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Clodius Romanus lived 1 year, 7 months, and 28 days. Claudius Theseus, father, for his sweetest child.
EDCS-38200016.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to the Chthonic Gods for Clodius Romanus by his father, Claudius Theseus.

EDCS-38200020.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Androbios Lycius, shipowner, lived an unimpeded life for 36 years. Apollonius, with Musaeus and his brother Theodorus, for his own memory.
EDCS-38200020.summary: This is a funerary monument for Androbios Lycius, a shipowner, erected by his brothers and Apollonius.

EDCS-38200021.translation: Julius Diadumenus, freedman of Julius Quadratus.
EDCS-38200021.summary: This inscription records Julius Diadumenus, who was the freedman of Julius Quadratus.

EDCS-38200022.translation: For Felicita, daughter of Hermodorus, aged six.
EDCS-38200022.summary: This is a tombstone for Felicita, daughter of Hermodorus, who died at the age of six.

EDCS-38200023.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Alexion of Sebaste, son of Julius, lived 40 years.
EDCS-38200023.summary: This funerary monument is for Alexion, son of Julius, who lived 40 years and is dedicated to the Chthonic Gods.

EDCS-38200024.translation: I, Ulpius Nicephorus of Antioch, merchant of Tyche, a goddess of Coele Syria near Daphne, lie here. I thought the gods were mortal, but here am I. Be brave, no one is immortal. I, Valens, erected this in memory. Be brave, Nicephorus, no one is immortal.
EDCS-38200024.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from Roman Syria, records the self-commemoration of a merchant named Ulpius Nicephorus, who also mentions a person named Valens.

EDCS-38200026.summary: A short epitaph recording the name, piety, and age of Julius Justus.

EDCS-38200027.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Aisaris, son of Sattaras, lived 52 years.
EDCS-38200027.summary: An epitaph dedicated to the Chthonic Gods, recording the lifespan of Aisaris, son of Sattaras.

EDCS-38200028.translation: Euskis lived 28 years. Astomachis lived 12 years.
EDCS-38200028.summary: A simple epitaph recording the lifespans of two individuals, Euskis and Astomachis.

EDCS-38200032.translation: Of the Mamertini. Of the Mamertini.
EDCS-38200032.summary: A very brief inscription possibly indicating ownership or belonging to the Mamertini gens or a related group.

EDCS-38301851.translation: Pottery work of Cosmus, from the estate of Quintus Asinius Marcellus.
EDCS-38301851.summary: This inscription indicates pottery produced by Cosmus, from the property of Quintus Asinius Marcellus.

EDCS-38301852.translation: From the potteries of Quintus Asinius Marcellus, pottery work of Caius Nunnidius Fortunatus, when Servianus and Varus were consuls (likely 267 AD).
EDCS-38301852.summary: This inscription from a pottery indicates work by Caius Nunnidius Fortunatus from the estates of Quintus Asinius Marcellus, dated by consuls to 267 AD.

EDCS-39101513.summary: A simple owner's mark or name inscription, identifying Aeschylus, son of Chryson.

EDCS-39101516.summary: A simple owner's mark or name inscription, identifying Callistratus, son of Rator.

EDCS-39101518.translation: Celadianus, Phiseinos, Paulinus // Lalos, Rufus, Eusebes; they rejoiced.
EDCS-39101518.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly a group of friends or associates, with a concluding sentiment of joy.

EDCS-39101519.summary: This inscription records the lifespan of Herodoros, a deceased individual.

EDCS-39101520.translation: In the time of Herakleios son of Aristonicus, the high priest of Zeus Orios, Aristarchos son of Nympon and Kabamos, from his own resources.
EDCS-39101520.summary: This is a dedication to Zeus Orios, made by Aristarchos son of Nympon and Kabamos, during the priesthood of Herakleios son of Aristonicus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-39101521.translation: To Apollo. Herakleios son of Aristophylos, having served as a decurio, from his own resources.
EDCS-39101521.summary: This is a dedication to Apollo by Herakleios son of Aristophylos, who served as a decurio, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-39101522.translation: A younger [dedication]: Archytas son of Nestodoros from Santra dedicated the temple and the statue from his own resources.
EDCS-39101522.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple and statue by Archytas son of Nestodoros from Santra, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-39101524.translation: Glykera, worthy one, greetings. You lived 53 years.
EDCS-39101524.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Glykera, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-39101525.translation: Damala, worthy one, greetings. You lived 60 years.
EDCS-39101525.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Damala, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-39101526.translation: Herman, worthy one, greetings. You lived 28 years.
EDCS-39101526.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Herman, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-39101527.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Euphrainon, greetings, worthy one. You lived 7 years and 3 months.
EDCS-39101527.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Euphrainon, dedicated to the Chthonic Gods and recording his lifespan.

EDCS-39101528.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Cornelia, worthy one, greetings. You lived 22 years.
EDCS-39101528.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Cornelia, dedicated to the Chthonic Gods and recording her lifespan.

EDCS-39101529.translation: Vetia Cornelia, most pious. She lived 50 years.
EDCS-39101529.summary: This inscription records the lifespan of Vetia Cornelia, described as most pious.

EDCS-39101535.summary: This inscription identifies a person named Diodoros, son of Apollonios.

EDCS-39101539.translation: Lucifera died belonging to the lady Agathe.
EDCS-39101539.summary: This inscription records the death of a slave named Lucifera, belonging to Agathe.

EDCS-39101540.summary: This inscription likely identifies the speaker as belonging to the Geloni, a Scythian people.

EDCS-39101541.summary: This inscription identifies the speaker as Longenaeus, possibly a public official or of public standing.

EDCS-39101543.translation: Argyles, this gift for the Pedioi.
EDCS-39101543.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Argyles for the Pedioi.

EDCS-39101545.translation: [...] Gaius Pien, a slave, I have purchased, Rannius [or Rionius?].
EDCS-39101545.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording a transaction involving a slave named Gaius Pien and a purchaser named Rannius/Rionius.

EDCS-39101546.translation: Anastasia, wife of Quintus, lies here, having lived in this life and departed from this life before the Kalends of August, having lived for 40 years.
EDCS-39101546.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Anastasia, wife of Quintus, who died before the Kalends of August and lived for 40 years.

EDCS-39101547.translation: Eternal praise to Baal Shamash, servant of Abd-'Eser and Yahya, son of 'Eser, son of 'Abd-'Eser, and Kasmou, son of 'Eser, son of 'Abd-'Eser. Dionysios and Sarapion, sons of Sarapion of Tyre, to Herakles the leader. Eternal praise to Baal Shamash, servant of Abd-'Eser and Yahya, son of 'Eser, son of 'Abd-'Eser, and Kasmou, son of 'Eser, son of 'Abd-'Eser. Dionysios and Sarapion, sons of Sarapion of Tyre, to Herakles the leader.
EDCS-39101547.summary: This is a bilingual inscription, featuring a Semitic dedication to Baal Shamash and a Greek dedication to Herakles by Dionysios and Sarapion, sons of Sarapion of Tyre, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-39101548.translation: Lucius Castricius Kyrreina Proudenus, equestrian of the Romans, first of the Melitaites and patron, having ruled and served the god Augustus.
EDCS-39101548.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Augustus by Lucius Castricius Kyrreina Proudenus, an equestrian official who held local leadership positions in Melite.

EDCS-39101550.translation: A cemetery purchased from Zosimetinus and Aniketos.
EDCS-39101550.summary: This inscription records the purchase of a cemetery by Zosimetinus and Aniketos.

EDCS-39101551.translation: Here lies Domestikos, the well-taught Christian and physician. He lived 68 years. He passed away on the 15th day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-39101551.summary: A funerary inscription for Domestikos, a Christian physician, detailing his life span and death date.

EDCS-39101553.translation: The people (honour) Biotos, son of Timandros, for his goodwill.
EDCS-39101553.summary: A dedication by the people honouring Biotos for his goodwill.

EDCS-39101554.translation: The municipality of the Alontini (honour) Gnaeus Pollienus Eumareus, son (of X), descendant of benefactors, for goodwill.
EDCS-39101554.summary: A municipal honour bestowed upon Gnaeus Pollienus Eumareus by the Alontini for his goodwill and ancestral benefactions.

EDCS-39101555.translation: To the gods, Aristocleia, daughter of Phil[...]
EDCS-39101555.summary: A partial dedication to the gods by Aristocleia.

EDCS-39101556.translation: The oil-painters (honour) Agatharchos, son of Herakleios.
EDCS-39101556.summary: A dedication by oil-painters honouring Agatharchos, son of Herakleios.

EDCS-39101557.translation: The oil-painters (honour) Orthos, son of Formion.
EDCS-39101557.summary: A dedication by oil-painters honouring Orthos, son of Formion.

EDCS-39101558.translation: The oil-painters (honour) Phinton, son of Eumachos.
EDCS-39101558.summary: A dedication by oil-painters honouring Phinton, son of Eumachos.

EDCS-39101559.summary: A partial inscription beginning with the name Artemidoros and his patronymic.

EDCS-39101562.translation: Prōtos and Menippe (dedicate this) to Artemis Eupraxia.
EDCS-39101562.summary: A dedication by Prōtos and Menippe to the goddess Artemis, identified with Eupraxia.

EDCS-39101565.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely recording the name of Junius Philēmon.

EDCS-39101568.translation: To the Underworld Gods. Perpetennius lived 35 years.
EDCS-39101568.summary: An epitaph for Perpetennius, recording his age at death and dedicated to the Underworld Gods.

EDCS-39101569.translation: To the Underworld Gods. Salvia lived 18 years.
EDCS-39101569.summary: An epitaph for Salvia, recording her age at death and dedicated to the Underworld Gods.

EDCS-39101571.translation: To Nemesis Grani, Nymphodorus the goldsmith. Farewell.
EDCS-39101571.summary: An inscription with a greeting to Nemesis, addressed to Grani, and naming Nymphodorus the goldsmith.

EDCS-39101572.summary: A dedication from Oltiskos (?) to Aristea.

EDCS-39101592.translation: Shipowners Olympias, daughter of Hyperbolos; Theugnis son of Eubius; Phryneidas son of Teisandros; Diokles son of [---]; Aristodamus son of Eubius; [---]oukos son of Eupeides; Xriarchos son of Peitharchos; Peisandros son of Agathon; Kleidas son of Teisandros; Apollodoros son of Archedemos; Zopyros son of Naukrates; [---] Hyperbolou; Aischron son of Aristoxenos. To Aphrodite.
EDCS-39101592.summary: A dedication to Aphrodite listing numerous shipowners, likely from a context related to maritime trade or a cult.

EDCS-39101594.translation: To Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus, pious father of the fatherland.
EDCS-39101594.summary: A dedication to the Emperor Antoninus Pius, honoring him as a pious father of the fatherland.

EDCS-39101595.translation: To Agrippinus the musician, a memorial.
EDCS-39101595.summary: A memorial inscription for Agrippinus, identified as a musician.

EDCS-39101596.translation: To the Underworld Gods. Androbios Lukios, shipowner, lived 36 years without trouble. Apollonius, together with Musaieos and his brother Theodoros, for his own memory.
EDCS-39101596.summary: An epitaph for the shipowner Androbios Lukios, recording his age and a peaceful life, with the inscription set up by his brothers Apollonius, Musaieos, and Theodoros.

EDCS-39101597.translation: Aurelius Eutychis, for the departed, for the sake of remembrance. To the Underworld Gods. Kyzikos was one fatherland for all, and fate destroyed us all, young men. O passer-by, he desired that one tomb hold us all. And weeping with bitter tears, the one who mourns, whose beautiful name adorns the one who is buried around him. How sweet is our longing even for the dead.
EDCS-39101597.summary: An epitaph dedicated to the Underworld Gods by Aurelius Eutychis, lamenting the loss of fellow citizens from Kyzikos and expressing enduring affection for the deceased.

EDCS-39101599.translation: To the Underworld Gods. Zosima lived E K OI S OAE years.
EDCS-39101599.summary: An epitaph for Zosima, dedicated to the Underworld Gods, recording her lifespan with partially legible numbers.

EDCS-39101602.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Valerius Socrates lived 20 years.
EDCS-39101602.summary: A funerary dedication to the Chthonic gods, recording the age of the deceased Valerius Socrates.

EDCS-39101603.translation: I, Paphios of Paphos, a comedian, having been released from life, lie beneath this earth, having received the crown of life.
EDCS-39101603.summary: A funerary epigram for a comedian named Paphios, lamenting his death and celebrating his life.

EDCS-39101605.translation: Sextus Pompeius Phoebus from Rome lies here.
EDCS-39101605.summary: A simple epitaph for Sextus Pompeius Phoebus, indicating his place of burial and origin.

EDCS-39101606.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Roscia Hermione lived 30 years. Zosimos for his dear wife, for the sake of remembrance.
EDCS-39101606.summary: A funerary dedication to the Chthonic gods by Zosimos for his wife Roscia Hermione, stating her age and his motive.

EDCS-39101608.translation: Phileikita, daughter of Hermodoros, aged six.
EDCS-39101608.summary: An epitaph for a child named Phileikita, indicating her parentage and age.

EDCS-39101609.translation: son of Sarapion, son of Epianakts.
EDCS-39101609.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording a patronymic and a family lineage.

EDCS-39101612.translation: Ulpius Nicephorus of Antioch, from Coele Syria near Daphne, a trader in miscellaneous goods, lies here.
EDCS-39101612.summary: An epitaph for Ulpius Nicephorus, an Antiochian merchant from Coele Syria, identifying his origin and profession.

EDCS-39101613.translation: This is the human condition; gods are here. Be of good cheer, no one is immortal. I, Valens, have dedicated these for remembrance. Be of good cheer, Nicephorus, no one is immortal.
EDCS-39101613.summary: A philosophical epitaph, possibly for Valens and Nicephorus, reflecting on mortality and the divine, and serving as a memorial.

EDCS-39101614.translation: The strategists for five-year terms: [list of names].
EDCS-39101614.summary: A list of strategists, indicating officials who served in five-year terms, likely from a Greek city in the Roman period.

EDCS-39101615.translation: Theocritus, son of Apollodoros, decree concerning the games: 66 talents of oil, 1200 amphorae. Games: 16. At the time of Hareas, son of M..., gymnasium directors: Euxenos, Damatrios, Nymphodoros; oil: 3 pitchers, 200 amphorae. Games: 4, 20. At the time of Deinion, son of Helorius, gymnasium directors: Nymphodoros, Eukleidas, Theodorus, son of Leon; oil: 4 pitchers, 200 amphorae, with reserve. Games: 4, 20. At the time of Herakles, son of Dionysios, gymnasium directors: Artemidoros, son of Athanis, Herakleidas, son of Olympios; oil: 6, 80, 100 amphorae. Games: 5, 20. At the time of Philônidas, gymnasium directors: [---], son of Tharrhippos, Apollodoros; oil: 5 measures, 900 amphorae, with reserve. Games: 8, 20. At the time of Moschos, gymnasium directors: oil, games. At the time of Nikomachos, son of Mnasius, gymnasium directors: [---], son of Aristarchos, Herakleitos, son of Dionysios; oil: 4 pitchers, 100 amphorae, with reserve. Games: 20. At the time of Agatharchos, son of Agatharchos, gymnasium directors: Agatharchos, son of Menôn, Phrynis, son of Phrynis; oil: 4 kotylai, 240, 100 amphorae. Games: 8, 30. At the time of Zoilus, son of Histiarches, gymnasium directors: Zosias, son of Nymphodoros, Om, Kallimachos, son of Philon; oil: 20 SA, trimetron, 80, 100 amphorae, with reserve. Games: 8, 30. At the time of Timolas, son of Xenon, gymnasium directors: Xenios, son of Epidoros, Kal, Aristarchos, son of Theodorus; oil: 4 kotylai, trimetron, pitcher, 9, 60, 100 amphorae. Games: 8, 30. At the time of Hierôn, son of Zotikos, gymnasium directors: Epigenes, son of Aristokrates, Damatrios, son of Philon; oil: trimetron, pitcher, 9, 60, 100 amphorae, with reserve. Games: 8, 30. At the time of Zopyros, son of Chairedamos, gymnasium directors: Agatharchos, son of Agatharchos, Philodamos, son of Aristopolis; oil: 4 kotylai, 8, 80, 100 amphorae. Games: 8, 30. At the time of Archa, son of Ariston, gymnasium directors: Apollodoros, son of Artemidoros, Eratus, son of Apollodoros; oil: trimetron, half-amphora, 17, 200 amphorae, with reserve. Games: 8, 30. At the time of Herakles, son of Apollodoros, gymnasium directors: Artemidoros, son of Philônidas, Apollodoros, son of Phrynis; oil: 10, 200 amphorae. Games: 1, 40. At the time of Gorgias, son of Agatharchos, gymnasium directors: Alexandros, son of Xenon, O As, son of Xenou; oil: Eta, 200 amphorae, with reserve. Games: 1, 40. At the time of Damatrios, son of Aristoboulos, gymnasium directors: Eukleidas, son of Nymphodoros, As, Nikostratos, son of Agathon; oil: trimetron, 4 pitchers, 9, 60, 200 amphorae, for 16 months. Games: 1, 40. At the time of Aristokrates, son of Zopyros, gymnasium directors: Philônidas, son of Krithon, Asit, Artemidoros, son of Theophilus; oil: 2 pitchers, 100 amphorae, for 8 months. Games: 1, 40. At the time of Artemidoros, gymnasium directors: Hyperbolos, son of Nymphodoros, Olympias, daughter of Theodorus; oil: trimetron, half-amphora, 200 amphorae. Games: 1, 40. At the time of Artemon, son of Hippokrates, gymnasium directors: Hyperbolos, son of Onasikrates, Asin, Phalacros, son of Aristokrates; Tan: income: 4 liters, 292,400, 90,000. Expenditure: 1, 60 liters, 8, 70, 300, 20,000 talents. Balance: 3, 60 liters, 3, 10, 100,000, 40,000. Oil income: 8, 30, 400 amphorae. Expenditure: trimetron, 9, 10, 400 amphorae. Of this for the games: trimetron, 8, 10, 200 amphorae. Balance: trimetron, 5 pitchers, 8, 10 amphorae. Games: 5, 30. At the time of Nikostratos, son of Philon, gymnasium directors: Andromachos, son of Apollonida, Dex, Phrynis, son of Olympios, Assit. Income: 7, 60 liters, 6, 20, 800, 2,000,000 talents. Expenditure: 8, 70 liters, 3, 70, 200, 2,000,000 talents. Balance: 3, 50 liters, 50, 200, 100,000 talents. And the borrowed money: 6, 50 liters, 2,300,000, 20,000 talents. Of this for the games and the dinner: 2 liters, 8, 80, 800, 3,000 talents. And in inns: 4, 50 liters, 4, 10, 400, 7,000,000 talents. Oil income: 3, 90, 100 amphorae. Expenditure: the same. At the time of Philistos, son of Hippôn, gymnasium directors: Damatrios, son of Philon, Pel, Eukleidas, son of Nymphodoros, Oin. Income: 8, 80 liters, 4, 400, 6,000,000 talents. Expenditure: 2, 40 liters, 2, 50, 400, 3,000,000 talents. Balance: 2, 10, 100 liters, 5, 30, 900, 2,000 talents. And the borrowed money: 4, 50 liters, 6, 10,000, 20,000 talents. Of this for the games and the dinner: 2, 600, 3,000 talents. And in inns: 4, 50 liters, 4, 10, 400, 7,000,000 talents. Oil income: 7, 70, 100 amphorae. Expenditure: the same. At the time of Eudoxos, son of Sosios, gymnasium directors: Apollodoros, son of Phrynis, Dam, Phrynis, son of Philistion, Spar. Income: 7, 70 liters, 3, 80, 200, 9,000,000 talents. Expenditure: 3, 70 liters, 5, 10, 5,000, 3,000,000 talents. Balance: 5, 40 liters, 7, 10, 600, 2,000 talents. And the borrowed money: 9, 70 liters, 50, 600, 10,000, 2,000,000 talents. Of this for the games and the dinner: 5, 20 liters, 6, 30, 200, 4,000 talents. And in inns: 4, 50 liters, 4, 10, 400, 7,000,000 talents. Oil income: half-amphora, 6, 20, 200 amphorae. Expenditure: the same. At the time of Aristopolis, son of Philodamos.
EDCS-39101615.summary: A detailed financial account of a series of games, listing the income, expenditure, and balances for oil, money, and other provisions under various gymnasium directors and officials over different periods.

EDCS-39101616.translation: During the prytany of Apollodorus son of Apollodorus, Artemisios, the priest Lyciscus son of Lyciscus of Asis, for the hieromnemon, income forty pounds seven hundred eighty-eight hundred talents, expenditure forty pounds nine hundred forty-three hundred talents, remaining eight hundred fifty hundred talents, of this, continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, forty pounds one hundred seventy hundred talents, and from the hieromnemon of the period of Ariston, remaining four hundred ninety hundred talents, ten thousand hundred thousand talents, of this, continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, eighty pounds eighty hundred four hundred talents, for the tamiai, income ten hundred pounds seven hundred twenty-six hundred seven thousand talents, expenditure seventy pounds four hundred eighty hundred seven thousand talents, remaining forty pounds three hundred forty-two hundred talents, and continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, forty pounds three hundred forty-two hundred talents, and from the tamiai of the period of Ariston, remaining eight hundred eighty pounds four hundred ninety-two hundred thousand six hundred myriads of talents, of this, continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, thirty hundred talents, and in the inn of Zoticus son of Euboulidas of Assis, eighty pounds six hundred sixty-six hundred one thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, income eleven and a half measures eight hundred sixty-seven hundred medimnoi, expenditure seven and a half measures remaining four and a half measures eight hundred sixty-seven hundred medimnoi, and from the former sitophylakes, for bronze, remaining fourteen pounds seven hundred thirty-five hundred five thousand talents, of this, continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias, twenty hundred talents, for beans, remaining from the former sitophylakes, three and a half measures eight hundred four hundred medimnoi and a half medimnos, eleven medimnoi of melinas. From the agertai, for beans, remaining ten medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, income eighty pounds one hundred seventy-nine hundred one thousand talents, expenditure eighty pounds one hundred seventy-nine hundred one thousand talents, and remaining three hundred fifty pounds eleven hundred nine hundred one thousand talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining forty pounds five hundred ninety-six hundred three thousand talents. For Dionysius, during the prytany of Heraclitus son of Apollodorus of Oetir, for the hieromnemon, income sixty pounds one hundred seventy-two hundred talents, expenditure twenty pounds two thousand talents, remaining forty pounds one hundred seventy talents. For the tamiai, income one hundred ninety pounds two hundred seventy-nine hundred two thousand talents, expenditure seventy hundred pounds nine hundred sixty-eight hundred two thousand talents, remaining four hundred pounds two talents. And continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, forty pounds three hundred forty-two hundred talents. And from the hieromnemon of the period of Ariston, remaining four hundred ninety hundred talents, ten thousand hundred thousand talents, of this, continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, eighty pounds four hundred eighty hundred four hundred talents. And from the tamiai of the period of Ariston, remaining eight hundred eighty pounds four hundred ninety-two hundred thousand six hundred myriads of talents, of this, continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, thirty hundred talents. And in the inn of Zoticus son of Euboulidas of Assis, eighty pounds six hundred sixty-six hundred one thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for bronze, income eighty pounds six hundred sixty hundred thousand talents, expenditure eighty pounds six hundred sixty hundred thousand talents. For beans, expenditure seven and a half measures two hundred medimnoi, remaining nine and a half measures seven hundred sixty-five hundred medimnoi. And from the former sitophylakes, for bronze, remaining fourteen pounds seven hundred thirty-five hundred five thousand talents, of this, continuing to Pausanias son of Pausanias of Areth, twenty hundred talents, for beans, remaining from the former sitophylakes, three and a half measures eight hundred four hundred medimnoi and a half medimnos, eleven medimnoi of melinas. From the agertai, for beans, remaining ten medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, expenditure one thousand talents, that which was stored. And remaining three hundred fifty pounds eleven hundred nine hundred talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, expenditure nine hundred seventy-three hundred two thousand talents, remaining forty pounds six hundred thirty hundred one thousand talents. For the prytany of Alk, for the hieromnemon, income eighty pounds thousand talents, expenditure nine hundred eighty-nine hundred talents, remaining one hundred talents. For the tamiai, income five pounds three hundred thirty-two hundred five thousand talents, expenditure seven hundred ninety pounds eight hundred ninety-two hundred thousand talents, remaining eight hundred twenty pounds four hundred twenty-three hundred talents. And from the hieromnemon of the period of Ariston, remaining fourteen pounds six hundred twenty-three hundred thousand ten thousand talents. And from the tamiai of the period of Ariston, remaining eight hundred forty pounds one hundred thirty-six hundred eight thousand five hundred myriads of talents. Of this, in the inn of Zoticus son of Euboulidas, eighty pounds six hundred sixty-six hundred one thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for bronze, income eighty pounds two hundred seventy hundred thousand talents, expenditure eighty pounds two hundred seventy hundred thousand talents. For beans, expenditure seven and a half measures two hundred fifty-three hundred medimnoi, remaining two and a half measures five hundred twenty hundred medimnoi. And from the former sitophylakes, for bronze, remaining fourteen pounds three hundred eighty-six hundred four thousand talents, for beans, remaining from the former sitophylakes, three and a half measures eight hundred four hundred medimnoi and a half medimnos, eleven medimnoi of melinas. From the agertai, for beans, remaining ten medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining three hundred fifty pounds eleven hundred nine hundred talents. From the sitonai of the period of Theodorus, for the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining forty pounds six hundred thirty hundred one thousand talents. From the agertai from the sitonia.
EDCS-39101616.summary: This is a financial account listing income and expenditure for various officials, including hieromnemon, tamiai, and sitophylakes, likely for a city or organization, with figures provided in pounds, talents, and medimnoi, spanning multiple prytaneis and periods.

EDCS-39101620.translation: For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, three thousand talents in silver works for the sitonion, from those who made pledges. Remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. During the prytany of Itonius, Nicias son of Lysias of Pam, for the hieromnemon, income eighty pounds nine hundred talents, expenditure two hundred pounds nine hundred sixty-eight hundred talents, remaining forty-two pounds eight hundred thirty hundred talents. For the tamiai, income eighteen pounds five hundred sixty-two thousand talents, expenditure ten hundred pounds eight hundred sixty-two hundred three thousand talents, remaining seventy pounds five talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, remaining from the lower storage, eight and a half measures seven hundred seventy medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, three thousand talents in silver works for the sitonion, from those who made pledges. Remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. During the prytany of Karneius, Phrynis son of Phrynius of Dam, for the hieromnemon, income forty pounds five hundred seventy hundred talents, expenditure eighty pounds nine hundred eighty hundred talents, remaining forty hundred pounds three hundred twenty hundred talents. For the tamiai, income thirty-three pounds seven hundred ninety-five hundred two thousand talents, expenditure seventy-six pounds eight hundred fifty-two thousand talents, remaining sixteen pounds eight hundred talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, remaining from the lower storage, eight and a half measures seven hundred seventy medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, three thousand talents in silver works for the sitonion, from those who made pledges. Remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. During the prytany of Lanothrus, O... of Oetir, for the hieromnemon, income thirty-eight pounds eight hundred seventy seven hundred talents, expenditure eighty pounds six hundred seventy seven hundred talents, remaining sixty-two pounds forty-five hundred talents. For the tamiai, income thirty-three pounds three hundred twenty-seven hundred two thousand four myriads of talents, expenditure eighty-four pounds three hundred seventy-two hundred seven thousand four myriads of talents. Excess five hundred thirty pounds two hundred thirty talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, remaining from the lower storage, eight and a half measures seven hundred seventy medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, three thousand talents in silver works for the sitonion, from those who made pledges. Remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, three thousand talents in silver works for the sitonion, from those who made pledges. Remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, three thousand talents in silver works for the sitonion, from those who made pledges. Remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. During the prytany of Apollonius, Olympias daughter of Heracles of Oetir, for the hieromnemon, income seven pounds three hundred sixty-three hundred talents, expenditure seven hundred forty hundred talents, remaining sixty-nine pounds one talent. For the tamiai, income three hundred eighty pounds five hundred eighty-seven hundred four thousand talents, expenditure sixty-five pounds thirty-seven hundred seven hundred four thousand talents, remaining one hundred three pounds fifty-ten talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, expenditure fifty hundred medimnoi, remaining ten and a half measures three hundred twenty hundred medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, three thousand talents in silver works for the sitonion, from those who made pledges, forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. During the prytany of Dodcates, Philistion son of Apollonius of Chalk, for the hieromnemon, income one hundred fifty pounds two hundred thirty-five hundred talents, expenditure sixty-five pounds three hundred talents, remaining sixty-nine hundred talents. For the tamiai, income eighty pounds seven hundred forty-two thousand talents, expenditure ten pounds thirty-five hundred one thousand talents, remaining forty-one pounds eighty-nine hundred one thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, expenditure ten and a half measures nine hundred thirty-two hundred medimnoi, remaining seven and a half measures seven thousand medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. For the sitonion, from those who made pledges, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. For purchase through sales, nine pounds eight hundred two hundred talents.
EDCS-39101620.summary: This inscription is a detailed financial ledger, likely from a public or religious institution, recording income and expenditures for various officials including sitonai, hieromnemon, tamiai, and sitophylakes, with specific mentions of funds from pledges and silver works, and covering multiple prytaneis.

EDCS-39101622.translation: During the prytany of Apellaios, Ammonios son of Agathon of Sparta, for the hieromnemon, income five pounds forty-one hundred talents, expenditure eleven pounds ten talents, remaining forty-two pounds eight hundred eighty-three thousand ten myriads of talents. For the tamiai, income thirty-three pounds six hundred twenty-two thousand talents, expenditure fifty-two pounds four hundred twenty-six hundred one thousand talents, remaining thirty-four pounds four hundred ninety-six hundred thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, the equivalent for the sitonion of Phrynius, the equivalent for the sitonion of Eucleidas, the equivalent for the sitonion, from those who made pledges, the equivalent. During the second prytany of Apellaios, all for the hieromnemon, income sixty pounds two hundred sixty hundred talents, expenditure eighty pounds four hundred fifty-two hundred talents, remaining twenty-two pounds five hundred ninety-six hundred three thousand ten myriads of talents. For the tamiai, income thirty-two pounds seven hundred forty-two thousand talents, expenditure forty-two pounds twenty-five hundred one thousand talents, remaining ten pounds one hundred sixty-six hundred thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, the equivalent for the sitonion of Phrynius, the equivalent for the sitonion of Eucleidas, the equivalent for the sitonion, from those who made pledges, the equivalent. During the prytany of Itonius, Nicias son of Lysias of Pam, for the hieromnemon, income hundred pounds hundred talents, expenditure forty-four pounds six hundred seventy-six hundred talents, remaining eighteen pounds twenty hundred three thousand ten myriads of talents. For the tamiai, income fifty-five pounds thirty hundred four hundred two myriads of talents, expenditure seventy-six pounds four hundred ninety-four hundred four thousand talents, remaining fourteen hundred pounds seven hundred fifty-five hundred four myriads of talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, the equivalent for the sitonion of Phrynius, the equivalent for the sitonion of Eucleidas, the equivalent for the sitonion, from those who made pledges, the equivalent. During the prytany of Soteles, Soteles son of Soteles of Par, for the hieromnemon, income sixty pounds two hundred ninety hundred talents, expenditure eighty pounds thirty hundred seven hundred eight thousand talents, remaining eighteen pounds one hundred eighty-four hundred four thousand talents. For the tamiai, income one hundred six hundred twenty-two hundred seven thousand two myriads of talents, expenditure ninety-five pounds six hundred twenty-eight thousand three myriads of talents, remaining forty-eight hundred seven hundred three thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, the equivalent for the sitonion of Phrynius, the equivalent for the sitonion of Eucleidas, the equivalent for the sitonion, from those who made pledges, the equivalent. For the sitophylakes, for beans, the equivalent for the sitonion, the equivalent. For the sitonion, the equivalent. During the prytany of Lanothrus, for the hieromnemon, income seventy talents, expenditure talents, remaining eight pounds talents. For the tamiai, income five thousand talents, expenditure hundred six thousand talents, remaining thirty-four pounds hundred two thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, expenditure four hundred medimnoi, remaining one half medimnos nine hundred sixty-eight hundred medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. For the sitonion, from those who made pledges, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. For purchase through sales, six hundred two thousand talents. During the prytany of Apollonius, Philistion son of Ageas of Sparta, for the hieromnemon, income thirty-three pounds seventy hundred two thousand talents, expenditure eight pounds sixty-seven hundred five thousand talents, remaining nine pounds twenty-seven hundred N talents. For the tamiai, income fifty pounds five thousand talents, expenditure three hundred seven thousand talents, remaining seventy X talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, expenditure five hundred medimnoi, remaining ten and a half measures three hundred twenty hundred thousand medimnoi.
