EDCS-39101622.summary: This lengthy inscription details financial accounts for various administrative and religious roles, including prytanes, hieromnemon, tamiai, and sitophylakes, across several prytaneis and periods, indicating the management of public funds and resources, with extensive use of numerical values and specific commodity designations.

EDCS-39101623.translation: For the sitonion, from those who made pledges of funds, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three thousand talents, and from the agertai for grain, two hundred fifty talents. During the prytany of Eucleius, Philistion son of Philistion of Sparta, for the hieromnemon, income five pounds thirty-three thousand talents, expenditure five pounds ninety-three hundred talents, remaining eight hundred forty pounds nine hundred thirty talents. For the tamiai, income nine hundred ten pounds two thousand myriads of talents, expenditure thirty-one pounds nine hundred eight thousand talents, remaining five hundred eight hundred five thousand talents, one thousand talents. From the gymnasiarchs, for..., six pounds sixty-six hundred one thousand talents, to be purchased. For the sitophylakes, for bronze, income thirty talents, expenditure the same. For beans, income two hundred medimnoi, expenditure one half medimnos eleven medimnoi, remaining two and a half measures sixteen hundred eight hundred medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. Of this, in silver works from the agertai, thirty talents. For the sitonion, from those who made pledges of funds, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three thousand talents, and from the agertai for grain, two hundred fifty talents. For purchase through sales, nine pounds eighty-two hundred talents. During the prytany of Dodcates, Olympios son of Tau, for the hieromnemon, income twenty pounds nineteen hundred talents, expenditure seven hundred twenty pounds sixty-four hundred one thousand talents, remaining sixty-four pounds forty-one hundred talents. For the tamiai, income sixty pounds seven hundred forty-two thousand talents, expenditure ten pounds thirty-five hundred one thousand talents, remaining forty-one pounds eighty-nine hundred one thousand talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, expenditure ten and a half measures nine hundred thirty-two hundred medimnoi, remaining seven and a half measures seven thousand medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. The equivalent. For the sitonion of Phrynius, the equivalent. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, the equivalent. For the sitonion, from those who made pledges, the equivalent. During the prytany of Eucleius, Ammonios son of Sarapion of Areth, for the hieromnemon, income eighty-three pounds five hundred seventy-seven hundred five thousand myriads of talents, expenditure seventy-six pounds eight hundred seventy-seven hundred four thousand talents, remaining eighty-one pounds forty-one hundred one thousand myriads of talents. Of this, ten thousand myriads of talents for Zeus, and thirty pounds seven hundred thousand talents for Dionysus. For the tamiai, income thirty-six pounds eight hundred forty-seven hundred five thousand four myriads of talents, expenditure sixty-five pounds seventy hundred one hundred three thousand two myriads of talents, remaining twelve pounds five hundred forty-five hundred four thousand two myriads of talents. For the sitophylakes, for beans, income three hundred medimnoi, expenditure ten and a half measures three hundred twenty hundred thousand medimnoi, remaining nine and a half measures three hundred sixty-two hundred thousand medimnoi. For the sitonion of Phrynius, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-three hundred thousand ten myriads of talents. For the sitonion of Eucleidas, remaining ten hundred pounds four hundred seventy-seven hundred one thousand two myriads of talents. For the sitonion, from those who made pledges, remaining forty pounds three hundred thirty-five hundred three thousand talents. For purchase through sales, sixty-four hundred two thousand talents.
EDCS-39101623.summary: This inscription details financial accounts for various public and religious bodies, including prytanes, hieromnemon, tamiai, and sitophylakes, spanning multiple prytaneis and periods, with specific mentions of funds for grain, bronze, and various deities, and including expenditures for purchases and sales.

EDCS-39101624.translation: For the holy goddesses, a thank-offering. Lucius Mallius Hermes, the builder.
EDCS-39101624.summary: This is a short votive inscription dedicated to goddesses by Lucius Mallius Hermes, identifying him as the builder.

EDCS-39101626.translation: Near these standing pillars, Barkaios son of Karneades, son of Eukritos, has set up a pure altar for Hestia Sarapis. Stranger, you who are a neokoros, and his wife Pythias, his daughter Eraso, may they always have joyful life and prosperity, in return for which, O queen, daughter of Zeus, may you grant them.
EDCS-39101626.summary: This is a dedication of an altar by Barkaios, son of Karneades, to Hestia Sarapis, with a prayer for his family, invoking the queen, daughter of Zeus.

EDCS-39101627.translation: The people of Tauromenion [honors] Olympios son of Olympios, victor in the Pythian Games by chariot.
EDCS-39101627.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Olympios son of Olympios, a victor in the Pythian Games in chariot racing, by the people of Tauromenion.

EDCS-39101628.translation: Gaius Claudius Marcellus, son of Marcus.
EDCS-39101628.summary: This is a simple funerary or commemorative inscription identifying Gaius Claudius Marcellus, son of Marcus.

EDCS-39101633.translation: Statius Eutychus lived blamelessly for 16 years, 3 months, and 7 days.
EDCS-39101633.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Statius Eutychus.

EDCS-39101634.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely funerary or dedicatory, identifying Fabeia, daughter of Ignotus.

EDCS-39101635.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Chairoisa, always most sweet, having lived, her father made this for his own daughter.
EDCS-39101635.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a father to his deceased daughter, addressed to the Chthonic Gods.

EDCS-39101638.translation: Here lies Agathon, faithful, having lived 7 years and 7 months. He rested in the consulship of Honorius for the 8th time and Theodosius for the 3rd time, August Emperors, on the day before the 3rd of the Ides of October, a day of the Moon.
EDCS-39101638.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy named Agathon, dating to the consulship of Honorius and Theodosius.

EDCS-39101640.translation: Klodianos and Mousa, with their slaves, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-39101640.summary: A votive inscription set up by Klodianos and Mousa and their slaves.

EDCS-39101644.summary: A dedicatory inscription to Persephone, identified as the queen of the Catanaeans.

EDCS-39101648.translation: Flavius Felix Eumathius, most illustrious, consular of the province, stated: The warm baths of Achilleia, from an ancient design, required for repairs... This inscription records the details of the repair and construction of the Achilleian warm baths, specifying materials, daily costs, and individuals involved, under the governorship of Liberalis and the consulship of Flavius Theodosius Augustus, with Maximinus also mentioned.
EDCS-39101648.summary: A lengthy inscription detailing the repair and construction of the Achilleian warm baths, including costs and personnel, from the period of the consulship of Flavius Theodosius Augustus.

EDCS-39101651.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Agathopous, good one, greetings. You lived [---] years.
EDCS-39101651.summary: A funerary monument to Agathopous, addressed to the Chthonic Gods and noting his lifespan.

EDCS-39101654.summary: A simple funerary epitaph for an infant named Asteris.

EDCS-39101656.translation: Aurelius Vitalis. Now I lie here, leaving the wandering of the world and ascending to the eternal home, having grieved no one, I lead a life here that is smooth. I lived 65 years.
EDCS-39101656.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Vitalis, who lived 65 years and describes his peaceful afterlife.

EDCS-39101657.translation: Ios Philo and Aurelia Clodianē, my wife, acquired a plot of land, founded it, and made a bedroom for ourselves and our heirs, and we declare: [---] eternal.
EDCS-39101657.summary: An inscription recording the purchase and foundation of a burial plot and tomb by Ios Philo and his wife Aurelia Clodianē for themselves and their heirs.

EDCS-39101658.translation: Aphrodisias and Theuda and Marullas, children, made this in memory of their parents.
EDCS-39101658.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by children (Aphrodisias, Theuda, and Marullas) in memory of their parents.

EDCS-39101660.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Galates lived 15 years. Her son Primigenis made this.
EDCS-39101660.summary: A funerary epigram for Galates, set up by her son Primigenis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101662.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Daphne, sweetest, farewell. You lived 100 years. Fate has decreed...
EDCS-39101662.summary: A funerary epigram for Daphne, addressed to her, lamenting her death and mentioning her lifespan of 100 years, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101666.translation: Remember Encarpis, good and blameless. She lived 50 years.
EDCS-39101666.summary: A funerary epitaph remembering Encarpis, described as good and blameless, who lived 50 years, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101667.translation: I am of the Erechtheids. Erathenas the son of Aides, lived seven years. Eukarpos, son of Genyos, before seven years... spirit.
EDCS-39101667.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription for Erathenas, son of Aides, who lived seven years, and potentially Eukarpos, son of Genyos, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101669.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Zotikos the father made this in memory of his child, who lived 28 years.
EDCS-39101669.summary: A funerary epigram for a child, dedicated by their father Zotikos, who lived 28 years, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101671.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Julia Aelianē lived 20 years, 2 months, 25 days. Aelianos to his most devoted daughter.
EDCS-39101671.summary: A funerary epigram for Julia Aelianē, who lived 20 years, 2 months, and 25 days, dedicated by her father Aelianos, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101672.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Julia Galēnē, the 'fond of the ship', lived 33 years.
EDCS-39101672.summary: A funerary epigram for Julia Galēnē, nicknamed 'fond of the ship', who lived 33 years, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101673.translation: Julia Germana, the god-beloved, lived 14 years, 11 months, 8 days. Julius Germanos made this for his sweetest daughter.
EDCS-39101673.summary: A funerary epigram for Julia Germana, described as god-beloved, who lived 14 years, 11 months, and 8 days, set up by her father Julius Germanos, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101674.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Julia Domitia lived 7 years, 3 months, 5 days. Parents made this.
EDCS-39101674.summary: A funerary epigram for Julia Domitia, who lived 7 years, 3 months, and 5 days, dedicated by her parents, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101675.summary: A simple epitaph wishing farewell to Lucius Caelius Felix, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101676.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Kassia Benousta lived eleven years, nine months, and eight days. Her father and mother made this for their sweetest child.
EDCS-39101676.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of young Kassia Benousta, for whom her parents erected a monument.

EDCS-39101677.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Klodia Valentina, farewell. She lived 40 years. Ignatis Karikos made this for his own wife in memory.
EDCS-39101677.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Ignatis, for whom her husband Ignatis Karikos erected a monument.

EDCS-39101678.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Cornelius Agathemeros lived thirty-eight years. Euphrosyne made this for her own spouse in memory.
EDCS-39101678.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Euphrosyne, for whom her spouse Cornelius Agathemeros erected a monument.

EDCS-39101681.translation: Libios, worthy and blameless, lived twenty-eight years.
EDCS-39101681.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Libios, described as worthy and blameless.

EDCS-39101682.translation: Lucifera made this for herself and all her own family while alive.
EDCS-39101682.summary: This funerary epitaph records Lucifera's provision for herself and her family.

EDCS-39101683.translation: Marcia Lucilia, worthy and blameless, [lived] alive twenty years.
EDCS-39101683.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Marcia Lucilia, described as worthy and blameless, who died at twenty years of age.

EDCS-39101684.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Myrtale, most worthy, lived thirty years and three months. Cornelius Agathemeros made this for his most pious wife.
EDCS-39101684.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Myrtale, for whom her husband Cornelius Agathemeros erected a monument.

EDCS-39101685.translation: Oporine, worthy one, farewell. You lived ten years.
EDCS-39101685.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Oporine, described as worthy.

EDCS-39101686.translation: Marcus Vibianius Zosimus, having lived well for fifty-five years, farewell, passerby.
EDCS-39101686.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Marcus Vibianius Zosimus, who lived a long and apparently good life.

EDCS-39101687.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Vibianius Zotikos lived thirty years. Herakleia [made this] for her most pious husband.
EDCS-39101687.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Vibianius Zotikos, for whom his wife Herakleia erected a monument.

EDCS-39101688.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Peskennis Calaticus lived 53 years and 3 months. Peskennia Gamica [made this] for her husband.
EDCS-39101688.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Peskennis Calaticus, made by his wife Peskennia Gamica.

EDCS-39101689.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Preimiteibus, good one, farewell. You lived 9 years. To the Chthonic Gods. Queinta lived 14 years. Copria her mother made [this].
EDCS-39101689.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Chthonic Gods and commemorates the lives of Preimiteibus and Queinta, with Copria identified as Queinta's mother.

EDCS-39101692.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Roscia Iucounda, pious, lived 60 years.
EDCS-39101692.summary: This is a funerary epigram dedicated to the Chthonic Gods for Roscia Iucounda, recording her pious life of 60 years.

EDCS-39101693.translation: Saboukis Callimorphos lived 32 years. Saboukis Zoilus, the patron, made [this].
EDCS-39101693.summary: This funerary epigram records the lifespan of Saboukis Callimorphos and states that Saboukis Zoilus, his patron, created it.

EDCS-39101695.translation: To Quintus Sossius and Petronia Sossia Chiea, for their daughter who lived 10 years and 22 days.
EDCS-39101695.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Quintus Sossius and Petronia Sossia Chiea to their daughter who died at the age of 10 years and 22 days.

EDCS-39101696.translation: To Sosia Elata, dearest, her husband Aphrodisios [dedicated this].
EDCS-39101696.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Aphrodisios to his wife Sosia Elata.

EDCS-39101697.summary: This funerary epigram records the lifespan of Statilia Theagenis as 60 years.

EDCS-39101698.summary: This funerary epigram records the lifespan of Stephani as 8 years and 3 days.

EDCS-39101703.translation: [She] lived 4 years. Hygeia made [this] in memory.
EDCS-39101703.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Hygeia in memory of a child who lived 4 years.

EDCS-39101704.translation: Horaia, good and blameless, farewell. Gamis, good and blameless, farewell. Urbanion, Gongius, good and blameless, farewell.
EDCS-39101704.summary: This is a funerary inscription containing multiple eulogies for Horaia, Gamis, Urbanion, and Gongius, wishing them farewell.

EDCS-39101707.summary: This is a funerary epigram for a woman named Perimyssa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101716.translation: Here lies Agatha, aged 40, 7 days before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-39101716.summary: A funerary epigram recording the death of Agatha, who died at the age of 40, with the date specified relative to the Roman calendar.

EDCS-39101717.translation: Agatha died at the age of 60. On the preparation day, the 9th from the Kalends of September, she was carried out on a Saturday, thanks be to the Lord and Christ.
EDCS-39101717.summary: A Christian funerary epigram for Agatha, detailing her age at death and the date of her burial, referencing Christian beliefs.

EDCS-39101718.translation: All nature and the wide air give birth to you, O Death. Suddenly you snatched my infant. Or what necessity is there? If he had grown old, he would not have been yours. The Lord [was born?]. He lived 11 months, died 10 days before the Kalends of September, on a Sunday, the day of the Sun. Good peace to the good.
EDCS-39101718.summary: A lament for a deceased infant, Agathon, expressing grief and questioning death, with dates provided in relation to Roman and Christian calendars.

EDCS-39101719.summary: A short inscription wishing long life to Hadrianus and Deuteris, possibly on a tomb or as a dedication.

EDCS-39101720.translation: Titus Aelius Victoreinus lived for 16 years, 5 months, and 17 days. His pious brothers made this with their mother from their own means.
EDCS-39101720.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Aelius Victoreinus, commissioned by his siblings and mother, specifying his age at death.

EDCS-39101722.translation: Marcus Aurelius Restutus and Zosime bought [this] from their own property.
EDCS-39101722.summary: An inscription recording the purchase of property or a burial plot by Marcus Aurelius Restutus and Zosime.

EDCS-39101725.translation: Here lies Victorina, who lived for 30 years, dying on the 6th day before the Ides [of a month].
EDCS-39101725.summary: A funerary inscription for Victorina, stating her age at death and the date relative to the Roman calendar.

EDCS-39101728.translation: A monument to Bonifatius. The endurance of many tribulations works out hope of salvation for all. Therefore, let the practice of endurance be offered to all of us who desire to attain eternal life, through which many have also attained heavenly life. Therefore, I too, the completely sinful, have prayed to escape the sojourn of this life quickly, and having prayed, I have attained this, after the change from this life to righteousness. Therefore, let the repose from the entire insatiable life be given to me with my fellow servant in Christ and my sweetest mother.
EDCS-39101728.summary: A Christian funerary text dedicated to Bonifatius, emphasizing endurance, hope for salvation, and the desire for eternal life.

EDCS-39101729.translation: Here lies Doia, who lived for 28 years, dying on the [date missing] day before the Kalends of October. She lived [duration missing].
EDCS-39101729.summary: A funerary inscription for Doia, indicating her age at death and the date relative to the Roman calendar, though some details are lost.

EDCS-39101734.translation: Here lies Eutychianus, who lived in Christ and died on the fourth day before the Kalends of August, during the consulship of Anicius Auchenius Bassus and Flavius Philippus.
EDCS-39101734.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eutychianus, dating to the consulship of Bassus and Philippus (likely 408 AD), recording his death and life in Christ.

EDCS-39101735.translation: As a token of affection, Abramius, having received a place in the bosom [of the earth], Eutychius lies here, having lived 35 years and died on the sixth day before the Ides of March.
EDCS-39101735.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eutychius, mentioning his friend Abramius, and dating to the consulship of Bassus and Philippus (likely 408 AD) by the mention of 'Brion'.

EDCS-39101736.translation: Here lies the memory of the blessed Euliba; may God remember you forever.
EDCS-39101736.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Euliba, expressing a prayer for her remembrance by God.

EDCS-39101737.translation: On the sixth day before the Kalends of June, I, Theodulus, while alive and having chosen, bought [this place] from Ariston.
EDCS-39101737.summary: This is a pre-burial inscription by Theodulus, who purchased a burial plot from Ariston on June 2nd.

EDCS-39101738.translation: Iulianus died on the fifteenth day before the Kalends of April, having lived 50 years.
EDCS-39101738.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulianus, recording his death date and age (50 years).

EDCS-39101739.translation: Callistus and Eucarpia, the devoted couple, made [this] for themselves while alive and for their own. Peace to all.
EDCS-39101739.summary: This is a pre-burial inscription by the couple Callistus and Eucarpia, who commissioned their tomb for themselves and their family.

EDCS-39101740.translation: Here lies Caecilianus, who lived 20 years. Peace to all in God.
EDCS-39101740.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caecilianus, recording his age at death (20 years) and offering a general blessing.

EDCS-39101741.translation: My lord Evagrius granted me, Theodulus, this place, and Constantis and Caecilia lie here.
EDCS-39101741.summary: This inscription records that Evagrius granted a burial plot to Theodulus, where Constantis and Caecilia are interred.

EDCS-39101742.translation: Martyrius lived twenty-two years. Peace to you.
EDCS-39101742.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Martyrius, recording his age at death (22 years) and a personal farewell.

EDCS-39101743.translation: Here lies Ursulus, who lived 25 years, 6 months, and 8 days, dying on the fifth day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-39101743.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ursulus, detailing his lifespan (25 years, 6 months, 8 days) and date of death.

EDCS-39101744.translation: Petronius lies here, having died at the age of 6 years.
EDCS-39101744.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of a young child named Petronius, who died at the age of 6.

EDCS-39101745.translation: Primogenes died, having lived for 9 years, and lies here.
EDCS-39101745.summary: This epitaph records the death of Primogenes, who died at the age of 9.

EDCS-39101747.translation: Rhodiene lies here; she lived for 5 years and [some months], and died on the third day before the Ides of November.
EDCS-39101747.summary: This tombstone states that Rhodiene died on the 29th of October, having lived for 5 years and some months.

EDCS-39101748.translation: With God and his Father, Christ, Sabina, the servant of God, fell asleep on the third day before the Kalends.
EDCS-39101748.summary: This is a Christian epitaph recording the death of Sabina, a servant of God.

EDCS-39101749.translation: Sotiris lies here, dying on the third day before the Kalends of December, having lived for 50 years. Christian.
EDCS-39101749.summary: This is a Christian tombstone for Sotiris, who died on November 29th, having lived for 50 years.

EDCS-39101750.translation: Eutychēmos died on the fifteenth day before the Kalends of January, having lived for 100 years, living a good life; he bought it for himself and his own.
EDCS-39101750.summary: This epitaph records the death of Eutychēmos, who died at the age of 100 and purchased the burial plot for himself and his family.

EDCS-39101971.translation: Hieronymus, son of Hierocles of Syracuse, king and governor, to all the gods.
EDCS-39101971.summary: This is a dedication from Hieronymus, who was king and governor of Syracuse, to all the gods.

EDCS-39101973.translation: Aristoboula, daughter of Theodoros, (dedicated) the triclinium and the altar to the Nymphs.
EDCS-39101973.summary: This is a dedication by Aristoboula, daughter of Theodoros, of a dining room and altar to the Nymphs.

EDCS-39101975.translation: To Agathos Daimon, his sons I., Simos, Ariston.
EDCS-39101975.summary: This is a dedication to the Good Spirit (Agathos Daimon) by his sons, identified by initials and names.

EDCS-39101980.summary: This inscription lists the names of six individuals: Zotikos, Dekomos, Euktēmōn, Onasimos, and Philēmōn.

EDCS-39101981.translation: Epikrates, son of Ageas, the ATODYMAS, having built the PELTHRON, gave it to his sons for the city.
EDCS-39101981.summary: This inscription records the construction of a building (PELTHRON) by Epikrates for his sons and the city, likely a public benefaction.

EDCS-39101986.summary: This is a maker's signature by Zenon of Aphrodisias, likely on a work of art or craft.

EDCS-39101989.translation: Agathon, good and blameless, greetings; he lived 40 years.
EDCS-39101989.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Agathon, recording his name, virtues, and age at death.

EDCS-39101990.translation: Here lie Agalmatis and Trianos, slaves of the most brilliant woman Sophia.
EDCS-39101990.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two slaves, Agalmatis and Trianos, belonging to Sophia.

EDCS-39101991.translation: Marcus Akeileianos Leikeinianos, good and blameless, lived 38 years and 4 months.
EDCS-39101991.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Akeileianos Leikeinianos, detailing his virtues and lifespan.

EDCS-39101993.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmented funerary epigram or dedication, mentioning Athedia and indicating good character.

EDCS-39101994.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Aphrodisias, daughter of Dionysios, greetings. Philiste her mother made (this) for her while she was alive.
EDCS-39101994.summary: This is a dedication to the Chthonic Gods, made by Philiste for her daughter Aphrodisias, likely a votive offering during Aphrodisias's lifetime.

EDCS-39101995.summary: This inscription likely marks a public building or establishment, possibly an inn or guesthouse, associated with Decomia Syrriska.

EDCS-39101996.summary: This is a very brief funerary inscription recording the extremely short lifespan of Doryphoros.

EDCS-39101997.summary: This is a funerary epigram or greeting for Epaphroditios, praising his character.

EDCS-39101998.translation: Epaphroditus, worthy and blameless, lived for 30 years and 2 months.
EDCS-39101998.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Epaphroditus.

EDCS-39101999.translation: To the Chthonian Gods. Here lies Eunoe, slave of Korennis.
EDCS-39101999.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the Chthonian Gods, recording the burial of Eunoe.

EDCS-39102000.translation: Euphrosyne, worthy and blameless, lived for 40 years.
EDCS-39102000.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Euphrosyne.

EDCS-39102002.translation: Zosime, worthy and blameless, farewell! You lived for 14 years, five months, and one day.
EDCS-39102002.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Zosime.

EDCS-39102004.translation: Ioustos, worthy and blameless, lived for twenty years and thirty days.
EDCS-39102004.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Ioustos.

EDCS-39102005.summary: This is a dedication to Claudius Thy.

EDCS-39102006.translation: To the Chthonian Gods. Dexter, son of Pierosiphitus, lived for...
EDCS-39102006.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the Chthonian Gods, beginning the recording of the life of Dexter.

EDCS-39102007.translation: To the Chthonian Gods. Claudia Sotiris, worthy and blameless, lived for 63 years, 3 months, and 9 days.
EDCS-39102007.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the Chthonian Gods, recording the age at death of Claudia Sotiris.

EDCS-39102008.summary: This is a funerary epitaph with a greeting to Numerius Clodius Posaides.

EDCS-39102009.translation: Lucis Cornelis Onesimus, aged ... years and ... months.
EDCS-39102009.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Lucis Cornelis Onesimus, with lacunae for the exact numbers.

EDCS-39102010.translation: Crispina, good and blameless, lived for 15 years.
EDCS-39102010.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Crispina, recording her age at death.

EDCS-39102011.translation: Leosthenes, son of Lepidos, and Erasmius lived for 20 years, 4 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-39102011.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Leosthenes, son of Lepidos, and Erasmius, recording their ages and lifespan.

EDCS-39102012.translation: To Quintus Lollius, son of Ariston, from the family of Marchus.
EDCS-39102012.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Quintus Lollius, possibly from his family or a patron, identifying his father and a family connection.

EDCS-39102013.translation: Magoulia Spes, good and blameless, lived for 8 years.
EDCS-39102013.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Magoulia Spes, recording her age at death.

EDCS-39102014.translation: Mycenas the youth, ten years old.
EDCS-39102014.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Mycenas, a youth, stating his age at death.

EDCS-39102015.translation: To the Chthonian Gods: Netheris, child, farewell; it is fated to die.
EDCS-39102015.summary: This is a funerary invocation to the underworld deities for Netheris, a child, expressing acceptance of fate.

EDCS-39102016.translation: To the procession/spirit of Pompeius. Elpis, good and blameless, farewell; lived for 24 years, 4 months, and 4 days.
EDCS-39102016.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Elpis, dedicated to the spirit or procession of Pompeius, recording her age and lifespan.

EDCS-39102017.translation: Preousa, good and blameless, farewell; lived for 8 years.
EDCS-39102017.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Preousa, recording her age at death.

EDCS-39102019.translation: Ispes lived for 12 years and 4 months and 4 days.
EDCS-39102019.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Ispes, recording her age and lifespan.

EDCS-39102020.translation: To the Chthonian Gods. Sozousa, good and blameless, lived for 5 years, 4 months, and 7 days.
EDCS-39102020.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Sozousa, dedicated to the underworld deities, recording her age and lifespan.

EDCS-39102021.translation: Telesphorus, good, lived for 3 years and 1 month.
EDCS-39102021.summary: A funerary epigram recording the lifespan of Telesphorus, a child.

EDCS-39102022.translation: Philadelphus, good and blameless, lived for 35 years and 6 months.
EDCS-39102022.summary: A funerary epigram recording the lifespan of Philadelphus, likely an adult.

EDCS-39102024.translation: Furia, good and blameless, greetings, lived for 31 years.
EDCS-39102024.summary: A funerary epigram recording the lifespan of Furia.

EDCS-39102025.translation: Phretenesia Statia Scribonia lived blamelessly and with dignity for 33 years, 8 months, and 24 days.
EDCS-39102025.summary: A funerary epigram detailing the life of Phretenesia Statia Scribonia, including her virtues and lifespan.

EDCS-39102026.translation: Chrys is laid here, a small bit of dust.
EDCS-39102026.summary: A short funerary epitaph stating that Chrys is buried here.

EDCS-39102029.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Menophile, it was said by fate that you should die.
EDCS-39102029.summary: An invocation to the Chthonic Gods regarding the death of Menophile.

EDCS-39102036.translation: Agathe lies here, buried on the tenth day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-39102036.summary: A funerary inscription marking the burial of Agathe on a specific date.

EDCS-39102037.translation: Swiftly dying after the fate of Nicostratus, I lie here, alas, perishing from my master's sorrow. For I was his steward and my name was wretched Agathon. A tearful life. On the first day before the Nones of February, after the consulship of Honorius for the 8th time and Theodosius for the 3rd time, Augusti.
EDCS-39102037.summary: A funerary epigram of Agathon, a steward of Nicostratus, lamenting his own death and his master's grief, dated to the consulship of Honorius and Theodosius.

EDCS-39102038.translation: Here lies Agapius, servant of God, who died on the ninth day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-39102038.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Agapius, identified as a servant of God, on a specific date.

EDCS-39102040.translation: Adeudata lies here, who died on the tenth day of October.
EDCS-39102040.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Adeudata on October 10th.

EDCS-39102042.translation: Aigeia, who was laid to rest in God, died on the third day before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-39102042.summary: This inscription records the death of Aigeia, likely a funerary epitaph, dated by its reference to Roman Kalends.

EDCS-39102043.translation: Acubia, a maiden, lies here, having lived for ten years, nine months, and fifteen days.
EDCS-39102043.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Acubia, a maiden, detailing her age at death.

EDCS-39102046.summary: This inscription appears to be a brief funerary record, possibly naming Alexander and his father Athanasius, with a numerical value that might represent an age or a date.

EDCS-39102047.summary: This inscription simply marks a burial plot belonging to Anastaea.

EDCS-39102049.translation: Antoninus lies here, having lived thirty years. [---]
EDCS-39102049.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Antoninus, stating his age at death, with illegible text following.

EDCS-39102052.translation: God, remember your servant Auxanon the blessed Christian.
EDCS-39102052.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription requesting God's remembrance for the deceased servant Auxanon.

EDCS-39102053.translation: The burial place of Aphrodisias and Euprosounos; let no one else have power over it.
EDCS-39102053.summary: This inscription marks a burial plot for Aphrodisias and Euprosounos, intended to reserve the space exclusively for them.

EDCS-39102054.translation: Aphros died on the fifteenth day before the Kalends of August.
EDCS-39102054.summary: This inscription records the death of Aphros, dated by its reference to Roman Kalends.

EDCS-39102055.translation: Achilleus lies here, having lived an impeccable life; he died on the fourth day before the Ides of February.
EDCS-39102055.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Achilleus, praising his life and stating his death date based on Roman Kalends.

EDCS-39102056.translation: Achilleus lies here, having lived 18 years and 13 days; he died on the seventh day before the Ides of October, on the day of Cronos.
EDCS-39102056.summary: This funerary epitaph for Achilleus records his age at death and the date of his passing, referencing the day of Cronos (Saturn).

EDCS-39102057.translation: Viktor purchased a plot from Aerios.
EDCS-39102057.summary: This inscription records the purchase of a plot of land by Viktor.

EDCS-39102059.translation: Here lies Viktoría, who lived 4 years and died 5 days before the Kalends of July, in the 14th consulship of Theodosius.
EDCS-39102059.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Viktoría, detailing her age at death and the date of her demise within the consulship of Theodosius.

EDCS-39102060.translation: Beiktorinos lies here, son of Epiphanios.
EDCS-39102060.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying Beiktorinos as the son of Epiphanios.

EDCS-39102062.translation: Here lies Bonifatis, child of Eusebios the banker, who died 5 days before the Ides of November.
EDCS-39102062.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Bonifatis, child of Eusebios the banker.

EDCS-39102063.translation: Burial of Gemellus, son of Clodia.
EDCS-39102063.summary: This inscription records the burial of Gemellus, son of Clodia.

EDCS-39102066.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying the deceased as Dafros.

EDCS-39102068.summary: This inscription likely names two individuals, possibly for a joint tomb or dedication.

EDCS-39102069.translation: Diomedes died 20 days before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-39102069.summary: This inscription records the date of death for Diomedes.

EDCS-39102070.translation: Dionysius and Elpis purchased two plots from the church of Nikonas.
EDCS-39102070.summary: This inscription records the purchase of two plots by Dionysius and Elpis from the church of Nikonas.

EDCS-39102071.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying the deceased as Dionysis.

EDCS-39102072.translation: Here lies Dionysia, who died on the 25th of September, and here lies Onesibius.
EDCS-39102072.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Dionysia and Onesibius, with a precise date of death.

EDCS-39102074.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Dionysia.

EDCS-39102076.translation: The place of Eirene and Theodoulos.
EDCS-39102076.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Eirene and Theodoulos.

EDCS-39102078.summary: This inscription seems to commemorate Helene and her child Theodoros, possibly on a shared tomb.

EDCS-39102079.translation: Here lies Elpis, the virgin, on the 30th day of the month of April.
EDCS-39102079.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Elpis, a virgin, with a precise date of death.

EDCS-39102080.translation: Epaphros died in the month of December.
EDCS-39102080.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Epaphros, with the month of death specified.

EDCS-39102082.translation: The place of Evanthis and Zoilos.
EDCS-39102082.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Evanthis and Zoilos.

EDCS-39102083.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age at death of Eunomos.

EDCS-39102084.translation: The place of Eupra and Dokletionos.
EDCS-39102084.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Eupra and Dokletionos.

EDCS-39102085.translation: Eusebios of blessed memory rested on the 22nd of September.
EDCS-39102085.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eusebios, with a precise date of death and a reference to his memory.

EDCS-39102086.translation: Here lies Euterpe, companion of the Muses, who lived pure, devout, and blameless for 22 years and 3 months. She died on the fifth day before the Kalends of December, in the consulship of the ordinary magistrates, the 10th and the 3rd time.
EDCS-39102086.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Euterpe, recording her virtuous life and death with precise dating information including consuls and an indiction cycle.

EDCS-39102087.translation: The place of Eutychiane. Here lies.
EDCS-39102087.summary: A simple funerary marker indicating the burial place of Eutychiane.

EDCS-39102088.translation: Here lies Eutychus. In the month of May, from the 14th day of the Kalends, eleven years. May you have good fortune.
EDCS-39102088.summary: A funerary inscription for Eutychus, providing his death month and a wish for good fortune.

EDCS-39102090.translation: Tomb of Eutychion. Here lies Irena, his wife.
EDCS-39102090.summary: A tomb inscription indicating the burial place of Irena, the wife of Eutychion.

EDCS-39102091.translation: Zodorus from the village of Makres died in the month of December.
EDCS-39102091.summary: A funerary inscription stating the name, origin, and death month of Zodorus.

EDCS-39102092.translation: Theodosia lies here, having lived for 3 years and 3 days.
EDCS-39102092.summary: A funerary inscription for Theodosia, recording her young age at death.

EDCS-39102093.summary: A tomb inscription simply stating the burial place of Theodotos.

EDCS-39102094.summary: A funerary inscription marking the burial place of Theodoulos.

EDCS-39102095.summary: A funerary inscription marking the burial place of Theodoulos.

EDCS-39102097.translation: Here lies Theodorus the Christian, who lived for 2 years and 8 months, in the 14th indiction, on the 7th day before the Ides of April.
EDCS-39102097.summary: A funerary inscription for a Christian named Theodorus, detailing his age and death date within an indiction cycle.

EDCS-39102098.translation: Ulpius, the mother, bore Theoktiste to his loins; Ulpius, well-fated, well-fated Theoktiste.
EDCS-39102098.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commemorating Ulpius and Theoktiste, likely a mother and child.

EDCS-39102099.translation: Ioubinos lies here; he lived for 15 years, in the month of October, on the 17th day.
EDCS-39102099.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Ioubinos, who died on October 17th.

EDCS-39102100.summary: This is a simple tombstone marking the burial place of Ioulianos.

EDCS-39102101.summary: This is a simple tombstone marking the burial place of Iouliane.

EDCS-39102104.translation: He rested, Ioannes of blessed memory, on the 9th day before the Kalends of June, after the consulship of Theodosius for the 16th time and Faustus.
EDCS-39102104.summary: This epitaph commemorates Ioannes, noting his repose and dating his death to the consulship of Theodosius and Faustus.

EDCS-39102106.summary: This inscription names Klementein and Serakoussa, possibly indicating a family or individuals associated with a place.

EDCS-39102108.translation: Klodios Rhomanos lived for 19 years and 4 days, blameless in life; may his soul rejoice eternally.
EDCS-39102108.summary: This epitaph records the life and death of Klodios Rhomanos, who lived 19 years and 4 days and wished for eternal joy for his soul.

EDCS-39102109.translation: Concordia lies here; she died five days before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-39102109.summary: This tombstone records the death of Concordia, who died five days before the Kalends of February.

EDCS-39102111.translation: Koprianos lies here, having lived for 38 years; he died on the 11th day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-39102111.summary: This tombstone records the life and death of Koprianos, who died on the 11th day before the Kalends of October after living 38 years.

EDCS-39102113.translation: Kuriakos and Salvia, on this ground lies Kuriakos; he lived for three years, ten months, and five days, with his saints, his soul in the name of Jesus. Kuriake the fair-named also lived 3 years.
EDCS-39102113.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of Kuriakos, who died at age 3 years, 10 months, and 5 days, and Kuriake, who also lived 3 years, mentioning their souls in the name of Jesus.

EDCS-39102114.translation: On the Lord's day, bound by unbreakable pains, on the bed of which the very name is Kyriakē, on the Lord's day she received the release of her whole life, which she sought before the first day of the Kalends of May.
EDCS-39102114.summary: This epitaph records the release from suffering of a woman named Kyriakē, possibly on her deathbed, dated to before the Kalends of May.

EDCS-39102115.translation: The purchase of Vitalius. Here lies Kyriakē of good memory; she dies in the fourth month, March.
EDCS-39102115.summary: This epitaph records the death of Kyriakē in March, noting that her burial place was purchased by Vitalius.

EDCS-39102116.translation: Here lies Kostantía, twenty years old, on the twenty-fourth day of the month of February, the day of Helios; a pleasant place, having been bought brand new.
EDCS-39102116.summary: This epitaph records the death of Kostantía at the age of twenty in February, noting that the burial plot was newly purchased.

EDCS-39102117.translation: Here lies Kostantis, son of Bonifas.
EDCS-39102117.summary: This is a simple epitaph identifying Kostantis, son of Bonifas.

EDCS-39102118.summary: This is a simple epitaph identifying Koustatia.

EDCS-39102119.translation: Here lie Loukianos, and Nikopole, and Stephane.
EDCS-39102119.summary: This epitaph records the burial of three individuals: Loukianos, Nikopole, and Stephane.

EDCS-39102120.translation: Makari, may you live in God.
EDCS-39102120.summary: This is a hopeful posthumous address to Makari, wishing them eternal life in God.

EDCS-39102121.summary: This is a simple epitaph identifying Markella.

EDCS-39102122.translation: Christ. Marcellina lies here, having lived 50 years. Christ. She died on the fourth day before the Kalends.
EDCS-39102122.summary: This epitaph records the death of Marcellina at the age of 50, dated to the fourth day before the Kalends, with a Christian invocation.

EDCS-39102124.translation: Marcianos died in the month of October, in the third gate, fifth bench, at 37 years of age.
EDCS-39102124.summary: This epitaph records the death of Marcianos at age 37 in October, specifying his burial location within a cemetery or columbarium.

EDCS-39102125.translation: In God and Christ, Marturios lies here.
EDCS-39102125.summary: This is a short epitaph for Marturios, likely dating to the early Christian period.

EDCS-39102126.translation: Marcella fell asleep on the fifth day of the month of June; may the Lord remember your falling asleep.
EDCS-39102126.summary: This epitaph records the death of Marcella and invokes God's remembrance, dating to the Christian era.

EDCS-39102127.translation: Burial place of Nokerias Naseteidos, Pythas of two plots lies here.
EDCS-39102127.summary: This inscription marks a burial plot belonging to Nokerias Naseteidos and Pythas.

EDCS-39102128.translation: Onesime, of good memory, a Christian, lies here, having departed to the Lord on the Kalends of January.
EDCS-39102128.summary: This epitaph commemorates Onesime, identifying her as a Christian who died on January 1st.

EDCS-39102130.translation: Urbica, daughter of Hesperianos, lies here, having lived months.
EDCS-39102130.summary: This is an incomplete epitaph for Urbica, daughter of Hesperianos, recording her lifespan in months.

EDCS-39102131.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly related to an official named Ursanus from Augusta.

EDCS-39102132.translation: May God remember your servant Phaschasius; rest on the 20th day of the month of October.
EDCS-39102132.summary: This epitaph requests God's remembrance for Phaschasius and records his repose on October 20th.

EDCS-39102133.translation: Paula lies here, having lived 25 years, and departed this life 8 days before the Kalends of December, under the consulship of Gerius and Ardabur, the most illustrious.
EDCS-39102133.summary: This epitaph records the death of Paula at age 25, specifying the date and consuls of 427 AD.

EDCS-39102134.translation: Son of Paulus, having lived eight years, rested.
EDCS-39102134.summary: This is a brief epitaph for the son of Paulus, who died at the age of eight.

EDCS-39102136.summary: A very brief epitaph marking the burial place of Petrus.

EDCS-39102137.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Pronikis and Kyriakos are buried here.

EDCS-39102138.translation: Rufina lies here, and Zois, daughter of the agorastes.
EDCS-39102138.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Rufina and Zois, daughter of an agorastes (market official).

EDCS-39102139.translation: In the month of November, Rhodon died, 9 days before the Kalends of December, on the day of the Sun.
EDCS-39102139.summary: This is a funerary epigram recording the death of Rhodon in November, noting the specific date and day of the week.

EDCS-39102140.translation: Tomb of Sabinus; within it lies Auxanos, his son, who lived for 25 years.
EDCS-39102140.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Auxanos, son of Sabinus, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-39102141.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to be a name, possibly funerary or dedicatory.

EDCS-39102142.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription stating that Statilia is buried here.

EDCS-39102143.translation: Stibadion died, 12 days before the Kalends of September, at the age of 40 years. NN
EDCS-39102143.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Stibadion at the age of 40 in August.

EDCS-39102144.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Sympotis is buried here.

EDCS-39102145.translation: Syntrophos lived for 49 years, 7 months, and 2 days.
EDCS-39102145.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Syntrophos.

EDCS-39102146.translation: In the time of the late Siracousios, agorastes, and Abundantia.
EDCS-39102146.summary: This inscription seems to record a date based on the mention of a deceased official, Siracousios, and Abundantia.

EDCS-39102147.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Soteria at the age of 30.

EDCS-39102149.translation: Tertulla, deacon of the candelabra, died...
EDCS-39102149.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Tertulla, identified as a deacon of the candelabra, likely from a Christian context.

EDCS-39102150.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph stating that Timothea lies here.

EDCS-39102151.translation: Timothes lies here, and his wife Irena, and also Krisconia, their beloved, who lived more or less twenty years.
EDCS-39102151.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Timothes, his wife Irena, and their daughter Krisconia, who lived approximately twenty years.

EDCS-39102152.summary: This is a funerary epitaph with a repetition of the name Tyche, possibly emphasizing fate or the deceased's name.

EDCS-39102153.translation: He brought water here for eternal remembrance, for the prayers of his masters, Hyginus, also called Gennadis.
EDCS-39102153.summary: This inscription records that Hyginus (also known as Gennadis) provided water for a permanent memorial, fulfilling the prayers of his masters.

EDCS-39102154.translation: In the month of October, from the Calends, the 7th day, Hygia died, remembering.
EDCS-39102154.summary: This inscription records the death of Hygia in October, specifying the date relative to the Calends, and suggests she was mindful or remembered.

EDCS-39102155.translation: Pribatus lived thirty-eight years, four months, and fourteen days. Be cheerful, soul.
EDCS-39102155.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Pribatus as 38 years, 4 months, and 14 days, with a concluding exhortation to be cheerful.

EDCS-39102156.summary: This is a fragment likely referring to Felicissimus, possibly as the owner or dedicatee of a monument.

EDCS-39102158.translation: Fortunata lived 20 years and died on the fifth day before the Calends of February. She lies here.
EDCS-39102158.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Fortunata died at the age of 20 on January 28th and is buried here.

EDCS-39102159.summary: This is a grave marker identifying the deceased as Furtonatus.

EDCS-39102161.translation: Here lie Photine and Philoumene, blessed virgins, chaste and holy virgins. The younger lived 25 years and the younger 34 years. I adjure you by God Almighty never to disturb them.
EDCS-39102161.summary: This epitaph records the death of two virgins, Photine and Philoumene, with their ages, and includes an imprecation against desecration.

EDCS-39102162.translation: Chresimus rested, having lived 50 years, 7 months, and 22 days, nine days before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-39102162.summary: This epitaph records the death of Chresimus, specifying his age and the date of his passing.

EDCS-39102163.translation: May God remember your servant Chrysis and grant her a place of light, a place of refreshment in the bosom of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. The blessed memory rested on the day before the Kalends of May. [Restored Greek abbreviations for date/period].
EDCS-39102163.summary: This epitaph is a prayer for Chrysis, requesting divine remembrance and a place in Abraham's bosom, indicating a Christian context and the date of rest.

EDCS-39102164.summary: This is a simple epitaph marking the burial place of Chrysis and Constantia.

EDCS-39102166.translation: Ilix, noble, sensible, glorious, fills the tomb with her body; Alesche, dear of heart.
EDCS-39102166.summary: This is a poetic epitaph for Ilix, describing her virtues and possibly her relationship to the writer or Alesche.

EDCS-39102168.translation: Paullina lies here, having lived 30 years, dying on the 13th of August. The place was bought by her mother, Mar[tia?]. [Partially restored name of mother and possibly a provenance].
EDCS-39102168.summary: This epitaph records the death of Paullina at age 30, noting the date of death and that her mother purchased the burial plot.

EDCS-39102170.translation: Having lived twenty years, she died, a Christian, pure, a recluse.
EDCS-39102170.summary: This epitaph describes a deceased woman as a Christian, pure, and a recluse, indicating her virtuous life and age at death.

EDCS-39102176.translation: Felicissimus, good and blameless, lived [unknown number of] years.
EDCS-39102176.summary: This epitaph describes Felicissimus as good and blameless and records his age at death, though the age itself is missing.

EDCS-39102178.translation: In the time of Aristodamus, son of Sosibius, Nymphon Hieros, remembering the holy goddesses.
EDCS-39102178.summary: This inscription records a dedication or a memorial made by Nymphon Hieros during the time of Aristodamus, son of Sosibius, to holy goddesses.

EDCS-39102182.translation: Under Herakleios son of Nymphodoros, the patrons of Hera and Aphrodite, Aristogeitos, Artamon, Pausanias, Dionysodoros, Krithon son of Philistion, Philistion, Sosios, Kleon son of Nymphodoros (scribe), Philistion.
EDCS-39102182.summary: This inscription lists the names of officials, including a scribe, who were patrons of the goddesses Hera and Aphrodite under the presidency of Herakleios son of Nymphodoros.

EDCS-39102183.translation: Under Herakleios son of Poseidios, for Aphrodite, the market supervisors Herakleidas son of Apollodoros and Herakleios son of Agathon, the triakadecharchoi Aristomachos son of Dionysodoros, Damainetos son of Lykon, Xenon, Aristeas, Nikasion, Philistos, Daikrates son of Aristoxenos, Nikasion son of Artamon, Euphraios son of Theophilos (scribe), Pausanias, Sosios, the scribes Hybrimos son of Sosios, Philistion son of Sosios.
EDCS-39102183.summary: This inscription records the names of market supervisors and other officials, including scribes, serving under Herakleios son of Poseidios for the goddess Aphrodite.

EDCS-39102184.translation: Under Herakleios son of Poseidios, the archons Epitos, Archagathos, Herakleidas, Agatharchos, Damokles son of Artamon, Phinton, Dionysios, Sosis son of Herakleidas, Diodoros son of Diodoros, Damokles son of Damatrios, Orthos.
EDCS-39102184.summary: This inscription lists the archons serving under Herakleios son of Poseidios.

EDCS-39102185.translation: Under Philokrates, for Aphrodite, the market supervisors Dionysodoros son of Zopyros and Euphraios son of Kallikrates, the triakadecharchoi Damokrates son of Aristokrates, Hageas son of Hageas, Apollonios son of Agathon, Heloris son of Apollonios, Aristophanes son of Archagathos, Herakleidas son of Damagetos, Aristeas son of Xenon, Kriton son of Kriton, Epigonos son of Epigonos (scribe and phradates), Pyrrichos son of Aristogeitos (scribe), Damokrates son of Philios (under-scribe), Aristomenes son of Zopyros (attendant), Menekrates son of Sosios (herald).
EDCS-39102185.summary: This inscription lists various officials, including market supervisors, triakadecharchoi, scribes, and a herald, serving under Philokrates for Aphrodite.

EDCS-39102186.translation: Under Aphrodite, the market supervisors Apollodoros son of Nymphodoros and Herakleios son of Herakleios, the triakadecharchoi Philistion son of Nikandros, Nymphon son of Lysias, Dionysios son of Hippon, Nikon son of Nikon, Heloris daughter of Herakleidas, Theodoros son of Euphraios, Eukles son of Philarchos, Aristomachos, Nikon son of Nymphodoros (scribe and phradates), Archagathos son of Archagathos (under-scribe), Sosis son of Pausanias, Antikrates son of Artamon (herald), Euphraios son of Euphraios (attendant).
EDCS-39102186.summary: This inscription lists market supervisors, triakadecharchoi, scribes, a herald, and attendants serving in honor of Aphrodite.

EDCS-39102188.translation: Philippos son of Aristonidas, Philonidas, Archonidas, Herakleidas, Aristokles, Polyarchos.
EDCS-39102188.summary: This inscription is a list of names, possibly officials or members of a group, with patronymics, the beginning of which is lost.

EDCS-39102189.translation: Of Hieron son of king Hieron of the Akraioi.
EDCS-39102189.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or ownership marker, possibly related to a king named Hieron and the people of Akraioi.

EDCS-39102191.translation: To Theodoros son of Sa[--] a place under the Aphrodision; to Philonidai son of Philonidas; to Morphianon a place under the Koreion; to Dion son of Theodoros a place above the Koreion; to Theodoros son of Dion a place above the Koreion; to Artemidoros son of Herakleios a place behind the Koreion; to Zopyros son of Artemidoros a place behind the Koreion; to Artemidoros son of Polyxenos a place in the balsamos near the Artemision; to Philokles son of Philon a place near the phratrion; to Poseidei son of Philon a place near the phratrion; to Simylos son of Eukles; to Netinon a place above the Koreion; to Aristogeiton son of Pausanias a place under the Aphrodision; to Herakleidai son of Herakleidas a place under the thalamoi near the house; to Damokratidai son of Philios a place near the Artemision; to Aischylos son of Dionysios a place above the Koreion; to Philoni son of Philistos a place above the Koreion; to Philistoi son of Philon a place above the Koreion; to [---] son of Philokrates a place near the laundry; to Archedamos son of Philon a place in Petikela; to Hieron son of Philon a place in Petikela; to Archedamos twice near the Lamias breasts; to Herakleios son of Dionysios and Kraserinos a place in two Kakkariroi; to Zopyros son of Epixenos a place under the Selinuntian gate; to Aristodamos son of Sosios a place near the Lamias breasts; to Apollonios son of Archagathos a place under the Selinuntian gate.
EDCS-39102191.summary: This long and complex inscription lists numerous designated 'places' (θέμα) in relation to various landmarks, deities (Aphrodite, Koreion, Artemision, Lamia), and individuals, likely marking property boundaries or allocations, possibly from an administrative or religious context.

EDCS-39102197.summary: This is a short funerary or greeting epigram addressed to Eumache son of Zopyros.

EDCS-39102198.summary: This is a short inscription honoring Zopyros as a 'good hero', likely a funerary dedication or a cultic marker.

EDCS-39102199.summary: A funerary greeting to Zotikē Genukia.

EDCS-39102201.summary: A funerary greeting to the blameless Kouinte.

EDCS-39102210.translation: Alphios Klōdis died before the eighth day of November, on the day of Helios, son of Lucius and Keikeilia, who lie here. Entering the gate on the right, having held the second office and having served as ambassador to the king and [with] three companions, he lived for 31 years, 10 months, and 21 days.
EDCS-39102210.summary: A funerary inscription for Alphios Klōdis, recording his death date, parentage, civic offices, and lifespan, likely from the Roman period given the mix of Greek and Roman names.

EDCS-39102211.translation: Eutyches lies here, having lived blamelessly for 26 years, and departs to the land of the righteous on the day before the first day of January.
EDCS-39102211.summary: A funerary inscription for Eutyches, stating his lifespan and date of departure to the afterlife, placing him in the Christian era.

EDCS-39102212.translation: Here lies someone by the name of Klodianos, who dies in the month of February on the Kalends; let no one disturb him.
EDCS-39102212.summary: A funerary epitaph for Klodianos, dated to February and including a prohibition against disturbance.

EDCS-39102213.translation: Marcianē lies here, venerable and blameless, who departed from this world to the Lord at the age of 11 years, leaving her soul on the ninth day before the Kalends of January. But you, friend, do not desecrate my tomb, do not shine light on me. But if you wish, show me the light; may God give you the light of fish.
EDCS-39102213.summary: A Christian funerary inscription for Marcianē, detailing her age at death and date, with a plea to visitors and a reference to the Christian symbol of the fish.

EDCS-39102214.translation: Steganos, a deacon of blessed memory, rests here, having found repose on the 14th day of the month of June in the consulship of Monaxius and Plinthus.
EDCS-39102214.summary: A funerary inscription for Steganos, a deacon, mentioning his repose date and consular dating, placing it in the 5th or 6th century CE.

EDCS-39102215.translation: Under the gymnasiarchs Aristion, son of Agathos, and Philistion, son of Epikrates, the youths of Hierōnei.
EDCS-39102215.summary: A dedication or record associated with the gymnasium, listing the gymnasiarchs and the youths of Hierōnei, likely from the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102216.translation: Euryn̄das Agath[---] Centorripinus constructed the fountain of Eleuthera.
EDCS-39102216.summary: A dedication recording the construction of a fountain named 'Eleuthera' by Euryn̄das Agath[---] Centorripinus.

EDCS-39102217.translation: To Atelia Domnina, for her most sweet husband Iulius Paulus, in memory, and for Paulla their daughter, who lived blamelessly in God for 11 years and 10 days.
EDCS-39102217.summary: A funerary dedication by Atelia Domnina to her husband Iulius Paulus and their daughter Paulla, mentioning the daughter's age and her piety in God.

EDCS-39102219.translation: Antōnius Eupraktos lies here; he died in the month of June, 25 days before the Kalends.
EDCS-39102219.summary: This inscription records the death of Antōnius Eupraktos in June, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-39102220.translation: Antōnius Satronilos lies here; he reposed in the month of December, 7 days before the Kalends. Antōnia Eutychia lies here; she reposed.
EDCS-39102220.summary: This inscription records the deaths of Antōnius Satronilos and Antōnia Eutychia in December, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-39102221.translation: In the consulship of Honorius for the 4th time and Eutychianus, Euskios died in the month of January, 11 days before the Kalends.
EDCS-39102221.summary: This inscription records the death of Euskios in January during the 4th consulship of Honorius and the consulship of Eutychianus.

EDCS-39102224.translation: Cornelia reposed in the month of October, when it was 9 days before the Kalends, on the day of Jupiter.
EDCS-39102224.summary: This inscription records the death of Cornelia in October on a day associated with Jupiter, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-39102225.translation: Sosius Bychchylos, deacon, lies here; he rested in the month of November, 3 days before the Kalends. Antōnia Euprosynē rested on the day before the 11th Kalends of December.
EDCS-39102225.summary: This inscription records the rest of Sosius Bychchylos, a deacon, and Antōnia Euprosynē in November/December, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-39102226.translation: Hyginos reposed in the month of November, when it was 21 days before the Kalends, on the day of Hermes.
EDCS-39102226.summary: This inscription records the death of Hyginos in November on a day associated with Hermes, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-39102227.translation: On the day eight days before the Kalends of January, on the day of Selene, SYN RIOIMANIKOSIIME died.
EDCS-39102227.summary: This inscription records the death of SYN RIOIMANIKOSIIME on a day before the Kalends of January, associated with Selene.

EDCS-39102228.translation: NIGEN died ten days before the Kalends of Februarius, on the day RENIIIOIS. May the lord remember her.
EDCS-39102228.summary: This inscription records the death of NIGEN ten days before the Kalends of Februarius, and includes a plea for remembrance.

EDCS-39102229.translation: Lanthanoūsa and Agathē, good and blameless, lived 52 years, 4 months, and 3 days. Her daughter lovingly honored her mother, who was dutiful. If anyone wronged her, he also wronged me; may he not escape God's notice.
EDCS-39102229.summary: This epitaph commemorates Lanthanoūsa and Agathē, detailing their lifespan and including a curse against anyone who wronged the deceased.

EDCS-39102230.summary: This inscription records the short lifespan of Taurōpē, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-39102231.translation: Trygete lived 30 years and 8 months, well and blamelessly.
EDCS-39102231.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Trygete, who lived a virtuous life.

EDCS-39102232.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Tryphon, identified as the elder.

EDCS-39102233.translation: During the tenure of priest Aristion, son of Istatos, the council of Sosios, son of Nymphodoros, in the second half-year of Karneios, on the thirtieth day, under the presidency of Hippocles, son of Hippocles, it was decreed by the assembly, as it was also by the council, that because the elected gymnasium director for the past year, Herakleidas, son of Zopyros, had taken care of the ephebes, the younger men, and all others who used the gymnasium, and had managed all other matters in the gymnasium excellently, acting worthily of himself and the virtue of his ancestors, it is therefore fitting to honor with an olive wreath in the assembly the gymnasium director Herakleidas, son of Zopyros, for his care and diligence in the gymnasium. This decree, once inscribed, is to be set up on a stele in the gymnasium. For the construction of the stele, the sum of thirty drachmas is to be expended, as the council shall direct. The people of Gelos praise and crown with an olive wreath the gymnasium director Herakleidas, son of Zopyros, for his care and diligence in the gymnasium. The crowned ephebes: Asklapiadas, son of Asklapiadas; Artemon, son of Euthymos; Antallos, son of Antallos; Polyxenos, son of Agatharchos; Protarchos, son of Protarchos; Gelios, son of Gorgidas; Aristion, son of Nymphodoros; Sosipolis, son of Isidotos; Nikarchos, son of Pyrrhommios; Apollonios, son of Satyros; Zopyros, son of Herakleidas.
EDCS-39102233.summary: This is a decree from the people of Gelos honouring Herakleidas, son of Zopyros, with an olive wreath for his excellent service as gymnasium director. It details the proceedings of the council and assembly and lists the names of the ephebes who were crowned.

EDCS-39102241.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Theano lived 19 years, 2 months, and 12 days. Sabina, her mother, to her most pure and dearest daughter.
EDCS-39102241.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Theano, set up by her mother Sabina, who died at a young age.

EDCS-39102247.translation: Anaxilas, son of Xenon, when he was archon of Gela, dedicated this.
EDCS-39102247.summary: This is a dedication by Anaxilas, son of Xenon, who served as an archon in Gela.

EDCS-39102248.translation: To Arkesos, son of Aischylos, for Hera, a votive offering.
EDCS-39102248.summary: This is a votive offering to the goddess Hera, made by Arkesos, son of Aischylos.

EDCS-39102249.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Aristodamos, son of Aristios.

EDCS-39102250.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Marcia, slave of Caesar, lived 35 years. Mystis, to her own daughter, in memory.
EDCS-39102250.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcia, a slave of Caesar, set up by her daughter Mystis.

EDCS-39102259.translation: During the tenure of treasurer Lucius Caecilius, son of Lucius Metellus, and Pasion, son of Dekkios Seisyriōn, Egestaios, who served as chiliarch.
EDCS-39102259.summary: This inscription records the names of officials, including a treasurer and a chiliarch, likely during a specific period or event.

EDCS-39102260.translation: [Gaius Asinn]ius Nicomachus [Julianus], most distinguished, consul, proconsul of Asia, most just. Asinn]ius Amiantos, procurator, [to his master].
EDCS-39102260.summary: This is an epitaph for Gaius Asinn]ius Nicomachus Julianus, a distinguished former consul and proconsul of Asia, dedicated by his procurator, Asinn]ius Amiantos.

EDCS-39102264.translation: Diodorus, son of Tittelos of Appira, (dedicated this) for his sister Minyra, priestess of Aphrodite Urania.
EDCS-39102264.summary: A dedication by Diodorus for his sister Minyra, a priestess of Aphrodite Urania, likely from the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102265.translation: The people of Egesta (honored) Phalacros, son of Diodorus of Erythrai, for his courage.
EDCS-39102265.summary: A public award by the people of Egesta to Phalacros for his bravery, probably dating to the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102268.translation: During the priesthood of Phaon, son of Nymphon Sopalianos, and the agoranomia of Xenarchos, son of Diodorus, and the supervision by him of the works of the wooden structure which was constructed.
EDCS-39102268.summary: An inscription detailing civic and religious offices held by Phaon and Xenarchos, relating to the construction of a wooden structure, likely from the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102269.translation: The hieromnamon Tittelos, son of Artemidoros, undertook the supervision of the works of the andron and the proedria with the hierophylakes.
EDCS-39102269.summary: A record of Tittelos, a hieromnamon, overseeing the construction and furnishing of a men's dining hall (andron) and a presidency (proedria), probably from the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102273.translation: Cleagoras of Massalia dedicated a table to Aphrodite.
EDCS-39102273.summary: A dedication of a table to the goddess Aphrodite by Cleagoras of Massalia, likely dating to the Hellenistic or Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102274.summary: A personal greeting inscribed, possibly on a tombstone or as a dedication, to Epaphrodite from Theodota, a Celt, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102275.translation: Ignatius Marianos, good and without fault, lived 50 years, 7 days.
EDCS-39102275.summary: An epitaph recording the lifespan of Ignatius Marianos, indicating he lived a virtuous life, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102276.translation: Kissos and Tryphon, sons of Ikadios, excellent and untimely (died), greetings.
EDCS-39102276.summary: An epitaph for two brothers, Kissos and Tryphon, sons of Ikadios, who died prematurely, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102277.summary: An epitaph recording the lifespan of Titus Nasidis Basileides, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102279.translation: Regulus Bersim... to his dearest son
EDCS-39102279.summary: A dedicatory or funerary inscription fragment, likely for a beloved son, mentioning Regulus and Bersim, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39102284.translation: Bettius Mauricus lived 25 years and his own life lies in this tomb.
EDCS-39102284.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Bettius Mauricus, indicating his age at death.

EDCS-39102286.translation: Iulius Iustus lived 30 years and survived.
EDCS-39102286.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph for Iulius Iustus, indicating his age at death.

EDCS-39102287.translation: Three companies of infantry who served under Apollonius and his ephebes honored Antallos, son of Antallos of Ornicha, who served as gymnasiarch, out of goodwill.
EDCS-39102287.summary: This is a dedication honoring Antallos, son of Antallos of Ornicha, for his service as gymnasiarch, made by soldiers and ephebes under Apollonius.

EDCS-39102289.translation: Dorotheos, Lysias, Antallos, son of Nymphon, having served as agoranomoi, dedicated [this] to Aphrodite from the people; the scribe was Apollodoros, son of Phalacros.
EDCS-39102289.summary: This is a dedication to Aphrodite by former agoranomoi, with Apollodoros serving as scribe.

EDCS-39102292.translation: Aristodamos of Neminis, son of Persios, erected [this] for his parents and their benefactor Aristodamos, and for his wife and himself.
EDCS-39102292.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Aristodamos for his parents, a benefactor, his wife, and himself.

EDCS-39102295.translation: Farewell, good Dekomanes, and you too, whoever you are, do not grieve me by touching.
EDCS-39102295.summary: This is an epitaph, a farewell addressed to the deceased Dekomanes and any passerby.

EDCS-39102296.summary: This is a tombstone epitaph wishing farewell to Demosthenes, son of Archeboulos.

EDCS-39102298.translation: Ioustos, son of Nigros, lived 25 years.

EDCS-41300019.translation: Here lies Kale, elderly, who lived 50 years blamelessly. She departs life on the 19th day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-41300019.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Kale, recording her age at death and the date of her passing.

EDCS-41300020.translation: Pasiada made this tomb for him/her.

EDCS-41300030.translation: Iason, son of Artafas, lived blamelessly for 100 years. Theodora lived blamelessly for 60 years.

EDCS-41300090.translation: Quintus Cornificius, Stephanephoros, good and blameless, greetings. He lived 12 years and 9 months.
EDCS-41300090.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Quintus Cornificius, who died at the age of 12 years and 9 months.

EDCS-41300092.translation: Here lies Kallityche of blessed memory. She lived 88 years and died on the day before the Kalends of February, a Saturday.
EDCS-41300092.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Kallityche, who died at the age of 88 on a Saturday before February 1st.

EDCS-41400005.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Kalimera.

EDCS-41400006.translation: Lanthánousa, also called Agathe, good and blameless, lived 72 years and 4 months and 3 days. And her daughter, devoted to her mother, made this. If anyone wronged her, he also grieved me; may he not escape the notice of the god.
EDCS-41400006.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Lanthánousa (Agathe), made by her devoted daughter, with a curse against anyone who wronged her.

EDCS-41400015.summary: This is a funerary epigram recording the death of two children, Potetas and Zoe Helena, with their ages and the month of death.

EDCS-43400793.translation: Lurritanus lies here in peace, who lived 8 years, 10 months, and 8 days, buried on the 16th day before the Kalends of November during the reign of our lords Honorius (consul for the 12th time) and Theodosius (consul for the 8th time), Augusti.
EDCS-43400793.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription recording the burial date and lifespan of Lurritanus, likely from the early 5th century CE.

EDCS-43400833.translation: Bibi sleeps here on the Kalends of September.
EDCS-43400833.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription recording the burial date of Bibi.

EDCS-43400834.translation: Here rests in peace the infant Bonipatia, who lived in the world approximately 11 years, departed on the Kalends of November during the consulship of our lord Theodosius Augustus.
EDCS-43400834.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription recording the burial date and lifespan of an infant named Bonipatia, likely from the 5th century CE.

EDCS-43400835.translation: Giddonis buried on the 7th day before the Kalends of December.
EDCS-43400835.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription recording the burial date of Giddonis.

EDCS-43400836.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription naming the deceased, Victoria.

EDCS-46600165.translation: In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, Cresconius built this holy work of Christ.

EDCS-52900048.translation: Dionisius the presbyter lived 55 years in peace.

EDCS-55500857.summary: This inscription appears to be an incomplete dedication or memorial for Gaius Marius Eupo.

EDCS-55702035.translation: Callisthenes of Olynthus was Alexander's epistolary scribe, from whom he writes down deeds, explaining the laws, about the Elian S L G. Quintus Fabius Pictorinus, called Romanus, son of Gaius, who recorded the arrival of Hercules in Italy and the return of Aeneas, ally of Lanovius, and Ascanius. Much later, Romulus and Remus were born, and the founding of Rome by Romulus, who first reigned. Philistus of Syracuse is said to have been a student of this Evenus the wise, who wrote the elegies, first to set up the star that is now prosperous, and to divide the people equally. And formerly it was customary to attack tyrants, and then
EDCS-55702035.summary: This inscription is a historical and literary text, likely a biographical or historical summary mentioning Callisthenes, Quintus Fabius Pictorinus, and the founding of Rome, possibly from a later period but referencing classical figures.

EDCS-55702038.translation: A vow of Pancharius and Ioannes, brothers.
EDCS-55702038.summary: This inscription records a votive offering or dedication made by two brothers, Pancharius and Ioannes.

EDCS-56500187.translation: Gaius Sestius, son of Publius, and Quintus Salonius, son of Quintus, sexviri quaestores, approved the enbolum.
EDCS-56500187.summary: This inscription records the approval of the 'enbolum' by two officials, Gaius Sestius and Quintus Salonius, who held the office of sexviri quaestores.

EDCS-57100026.translation: Marcus Roscius, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, dedicated this to Manlia, daughter of Titus, wife of Marcus Roscius, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, from his own funds.
EDCS-57100026.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Marcus Roscius to his wife, Manlia, funded by himself.

EDCS-57100042.translation: Memorial of Ianuarius, our lord Gentionis.
EDCS-57100042.summary: This inscription is a memorial for Ianuarius, likely a tomb inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-57100043.translation: To the departed spirits. Allina, his wife, made this for her well-deserving husband, Titus Flavius Ganamalus, son of Ganamion.
EDCS-57100043.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife to her husband, Titus Flavius Ganamalus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59500343.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Valeria, likely a funerary epitaph from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59500344.translation: To Marcus Junius Felix, council member of Lilybaeum, who, out of generosity towards his fatherland, the community of the Kinakoi, from his own funds, [honored?] a worthy patron.
EDCS-59500344.summary: This bilingual (Greek and Latin) inscription honors Marcus Junius Felix, a council member of Lilybaeum, for his benefactions to his city, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59700188.translation: Achilles, son of Prothymos, greetings, and [farewell] even in the underworld.
EDCS-59700188.summary: This is a funerary inscription with a Greek greeting and farewell, likely from the Roman Imperial period.
