EDCS-59700193.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or a dedication related to Cassius, son of Aulus Annius Donatus, possibly on a tomb or a public monument, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59700194.summary: This inscription is a genitive dedication, likely for Aulus Annius Erotis.

EDCS-60200096.summary: This inscription is a genitive phrase, identifying someone as the son of Vel Sceva.

EDCS-60800058.translation: T. B. Dipil, son of Salvius
EDCS-60800058.summary: This inscription provides a name and filiation, possibly for a funerary monument.

EDCS-60800060.translation: Of Arcia, wife of Aulus Sestius
EDCS-60800060.summary: This inscription, in the genitive, likely refers to Arcia, wife of Aulus Sestius, possibly on a monument.

EDCS-60800064.translation: Prosdecte, pious, hail! She lived 18 years.
EDCS-60800064.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Prosdecte, recording her piety and lifespan of 18 years.

EDCS-60800080.translation: In office as amphipolus in Syracuse, Aprus Sextius Augureinus, and as priestess beside the Paides and Anna, daughter of Maiorus, of Cecilia Artemitis. Lucius Cornelius Aquila dedicated this votive offering for himself, for his mother Poilla Cornelia, and for his own wife Mustia Volumnilla, to Apollo, Paides, and Anna.
EDCS-60800080.summary: This is a complex dedication from Syracuse, made by Lucius Cornelius Aquila for his family, to Apollo, Paides, and Anna, mentioning local officials and priests, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-61300082.translation: Prisca, Prisca, Prisca, Prisca, Prisca, Prisca
EDCS-61300082.summary: This inscription repeats the name Prisca multiple times, possibly for emphasis in a funerary context or as a magical invocation.

EDCS-61300083.summary: This inscription is a dedication in the dative case, likely on a monument for Ehemeros and his relatives.

EDCS-61400197.summary: This inscription records the name Sextus Alfius Arion, likely on a funerary monument.

EDCS-61500395.summary: This inscription greets two pious women, Orthris and Epiteuxis, likely on a shared funerary monument.

EDCS-61500396.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Cia Tais lived 27 years. Vitalis [her] mother lived 1 year.
EDCS-61500396.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cia Tais, set up by her mother Vitalis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61500397.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Arrius Eventus made [this] for Elpidius and his own [family].
EDCS-61500397.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Arrius Eventus for Elpidius and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61700691.summary: This is a sepulchral greeting to Lucius Arrius Dionysus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61700692.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the name and filiation of Marcus Coelius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61700693.translation: For Lucius GAVIUS Philargyrus, freedman of Lucius, and Gavia Prima, freedwoman of Lucius. 6 feet in front, 6 feet in the ground.
EDCS-61700693.summary: This funerary inscription specifies the dimensions of a burial plot for Lucius Gavius Philargyrus and Gavia Prima, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61700694.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Volusius Hymnus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61700695.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Volusius Hymnus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61700696.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Volusius Hymnus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-61700697.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Volusius Hymnus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-62900290.summary: This is a sepulchral greeting to Decimus Hirtilianus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-62900291.summary: A personal greeting inscribed on an unknown object, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-62900294.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. For Pumidia Natali, dearest nurse.
EDCS-62900294.summary: A funerary dedication for a nurse named Pumidia Natali, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64300052.summary: The names Secunda and Oecumenia, possibly a dedication or identification, inscribed on an unspecified object, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400030.translation: For the health and perpetuity of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, and of his entire household, and of the Roman people, and of the sacred senate. Syrion, son of Demi.
EDCS-64400030.summary: A dedication for the well-being of Emperor Antoninus Pius and the Roman state by Syrion, son of Demi, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400034.translation: Kalpurnianos the lover of music, the same hieromnemones, a thank-offering.
EDCS-64400034.summary: A votive inscription by hieromnemones (temple officials) associated with someone named Kalpurnianos, indicating a thank-offering, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400278.translation: Mykon, son of Damios of Kobetos, requested that Anthokritos guarantee Apellis, but I said I was not, he was deceived. Leon the foreigner, Mykon, were sitting in the square, having silver, he came, he shouted for honor, he did not buy it, but he set it up for himself before the time of Mnasimachos, son of Empedocles, to guarantee.
EDCS-64400278.summary: A complex and partly obscure inscription possibly relating to a legal dispute or a failed business deal involving guarantees and money, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400279.translation: Vow of Apellas concerning the affection of Eunike, may nothing be more urgent than Eunike, nor Phinton, but praise him willingly and unwillingly. And Philetan, concerning the affection of Eunike, register all the choragi for immunity and for their work and their deeds, and their children from ten years old, and their fathers, and let them be ineffective and in contests and outside contests, those who leave me behind. Kaldeia register from Apellis and all those who will have them for the middle. Sosia register from the shop. Alkidades, concerning the affection of Anthios, Pyrrias, Myskelos, Damophantos, and the one who registers from the boy and the father and all the others who come here. Let nothing be more urgent for Eunike, neither among men nor women. So much desire, they also drew Diotima. To Eunike, always victory, completely, and the one who registers.
EDCS-64400279.summary: A lengthy and complex inscription detailing a vow related to affection and granting privileges (immunity from taxes and duties) to various individuals, possibly connected to a festival or public office, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400285.summary: A short inscription expressing admiration for Theognis, invoking Zeus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400286.translation: I am Latin, I am from Reginos.
EDCS-64400286.summary: A simple statement of identity, identifying the speaker as both Latin and from a place or person named Reginos, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400287.translation: Alas for Selini, daughter of Philinus!
EDCS-64400287.summary: An expression of lament or grief for Selini, daughter of Philinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64400323.translation: God Tyche and Archon owe to the Goddess TT talents with their interest, principal 14,000, drachmas TH T HT HT talents. These are not for mortals but for purchase. Statues and vessels are to be purchased with this money. And the sister of Archon owes, and her own property, and Archon's property with this money, and the children owe Cyprus. Saiso, free themselves and their children, because Archon the debtor will repay this money. Then they will be free.
EDCS-64400323.summary: This is a fragment of a legal or financial document detailing a debt owed to a goddess, likely concerning the purchase of religious items and the emancipation of individuals.

EDCS-64400324.translation: Sime, wife of Pratomaques, son of Leptinas, of Praxias, son of Raturos, Kukuies, Araotes, a pyre... the stone is placed, and the earth is laid. Then... if... it is necessary, it is placed.
EDCS-64400324.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary inscription, possibly a boundary marker or a dedication related to burial rites, with names and some description of placement.

EDCS-64400327.translation: Under Lucius son of Cassius and Philonis daughter of Philo, and Adonis, on the fourth day of the month, it was decided by the assembly, as it was also by the council, that since the common affairs of the Naconians have been well managed by the fortunes, it is also expedient for the future to govern in concord. Ambassadors from the Egestaeans, Apellichos son of Aleidas, Attikos son of Piston, and Dionysius son of Decius, having been sent concerning matters of common interest to all citizens, advised that it be decreed by the assembly on the fourth day of the month, on which Adonis is honored, to convene the assembly of citizens, and that those among the citizens who have had disputes concerning common affairs, having been summoned to the assembly, should reach a settlement among themselves. Thirty are to be appointed for each side from the previously designated. Those who have been opposed in past times should each designate thirty from each side. The archons, having selected the names by lot, shall place one from each into two urns, and shall draw lots for one from each urn, and shall select three from the remaining citizens for every two, outside the nearest relatives, which the law orders to be removed from the courts. And in the same way, the brothers elected together, in agreement with each other, with all justice and friendship. And when all sixty lots have been drawn, and those who have drawn lots with them, the remaining citizens, five at a time, shall draw lots, not drawing lots for the nearest relatives as written. And in the same way, brothers, having drawn lots together, as they have drawn lots with them previously. And the hieromnamons shall sacrifice a white goat for the sacrifice, and the treasurer shall provide whatever is necessary for the sacrifice. Likewise, all the officials following shall sacrifice each year on this day for the ancestors and for Concord, a victim for each, as they deem fit. And all citizens shall celebrate among themselves according to the adelphothetiai. And the archons, having inscribed this decree on bronze, shall dedicate it in the pronaos of Olympian Zeus.
EDCS-64400327.summary: This is a decree from a Greek city (likely Naconians) concerning the resolution of disputes among citizens and the establishment of legal and religious procedures, including lottery selection of jurors and annual sacrifices, to be inscribed on bronze.

EDCS-64400341.translation: Artemidora dedicated to her father, Artemon, son of Nymphon, and Aleidas, who served as gymnasiarch, according to his will.
EDCS-64400341.summary: A dedication by a daughter to her father, honoring his service as gymnasiarch, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-64400345.translation: Krinos to Teisikles, the handsome, it seems.
EDCS-64400345.summary: A very short, potentially epigrammatic or personal inscription, possibly a dedication or a brief statement of admiration.

EDCS-64400364.translation: Damatrios son of Histieus, of the twelfth (tribe/group).
EDCS-64400364.summary: A simple inscription identifying an individual, likely Damatrios, and his affiliation with a group or tribe.

EDCS-64400366.summary: A brief personal inscription, possibly identifying Andreas and a numerical designation, perhaps a group or rank.

EDCS-64400370.translation: Damatrios son of Sattos, of the eighth (tribe/group).
EDCS-64400370.summary: A simple inscription identifying an individual, likely Damatrios, and his affiliation with a group or tribe.

EDCS-64400390.summary: An inscription indicating the makers of an object, possibly a tombstone or a votive offering, by Pyrais and Sytas.

EDCS-64400392.summary: A short inscription, possibly a dedication or a greeting, mentioning two individuals and a place of dedication.

EDCS-64400394.translation: Hippocrates the magistrate, with his fellow magistrates Oagoria and Erka, carved this inscription and wrote backward: Klagok Davies, Rokos, Arche demos, Rokos, Philippos, Hippias, Anoaoios, Sasamos, Amsitystgd, Areta, pay to me.
EDCS-64400394.summary: This is a public inscription from a magistrate and his colleagues, likely related to civic duties or payments, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-64400408.translation: Sosios, son of Heracleidas, in Deka.
EDCS-64400408.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and patronymic of the deceased, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-64400446.summary: This is a short inscription, possibly a list of names or owners, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500135.translation: Burial on the Kalends of September.
EDCS-64500135.summary: This inscription records the date of burial for Leonia, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500136.translation: The place of Dionysia and of Eirene.
EDCS-64500136.summary: This inscription likely marks a burial plot or property belonging to Dionysia and Eirene, from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500137.translation: Here lies the Gerousiarch Philentolos and Eutychia, his wife, the elder.
EDCS-64500137.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Philentolos, a Gerousiarch (member of a council of elders), and his wife, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500143.translation: I am of Pausanias, the most boastful.
EDCS-64500143.summary: This is a short inscription, possibly identifying ownership or a boastful statement, from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500144.translation: Archias and Enthimos and Apelos, sons of Pyrrhinos.
EDCS-64500144.summary: This inscription lists three individuals and their father, likely a familial record from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500156.translation: I received from Eira, daughter of Kinas.
EDCS-64500156.summary: This inscription records a transaction or receipt involving Eira, daughter of Kinas, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500162.summary: This inscription records a prize received from Kallias, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-64500163.translation: Thraso, son of Gelois, one, one, one, ten.
EDCS-64500163.summary: This inscription appears to record a numerical tally or account, possibly related to quantities or debts, associated with the individual Thraso.

EDCS-64500165.translation: Kleias, son of Neminion, one, ten.
EDCS-64500165.summary: This inscription seems to be a numerical record or account involving Kleias, the son of Neminion.

EDCS-64500166.translation: Deinias, son of Eukleidas, one, one, one, one, five.
EDCS-64500166.summary: This inscription records a numerical tally or account associated with Deinias, son of Eukleidas.

EDCS-64500167.summary: This inscription likely represents a numerical record or accounting, possibly related to a group or activity, involving Philiarchos.

EDCS-64500168.summary: This inscription appears to be a numerical record or tally associated with Satyros, son of Hippias.

EDCS-64500169.summary: This inscription records a single unit or quantity associated with Gelois, son of Eupolemos.

EDCS-64500170.summary: This inscription records a single unit or quantity associated with Agatharchos, son of Aischrion.

EDCS-64500388.translation: Perexsi, Panta, Prokos, Charmas, Philoxenos, Pytheas, Hestie, Hestia, Kleom, th, Nikomachos, Dikaios, Samokles, Pithaiqos, Apontios, Phrynis, Phryneos, Ky, a.
EDCS-64500388.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly related to a communal record, honorific list, or a group participating in an event.

EDCS-64500407.translation: Myrta, to the dear one who writes.
EDCS-64500407.summary: This is a short dedicatory or affectionate message, possibly an epitaph or a note, from Myrta to someone.

EDCS-64500531.summary: This fragmented inscription likely refers to a horse, possibly a dedication or a description.

EDCS-64500537.summary: This is a dedication of a statue or monument by Eupraxia to Heracleidas, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-64500587.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a funerary epitaph or a name tag, possibly indicating a person's origin.

EDCS-64600116.translation: Tomb of those lying in repose in God: Constantine, who lived 12 years, Mellosus, who lived 10 years, and Nicetas, who lived 8 years. Remember them, Lord, in your kingdom. Completed in the month of December, the 27th, in the sixth indiction.
EDCS-64600116.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription commemorating the lives of three children, Constantine, Mellosus, and Nicetas, and was completed in December.

EDCS-64600163.translation: Of Damarchos Achyrios, one hundred and fifteen gold pieces.
EDCS-64600163.summary: This inscription records a sum of money, 115 gold pieces, associated with Damarchos Achyrios, possibly related to a financial transaction or offering.

EDCS-64600165.translation: In the name of the Lord Jesus Christ, holy angels who write the vineyard of the vinedresser, help me for the fruit for the vineyard of God. God, God, Lord Gabriel, Michael.
EDCS-64600165.summary: This is a Christian inscription invoking Jesus Christ and holy angels for help in the cultivation of a vineyard, likely from the late antique or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-64600168.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a patronymic or familial relationship, possibly part of a longer funerary or dedicatory text.

EDCS-64600190.summary: This is a possessive inscription, possibly a funerary marker, stating that Menna belongs to or is associated with Kallikrates.

EDCS-64600215.translation: Perseus, Andromeda, Euaios, the beautiful, of Aeschylus.
EDCS-64600215.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly related to a theatrical production or a collection of artworks, referencing figures from mythology and literature.

EDCS-64600230.translation: To the Gods Below. Greetings. Publius Aelius Hermolaos of Pergamon, comedian and lyre-player, lived 25 years. Be well.
EDCS-64600230.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Hermolaos, a comedian and lyre-player from Pergamon, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-64600232.translation: He dedicated daughters: Testan, daughter of Ariston son of Hagetor; Charossa, daughter of Antallidas; Thearo; Sosias; Hagia; a priestess; Pleistarchos, son of Menon; Apophylioi; Lysis, son of Hermai; Nympharatos, son of Alkamenes; Menas, son of Psiphyros; gods; Andreas Exako.
EDCS-64600232.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription listing several individuals, possibly family members and servants, dedicated by an unnamed person, with mention of a priestess and possibly divine figures.

EDCS-64600246.summary: This inscription records the names of three individuals, likely a funerary or honorary dedication.

EDCS-64600454.translation: I adjure you, the great living God above Sabaoth, Iao, above Iao, Eloean, above Eloean, Elan, above Elan, Marmarion, above Marmarion, Jacob, above Jaboch, On! Preserve Schybon, whom Maryllina bore.
EDCS-64600454.summary: This is a magical or imprecatory text invoking various divine names to protect a person named Schybon, born to Maryllina.

EDCS-64600456.translation: Amel, Gabriel, Christ, who filled the stars in the sky and the water in the sea, fill also the fruits in the vineyard for Paulus.
EDCS-64600456.summary: This is a prayer or invocation to divine beings, likely Christ and angels, to bless the vineyard of Paulus.

EDCS-64600461.translation: Epina, Symeon, O Salamax, Bameaza, AD ON, in worthiness, Marcianus, whom Lavinia bore, N, Iaoalon, for every soul. Iao, on, eat, eat, eat, eat, eat, eat, eat, eat, eat, eat, eat.
EDCS-64600461.summary: This inscription appears to be a complex magical text with names, divine invocations, and possibly spells or incantations, intended for every soul and associated with Marcianus.

EDCS-64700405.translation: This is the tomb of Eucritus, son of Andros.
EDCS-64700405.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying the deceased as Eucritus, son of Andros.

EDCS-64700406.translation: I am the tomb of Mysco, son of Menepolemos.
EDCS-64700406.summary: This is a funerary epitaph stating that the tomb belongs to Mysco, son of Menepolemos.

EDCS-64700407.translation: I am the tomb of Agasia, daughter of Karias. Alas!
EDCS-64700407.summary: This is a funerary epitaph stating the tomb belongs to Agasia, daughter of Karias, with an expression of grief.

EDCS-64700408.translation: The tomb of Theoxenos, son of Niccolas.
EDCS-64700408.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying the deceased as Theoxenos, son of Niccolas.

EDCS-64700409.translation: Dionysios, this is the tomb of Selinios.
EDCS-64700409.summary: This is a funerary epitaph indicating that this tomb belongs to Selinios, and the inscription is by Dionysios.

EDCS-64800020.translation: To Damatrios, the deceased Poplius. Greetings.
EDCS-64800020.summary: This is a funerary greeting addressed to Damatrios, the deceased Poplius.

EDCS-64800107.translation: This tomb is for Pasiadas; Krates made it.
EDCS-64800107.summary: A tomb inscription stating that Krates made the tomb for Pasiadas, likely from the Hellenistic period.

EDCS-64800108.translation: Pantares dedicated me as a prize to Zeus Hilaros, from Menekratios.
EDCS-64800108.summary: A dedication to Zeus Hilaros, given by Menekratios as a prize, possibly from a competition.

EDCS-64800109.translation: I belong to Kynaithos, the tomb of Epochos.
EDCS-64800109.summary: A tomb inscription identifying the deceased as belonging to Kynaithos and being the tomb of Epochos.

EDCS-64800121.summary: An inscription simply naming Alkimidas and Moschina, possibly a dedication or a funerary marker.

EDCS-64800127.summary: A dedication by Mnasithales to Antiphamos.

EDCS-64800135.translation: Philistidas, the good maker, son of Euxenos.
EDCS-64800135.summary: An inscription identifying Philistidas, son of Euxenos, as a 'good maker', likely a craftsman.

EDCS-64800140.translation: Tirinaxinos, Oinitis, Kaposos, Damon, Sosias, Neias, Alkia, Bitos, Deinos, the Geloi, Neias, Ne.
EDCS-64800140.summary: A list of names, possibly recording members of a group, tribe, or participants in an event.

EDCS-64800142.translation: A votive offering from Apellas, in friendship with Eunike; may no one hasten more than Eunikos, nor Phinton, but praise him, both willingly and unwillingly, and Philetan, in friendship with Eunike, the choragos, register all the choruses for exemption from tax, and work, and their children, from their parents, for their inactivity and for being in the assembly and for being hated by the childless, those who leave me behind. Kaledias, register from Apellios and all his descendants, for the middle-aged. Sosias, register from the magistrate. Alkiades, for his great friendship. Pyrrias, Myskelos, Damophantos, and his son, and his father, and all others who come here, may none of them be more hasty than Eunikos, neither men nor women. To so many blessings, may they help to save the Eunikoi, may they always be victorious in all battles, for friendship with Eunike, for the deed.
EDCS-64800142.summary: A complex inscription concerning a votive offering and registration for privileges by Apellas, likely related to a religious festival or civic duty, involving multiple individuals and beneficiaries, possibly dating to the Hellenistic period.

EDCS-64800149.summary: A simple epitaph or dedication naming Exakessos, son of Ariomos.

EDCS-64800158.summary: A funerary inscription or dedication naming Leukon, son of Chairesilaos.

EDCS-64800167.translation: I am from Ndai and from the Arimans.
EDCS-64800167.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning origins from two places, likely a personal statement on a monument.

EDCS-64800170.translation: Myrkon, son of Damios, son of Kobetos. Anthemo kritos attempted to betroth Apelis, but she said she was not available; he deceived Myrkon in the square, offering silver as payment. He brought her as a bride for honour, not for purchase. Once, for Mnasi machos, son of Empedokles, he arranged a betrothal.
EDCS-64800170.summary: This inscription details a complex marriage negotiation and agreement, possibly related to a legal dispute or a testament, from the Roman period.

EDCS-64800179.translation: I am Kleos, son of Anchemaxos.
EDCS-64800179.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription identifying the deceased, Kleos, son of Anchemaxos.

EDCS-64800190.translation: I am Mullos, son of Sakos, and I am Sakos, son of Mullos.
EDCS-64800190.summary: This inscription appears to be a double epitaph or declaration of identity for two individuals named Mullos and Sakos, likely father and son.

EDCS-64800194.summary: This inscription records the maker of pyres, Klisumis Pabius, likely related to funerary practices.

EDCS-64800199.translation: Melampus, son of Eupoleides, deceased in the ninth [month/year?].
EDCS-64800199.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Melampus, son of Eupoleides, with an unclear temporal reference.

EDCS-64800205.translation: Sime Praotoma, Leptina Praxia, Rator, Kykyies, Araotes. [---] fire fell. [---] a stone. [---] two fell. [---] it fell again. [---] because of the fire, he suffered.
EDCS-64800205.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that seems to record the names of individuals and a destructive event, possibly a fire.

EDCS-64800206.translation: God and Fortune. Archon owes the goddess 1 talent, with interest. Not a mortal, but images and vessels are to be purchased with this money. And 100 litres. He owes the goddess and Archon's sisters. Her property and Archon's property from this money, and the children are to be freed when Archon repays this money, at which time they will be free.
EDCS-64800206.summary: This inscription is a religious dedication and legal document concerning a debt owed to a goddess, specifying the use of funds and the manumission of slaves upon repayment.

EDCS-64800207.translation: Porcos dedicates this drinking cup to the association of drinkers. If Phryne is willing, no one else will be punished. The one who wrote this will be struck in the rear.
EDCS-64800207.summary: This inscription is a dedication of a drinking cup to a social group, with a humorous threat against the scribe and a mention of Phryne.

EDCS-64800208.translation: Gelos, son of Akkas, desires. Gelos, son of Akkas, desires. Eumachos hires.
EDCS-64800208.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of individuals' intentions or actions, possibly related to a legal or business transaction, mentioning Gelos and Eumachos.

EDCS-64800214.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Euparchidas, son of Callisthenes, identified by the epithet Hypatas.

EDCS-64800215.translation: Menos, son of Damas, for Echekrates, son of Archedamos, Aristedamos, son of Chairippos, Atheles, son of Chiron, Heraklidas, son of Archytas, Sosias, son of Archonidas, Sosias, son of Archias, Symaris, daughter of Sikanas, Timokrateia, daughter of Polemaios, Prodoxos, Menos, son of Damas.
EDCS-64800215.summary: This inscription lists numerous individuals, likely a funerary or dedicatory list, possibly related to a communal burial or offering.

EDCS-64800216.translation: Here are inscribed, in misfortune: Kerdon, son of Elachixos, Pertos, Pythos, son of Diokles, Titas, son of Exakonos, Exakonos, Melanthios, son of Atimos, Dion, son of Parmenon, Onasimos the Messenian, Darchonidas, son of Entellos, Euthymos, son of Euphraios, Gelois, Heraklidas, son of Gelois, Gerys, son of Paragmos, Chairis, son of Diobes, Xenippos, son of Mios, Naraonidas; they are without lifeblood. Here all are ill-fated.
EDCS-64800216.summary: This inscription lists individuals who suffered misfortunes, possibly a curse or a record of unfortunate deaths, with a somber tone.

EDCS-64800217.translation: Lacaena, Damareta, having dedicated, for Damareta, her daughter's wine.
EDCS-64800217.summary: This is a dedication, likely a libation or offering, made by Lacaena Damareta for her daughter.

EDCS-64800218.translation: Nairogenes, Phymetos, Eukleides, Aulus, Gelsos, Orphanos, Aratios, Heraklidas, Nikias, Skytas, Glykys.
EDCS-64800218.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, potentially a funerary epitaph or a list of members of a group.

EDCS-64800219.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph identifying Athanes, son of Ankisskos.

EDCS-64800220.translation: To Aristoi, Epikeis, son of Faullos, Matr...ias.
EDCS-64800220.summary: This inscription is likely a dedication or a funerary epitaph to Aristoi, Epikeis, son of Faullos.

EDCS-64800221.translation: Soka, son of Cha[...], Soka, son of Nil[...]
EDCS-64800221.summary: This inscription lists two individuals named Soka, son of Cha[...] and Soka, son of Nil[...], likely a funerary epitaph.

EDCS-64800224.translation: Here in the dance, Apelos lies, lies having received death, and the handsome Huia buried his friend.
EDCS-64800224.summary: This is a poetic funerary epitaph commemorating Apelos and another individual, with a mention of burial by a friend.

EDCS-64800227.summary: This is a funerary epitaph identifying Aristion, son of Nisios.

EDCS-64800228.translation: Somrotida, daughter of Hiatros, son of Mandrokles.
EDCS-64800228.summary: This inscription names an individual, Somrotida, and her paternal lineage.

EDCS-64800229.translation: This is the tomb of Marylos.
EDCS-64800229.summary: This is a funerary marker identifying the deceased as Marylos.

EDCS-64800231.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or dedication to the Muse Calliope or a person named Calliope.

EDCS-64800232.translation: I am the daughter of the Holy One, Kaprogono.
EDCS-64800232.summary: This inscription identifies the speaker as Kaprogono, daughter of a person referred to as 'the Holy One'.

EDCS-64800234.translation: This is the tomb of Thessalos, son of Euios.
EDCS-64800234.summary: This is a funerary marker identifying the deceased as Thessalos, son of Euios.

EDCS-64800235.translation: I am Pantareos and I am shared with my friend.
EDCS-64800235.summary: This inscription suggests a shared tomb or possession belonging to Pantareos and a friend.

EDCS-64800237.translation: Fintylos, son of Hougritos, dedicated this stele.
EDCS-64800237.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a stele by Fintylos, son of Hougritos.

EDCS-64800238.translation: Pasaratos, son of Haides, let him pay an ox; if not, let him pay ten litrai.
EDCS-64800238.summary: This inscription seems to be a legal or financial record concerning Pasaratos, son of Haides, and a debt or penalty.

EDCS-64800242.translation: I am Aristogeitos, son of Arkadionos. I died as a Hypo under Motywa.
EDCS-64800242.summary: This inscription identifies Aristogeitos, son of Arkadionos, and mentions his death in a specific role or under a particular condition related to Motywa.

EDCS-64800244.translation: I am Epicharmos, son of Mnasanridas.
EDCS-64800244.summary: This inscription identifies Epicharmos, son of Mnasanridas.

EDCS-64800245.summary: This is a funerary epigram identifying Silanos and his father Euthymidas.

EDCS-64800248.summary: This is a funerary epigram lamenting Lyciske.

EDCS-64800251.translation: Alas, Phile, daughter of Selinus, daughter of Lasos!
EDCS-64800251.summary: This is a funerary epigram lamenting Phile, daughter of Selinus and granddaughter of Lasos.

EDCS-64800253.summary: This is a funerary epigram lamenting Eurypohone, daughter of Archinidas.

EDCS-64800254.translation: Alas, Archedamas, son of Pytheas! Selinontios, wool.
EDCS-64800254.summary: This is a funerary epigram lamenting Archedamas, son of Pytheas, and possibly recording his occupation or a trade good.

EDCS-64800256.translation: I belong to the sacred precinct of Herakles. Aristylos, son of Hermias, dedicated me.
EDCS-64800256.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred object or place to Herakles by Aristylos, son of Hermias.

EDCS-64800258.translation: Alexias, son of Xenon, dedicated [this] to the angeloi (messengers) and Hekate.
EDCS-64800258.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Alexias, son of Xenon, to divine messengers and the goddess Hekate.

EDCS-64800261.summary: This is a funerary epigram identifying Melichios as a member of the Klyeid family.

EDCS-64800263.summary: This is a funerary epigram identifying Melichios as belonging to Lyciscus.

EDCS-64800268.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Heuris to Meilichios.

EDCS-64800269.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hagesandros, son of Sauris, likely from the Hellenistic or Roman period.

EDCS-64800270.translation: I belong to Zeus the Merciful; I am Pyrria, daughter of Eumenes, of Pediarchos.
EDCS-64800270.summary: This is a personal dedication or epitaph, identifying Pyrria as belonging to Zeus and indicating her lineage and possibly a social status or group affiliation.

EDCS-64800271.translation: To Melichios, of the family of Hermion, and to the children of Eukles.
EDCS-64800271.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial, possibly to a deity or honoring family lines, specifically mentioning Melichios and the children of Eukles and Hermion.

EDCS-64800273.translation: I am Aristokleia the beautiful, beautiful. Pithagos has asked for me.
EDCS-64800273.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, possibly for a statue or a tomb, describing Aristokleia as beautiful and noting that Pithagos requested her.

EDCS-64800274.translation: I belong to Archetius, son of Eukles.
EDCS-64800274.summary: This is a simple owner's mark or epitaph identifying Archetius as belonging to Eukles.

EDCS-64800276.translation: The tongue of Eukles, and of Aristophanes, and of Angeilos, and of Alkiphron, and of Hagestratos the synod of Eukles, and of Aristophanes, and the tongues of Lyra and of Oinotheos and of Aga.
EDCS-64800276.summary: This inscription lists the 'tongues' or speeches/works of several individuals, likely related to a literary or rhetorical context, possibly a collection or a memorial.

EDCS-64800277.translation: The tongues of Selinontios and Selinontia, turned away for exemption for the inscribed members and the foreign synod; the tongues, turned away for exemption for the inscribed members, to Timasios and the tongue of Timaso, turned away for exemption for the inscribed members, to Tyrrhena and the tongue of Tyrrhenas, turned away for exemption for the inscribed members, for all.
EDCS-64800277.summary: This inscription appears to grant tax or duty exemptions ('ateleia') related to 'tongues' (possibly orations, documents, or rights) for inscribed members and foreign synods, mentioning Selinontios, Timasios, and Tyrrhena.

EDCS-64800278.summary: This inscription likely lists the names of individuals associated with a kylix (drinking cup), possibly owners, artists, or participants in a symposium.

EDCS-64800279.translation: Apelon, son of Lykinos, I record [my] soul and power with the Holy Goddess, and Lykinos, son of Halos, and his brother, and himself, with the Holy Goddess; Naveros, son of Halos, and Botylos, son of Tamiras, and his sons, and Saris, and Apelon, and Romin, son of Kallios, with the Holy Goddess, and his sons, and Saris, son of Pyrrhinos, and Pyrrhos, son of Pyrrhos, with the Holy Goddess, and the sons of Rotylos, son of Pyrrhos, with the Holy Goddess, and powers and tongues. Plakitas, son of Nanelaios, and Halos, son of Pykelei, I record [my] soul with the Holy Goddess and his power. Kadosis, son of Matylaios, and Ekotis, son of Magos, I record [my] soul with the Holy Goddess, his son of Phoinix, son of Kallios, I record with the Holy Goddess. Apelos, son of Lykinos, Lykinos, son of Pyrrhos, Nanelaios, Ekotis, son of Magos, Halos, son of Pykelei, Romis, son of Kallios, Apelos, son of Phoinix, Tittelos, son of Phoinix, Attos, son of Naveriadas, Tittelos, son of Nanelaios, Saris, son of Romios.
EDCS-64800279.summary: This is a complex inscription detailing the dedication of souls, powers, and possibly funerary rights ('tongues') to a 'Holy Goddess' by numerous individuals and their family members, likely from a religious or funerary cult.

EDCS-64800280.translation: Polykles, son of Onesimos, Adeimantos, Mycha, Meichylos, Areiadas, Exakestos, Histiarchos.
EDCS-64800280.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly an honorific list, a dedication, or a group of individuals associated with a specific event or place.

EDCS-64800283.translation: To Sopatros, his son and the daughter of Sopatros, Phrynis the interpreter of Noabaris and the daughter of Phrynis, the interpreter, offered a sacrifice. The president of the synod also offered...
EDCS-64800283.summary: This is a dedication to Sopatros and his children by Phrynis, who is identified as an interpreter, likely from a religious or civic context.

EDCS-64800285.translation: In the time of destruction, both he and his descendants. Nikyllos, Kapos, Dendilos, Mamonos, Ainos, Bepos, Xenios, Pontios, Herakleidas, Sauris, Anis, Athanis, Tammaros, Herakleidas, Ras, Pharmaua, Dion, Piakios, Piththias, Chaion, Mammaros, Zosta, Agathyllos, Xenios, Herakleidas, Synetos, Xenonos.
EDCS-64800285.summary: This inscription is a list of names, possibly of individuals who were present or involved during a period of 'destruction', likely a record of a group or a list of those who suffered.

EDCS-64800286.translation: Pithon, Gorgias, Pythodoros, Deias, Chimaras, Philoleos, Akroikoi, Selinoi.
EDCS-64800286.summary: This appears to be a list of personal names, possibly related to a local community, a family, or a group, with some names potentially indicating origin or affiliation.

EDCS-64800288.summary: This is a simple ownership inscription on a cup, identifying it as belonging to Epameinonas.

EDCS-64800289.translation: I am the property of Kallikrates' daughter, Mennas.
EDCS-64800289.summary: This inscription indicates ownership or dedication by Mennas, daughter of Kallikrates, likely on a personal item or monument.

EDCS-64800294.translation: Wine was given by Mtichai and the ribbon.
EDCS-64800294.summary: This inscription records a gift of wine, possibly as an offering or a payment, by Mtichai, associated with a ribbon, perhaps a marker or a token.

EDCS-64800563.summary: This is an ownership inscription, stating that the object or monument belongs to or is associated with Ainesos.

EDCS-64800566.summary: This is a tomb inscription for a freedman, wishing him well.

EDCS-64800567.translation: Publicia Agathis, good one, greetings. She lived 33 years.
EDCS-64800567.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Publicia Agathis, recording her name, a eulogy, and her age at death.

EDCS-64800581.summary: This is a prayer or invocation for help, asking the Lord to assist Ganos, who identifies himself as a servant.

EDCS-64900328.translation: The people of Lilybaeum [honour] Diodotus, son of Damatris, as Great Benefactor.
EDCS-64900328.summary: A public dedication honouring a benefactor, likely from Lilybaeum, a city in Sicily, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-64900329.translation: I hold a good stranger in my arms, traveller, a man Posidonius, a native of Massalia, dear son of Pythagoras, whom cruel fate, striking him here, destroyed far from his dear homeland.
EDCS-64900329.summary: A funerary epitaph for Posidonius of Massalia, son of Pythagoras, who died far from home.

EDCS-64900333.summary: A very brief funerary or personal inscription wishing greetings and farewell, possibly to two women named Nice and Plania.

EDCS-64900426.summary: A maker's signature, likely on a piece of pottery or a sculpture, indicating Nicosthenes as the craftsman.

EDCS-64900431.translation: Nicopius Silanus, son of Callimachus: a commission of eight silver pounds, seven pounds.
EDCS-64900431.summary: A payment record or contract detailing a fee of eight pounds and seven pounds of silver for Nicopius Silanus, son of Callimachus.

EDCS-64900438.translation: To Premiteiba, beloved, greetings! To Manna, child, greetings! [This is dedicated] by Manna to his wife and child.
EDCS-64900438.summary: A dedication or funerary monument from Manna to his wife and child, with greetings to Premiteiba and Manna.

EDCS-64900490.summary: A very short inscription listing names, possibly identifying individuals or figures in a scene, perhaps mythological.

EDCS-64900500.translation: In the time of priest Philo, son of Apollonius, Ariston, son of Apollonius, honoured his father Apollonius, son of Ariston, who served Olympian Zeus and all the gods. In the time of priest Philo, son of Ariston, Apollonius and Philo and Ariston, son of Ariston, honoured his father Ariston, son of Apollonius, who served Olympian Zeus and all the gods.
EDCS-64900500.summary: A complex religious inscription recording the service and honouring of fathers and ancestors by priests and their sons to Zeus and all gods.

EDCS-64900545.summary: A dedication or ownership marker with repeated personal names and an adjective suggesting a public or devoted context, possibly related to a property or a cult.

EDCS-64900559.summary: A fragment of a funerary inscription recording the name and partial age of an individual named Preigomenia.

EDCS-64900589.summary: This inscription records a counting or a tally, possibly related to a transaction or inventory, with the name Glaúkos and the number ten.

EDCS-64900602.summary: This inscription records a counting or tally, possibly related to a transaction or inventory, with the name Herakleidas and the number three.

EDCS-64900709.translation: The people of Syracuse [honour] King Gelo, son of King Hierôn, to Zeus Hellenios.
EDCS-64900709.summary: This is a dedication by the people of Syracuse to Zeus Hellenios, honouring King Gelo, son of King Hierôn.

EDCS-64900713.summary: This inscription appears to be a personal lament or a record of illness concerning a person named Phintias.

EDCS-64900715.translation: Cleomenes made the shield for Knydides, excellent works for the stele.
EDCS-64900715.summary: This is an epigrammatic inscription by Cleomenes, who crafted a shield for Knydides and other fine works for a stele.

EDCS-64900718.translation: Hiaron, son of Deinomenes, dedicated [this] to the sacred Syracuse.
EDCS-64900718.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Hiaron, son of Deinomenes, to the sacred city of Syracuse.

EDCS-64900719.translation: Gelo, son of Deinomenes, dedicated [this] to Apollo. The Syracusans of sacred Syracuse. Bion, son of Diodoros of Miletos, made the tripod and the Nike.
EDCS-64900719.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Gelo, son of Deinomenes, to Apollo, with Bion of Miletos credited for crafting a tripod and a Nike.

EDCS-64900720.translation: Hiaron, son of Deinomenes, and the Syracusans [dedicated this] to Zeus Tyrannos from Cumae.
EDCS-64900720.summary: This inscription records a joint dedication by Hiaron, son of Deinomenes, and the Syracusans to Zeus Tyrannos, originating from Cumae.

EDCS-64900721.translation: Hiaron, son of Deinomenes, and the Syracusans [dedicated this] to Zeus Tyrannos from Cumae.
EDCS-64900721.summary: This inscription records a joint dedication by Hiaron, son of Deinomenes, and the Syracusans to Zeus Tyrannos, originating from Cumae.

EDCS-64900725.summary: This is a simple sepulchral dedication, identifying the tomb of Alexios.

EDCS-64900727.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram, likely identifying the deceased Anaxagora of Syracuse.

EDCS-64900729.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram, likely identifying the deceased Lissias, son of Neminios.

EDCS-64900731.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram, likely identifying the deceased Lysis, son of Timados.

EDCS-64900735.translation: I am the tomb of Krates, son of Eupylos
EDCS-64900735.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram, identifying the deceased Krates, son of Eupylos, and the monument as his tomb.

EDCS-64900740.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram, an exclamation of grief for Epaly, daughter of Sankos.

EDCS-64900772.summary: This is a Greek inscription, possibly a dedication or honorary text by the Danklaioi people for someone named Region.

EDCS-64900775.summary: This is a Greek inscription, possibly a dedication or honorary text by the Messenians for someone named Mylaios.

EDCS-64900777.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram, identifying the deceased Attikos, son of Agathinos.

EDCS-64900778.translation: [---], son of [---], [---], greetings. Hera, daughter of Hyrulos, greetings.
EDCS-64900778.summary: This is a Greek funerary epigram, identifying Hera, daughter of Hyrulos, as the deceased, with preceding illegible names.

EDCS-64900786.translation: Evopidas, priestess of the Diouches, leader of the cohort. Daitis, I pray that you may let me, a foreigner, receive a stranger well. [---] own [---]
EDCS-64900786.summary: This is a Greek inscription recording a prayer by a priestess named Daitis, likely related to her role as a foreigner seeking good hospitality in a foreign land.

EDCS-64900803.summary: This inscription is a personal dedication or tomb inscription, likely naming individuals.

EDCS-64900807.summary: This inscription is a personal dedication or tomb inscription, likely naming individuals.

EDCS-64900808.summary: This inscription is a personal dedication or tomb inscription, likely naming individuals.

EDCS-64900823.translation: In the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit, I adjure you according to the seal of Solomon, I adjure you according to the bearer of the throne of God Michael, I adjure you according to the Cherubim, I adjure you according to the 12 patriarchs and the 18 prophets who serve in the heavens, I adjure you according to the Jordan River and its baptism, whether from witchcraft or from insult or from an evil encounter, from head to toe, flee, be driven away, every evil, from the servant of God, Julian. Amen.
EDCS-64900823.summary: This is an exorcism or curse text, likely inscribed for protection, invoking religious figures and biblical references, possibly from a later period.

EDCS-64900835.summary: This inscription likely refers to an individual named Theodoros and a location or group called Deka.

EDCS-64900837.translation: For Caesar Augustus, flamen of Lucius Egnatius, son of Lucius, of the Gal(erian) tribe. Two men... Sextus Egnatius Rufus, son of Sextus.
EDCS-64900837.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Augustus, mentioning priests (flamen) and family lines, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-64900839.translation: Greetings, passerby! From Fabius Saturnilus, who lived 21 years, 8 months, and 15 days. Air, do not be afraid!
EDCS-64900839.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Fabius Saturnilus, providing his age at death and a farewell to passersby.

EDCS-64900846.translation: Celsus Nonis, Capito Tragoxidas, Casutis, Gorgos Plotis, Trebonis, Ninios, Apion, Capito, Hegistus, Nistiatis, Ias. I record.
EDCS-64900846.summary: This inscription lists numerous names, possibly a record of a group or individuals involved in some transaction or event.

EDCS-64900899.translation: Agroetes made the kylix for Zopyros.
EDCS-64900899.summary: This inscription records the maker of a vessel, likely a kylix, and its recipient or owner.

EDCS-64901069.summary: This inscription is a short dedication or reference to Saint Theodorus.

EDCS-64901072.summary: This inscription is a brief mention of a woman named Tacima, identified as a wife.

EDCS-64901073.translation: The Chithaei and Polemarchos and Gellias dedicated [this].
EDCS-64901073.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the Chithaei and two individuals, Polemarchos and Gellias.

EDCS-64901074.translation: Nicomachus, under the leadership of Gellias son of Antisthenes, and the Lycumnii.
EDCS-64901074.summary: This inscription seems to record a group (the Lycumnii) and individuals (Nicomachus, Gellias, Antisthenes) possibly in a military or administrative context.

EDCS-64901078.translation: Damatrios son of Sattu [dedicated this] in the Eleventh [rank/year].
EDCS-64901078.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Damatrios son of Sattu, possibly related to a military unit or a specific year.

EDCS-64901092.translation: Chaeiras, handsome, son of Phanos, of Oxy [---].
EDCS-64901092.summary: This inscription is a brief funerary or honorific epigram for Chaeiras, son of Phanos, with a partial place name.

EDCS-64901099.translation: Here lies Nonius, son of [---], a builder, whom once Kallistos and Moira killed; he left behind for his father the most terrible grief; he lived 12 years.
EDCS-64901099.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Nonius, a builder who died at the age of 12, tragically killed by Kallistos and Moira, leaving his father in great sorrow.

EDCS-64901100.translation: [---]us entrusts the [belongings/money?] at the temple of Heracles.
EDCS-64901100.summary: This inscription records a deposit or entrustment of goods at a temple of Heracles by an unidentified person.

EDCS-64901133.summary: This inscription is an invocation or dedication to two figures named Silanus, referred to as good heroes.

EDCS-65000075.translation: This gift of the hippodrome to the Pedioi.
EDCS-65000075.summary: This inscription records a gift related to a hippodrome, dedicated to a group called the Pedioi.

EDCS-65100050.translation: Good Preamiteba, farewell. Manna, child, farewell. [This is for] Manna's wife and child.
EDCS-65100050.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a woman named Preamiteba and her child Manna, possibly commissioned by Manna's wife and child.

EDCS-65100052.summary: This inscription is a devotional text honoring Saint Alexander.

EDCS-65100060.translation: Dionysia died three days before the Kalends of February.
EDCS-65100060.summary: This inscription records the death date of a woman named Dionysia.

EDCS-65100061.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial for Avlia Helena.

EDCS-65100063.summary: This inscription seems to record the name of Gnaeus Domitius Philis, likely as a dedicator or deceased individual.

EDCS-65100065.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Publius Clodius Siculus, possibly as a benefactor or the deceased.

EDCS-65100066.summary: This inscription records the name of Clodia Aristobula, possibly in a funerary or dedicatory context.

EDCS-65100067.summary: This inscription records the name of Clodia Lycisca, likely in a funerary or dedicatory context.

EDCS-65100068.summary: This inscription appears to record the name of Euchrestus Minucius Diodorus.

EDCS-65100069.summary: This inscription records the name of Nearchia Philocos, likely in a funerary or dedicatory context.

EDCS-65100071.summary: This inscription records the name of Pompeia Hecale Caecina, possibly in a funerary or dedicatory context.

EDCS-65100092.summary: This is a dedication to the god Herakles, likely on an altar or statue base.

EDCS-65200116.translation: I beg you, Hermes below, Hermes who holds fast, to you and the many attendants, the uncarved Telchines, as a gift I offer the girl Primiana, asking for the girl. I give you as a fine gift, the girl Primiana Allia, who has beautiful hair, a beautiful face, a beautiful forehead, beautiful eyebrows, beautiful eyes, two smooth ears, two nostrils, a mouth, teeth, smooth ears, a neck, limbs, extremities. I bury her, so that it may be a tomb for Aphrodite-like Allia Primiana. This inscription and the... Two lovers mourn, one heart. Cerberus of Allia Prima. A beautiful mound of hair, a beautiful body, beautiful thighs, beautiful shins, beautiful extremities, all beautiful. Allia Prima, I hand over to you as a gift. Hermes, so that you may deliver her to the mistress. I ask, Hermes who holds fast, chthonic one, that you may remove Primiana Allia. To the mistress Persephone, I bury her.
EDCS-65200116.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Allia Primiana, likely from the Roman Imperial period, depicting a complex ritual and plea to the gods Hermes and Persephone for the deceased's safe passage and eternal rest.

EDCS-65300044.summary: This inscription likely commemorates or identifies Saint Alexander.

EDCS-65300056.summary: This is a possessive or genitive inscription, possibly indicating ownership or authorship by Cassius Caecilianus.

EDCS-65300214.summary: This is a dedication of an object or structure to the god Hermes by Phalacros.

EDCS-65300226.translation: Studios, good man, farewell. He lived 60 years. Lydia
EDCS-65300226.summary: This is a funerary epigram for a man named Studios who lived 60 years, identified as a 'lydiastes' (possibly a performer or associated with Lydia).

EDCS-65700010.translation: Claudius Orestas and Seius Barex, having served as agoranomoi (market officials), the platform of the treasury
EDCS-65700010.summary: This inscription records the names of two former market officials, Claudius Orestas and Seius Barex, possibly commemorating a public work or their service related to the treasury platform.

EDCS-65700011.summary: This is a simple patronymic inscription, identifying 'Materis' as the son of 'Peisandros'.

EDCS-65700036.summary: This inscription identifies 'Euthymiskos' and his place of origin or affiliation as 'Eleata'.

EDCS-65700071.summary: This is a simple patronymic inscription, identifying 'Teisarchos' as the son of 'Apollonios'.

EDCS-65700176.translation: Zopyriscus, son of Heracleidas, from Syracuse.
EDCS-65700176.summary: This inscription is a name and origin, likely serving as a funerary marker or an owner's identification.

EDCS-65700342.summary: This inscription is a name and patronymic, possibly from a funerary monument.

EDCS-65700346.summary: This is a tomb inscription, a farewell greeting to the deceased.

EDCS-65700347.summary: This inscription is a name and patronymic, likely from a funerary monument.

EDCS-65700348.summary: This inscription is a name and patronymic, likely from a funerary monument.

EDCS-65700353.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, possibly a couple, on a tomb or other shared monument.

EDCS-65700358.summary: This is a tomb inscription, a farewell greeting to the deceased.

EDCS-65700359.summary: This inscription is a plural farewell, possibly addressing the Muses associated with Apollonidas, perhaps on a monument related to arts or learning.

EDCS-65700360.summary: This inscription records the lifespan of a child named Apollonius, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-65700367.translation: Ariston, the Black-Stone [or from Black Stone].
EDCS-65700367.summary: This inscription provides a name, Ariston, and a possible epithet or place of origin, 'Blakorna'.

EDCS-65700371.summary: This is a funerary or ownership inscription naming Archagathos, son of Moschos.

EDCS-65700372.summary: This is a funerary inscription wishing a good farewell to a person named Aspis.

EDCS-65700375.summary: This inscription records a dedication or marker made by the associates of Atimetos.

EDCS-65700377.summary: This is a funerary or ownership inscription naming Aphrodisias, daughter of Dikaios.

EDCS-65700378.summary: This is likely a funerary or ownership inscription naming Barbaros Mallianos.

EDCS-65700381.translation: Of Gamike, daughter of Gnostos, Euphrosyne.
EDCS-65700381.summary: This inscription names Gamike, daughter of Gnostos, and Euphrosyne, likely for funerary or ownership purposes.

EDCS-65700384.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Of the people of Cappadocia, you know the fertile fields; from there I grew from good parents. I left them even then; I came to the setting and also the rising. My name is Glaphyros, and my mind was sharp; I lived sixty years completely free, and I know life to be both good fortune and bitter.
EDCS-65700384.summary: This is a self-commemorative epitaph of Glaphyros, who identifies his origins from Cappadocia and reflects on his life, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-65700385.translation: Of Gnesios, son of Heroxenos, of Olothra.
EDCS-65700385.summary: This is a funerary or ownership inscription naming Gnesios, son of Heroxenos, from Olothra.

EDCS-65700389.summary: This is a funerary inscription wishing a good farewell to Demokritos.

EDCS-65700395.summary: This is a funerary or ownership inscription naming Dionysios from Rhodes.

EDCS-65700396.summary: This is a funerary or ownership mark of Diogenes, son of Panphilos.

EDCS-65700398.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Duronius, son of Heraclidas.

EDCS-65700405.summary: This inscription likely records Epaphras, a freedman of Irtianus, possibly in a funerary context.

EDCS-65700409.translation: Eumousas, daughter of Hebe, of Nearchia.
EDCS-65700409.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eumousas, daughter of Hebe and belonging to the family of Nearchia.

EDCS-65700415.translation: Euphrosyne, daughter of Stactes, devoted to her master.
EDCS-65700415.summary: This funerary inscription honors Euphrosyne, daughter of Stactes, who was devoted to her master.

EDCS-65700419.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Zodica, a woman of good character.

EDCS-65700754.translation: To the Household Gods. Scribonius Anthos lived 45 years, 7 months. His wife, Anthis, made this for her well-living husband.
EDCS-65700754.summary: This funerary monument records the lifespan of Scribonius Anthos and was dedicated by his wife Anthis.
